WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL 13.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 1, 1884	No 3.


A DRIFTING BOAT.

BY CHARLES A. FOSTER.

It had floated away from the beach and bay,
	Out of sight of tower and town,
An empty and a battered boat;
	But that boat would not go down.
The morning rose on the waters wide,
	And the night fell cold and dark,
Yet ever on with the wind and tide
	Drifted that battered bark.

The sail had passed from its broken mast,
	And its painted pride was dim;
The salt sea-weed clung round its bows
	Which had been so sharp and trim.
Where were the merry mates and free
	Who had gone with it afloat?
We never learned; but the world's wide sea
	Hath lives like that drifting boat.

Lives that in early storms have lost
	Anchor and sail and oar,
And never, except on Lethe's shore,
	Can come to moorings more;
Out of whose loveless, trustless days
	The hope and the heart hath gone—
Good ships go down on the stormy seas,
	But those empty boats drift on.

They had hearts to sail in the wind's eye once;
	They had hands to reef and steer,
With a strength that would not stoop to chance,
	And a faith that knew no fear.
But the years were long and the storms were strong,
	And the rainbow flag was furled,
And they that launched for the skies became
	But the driftwood of the world.
—Woman's Journal.


[Column 1]
﻿A VISIT TO THE COUNTRY.

	Oh, for an "out" into the fresh green coun-
try! Inspired by this impulse, my sister, with
our two little girls chatting with delight over
our anticipated ride, secured seats in a northern
bound train last Decoration Day—as is usually
the case on that memorable day the heavens
showered its tears upon the graves of the dead
heroes of our country—but after the ceremony
of strewing flowers, nature seemed cheered,
dried her eyes and the sun shone forth with
increased brilliancy—in token of approval.
	An example of "Might over Right," which
still holds sway in this life, was exhibited in
the cars by a man in appearance rudely taking
possession of a lady's seat during her absence
for a few moments. When informed that a lady
had already secured the seat, he answered,
"Then she may find another," refusing to arise
when she returned, although other seats were
vacant. The blood in my veins ran up to fev-
er heat, but the train sped on, leaving us at a
little quiet station, from whence we were borne
by a pair of fine animals through winding wil-
low-fringed lanes and flower-decked roads,
sweet with the fragrance of bloom and bird
song. Nature's silent influence has soothed
our indignant spirit, we breathe the cool, sweet
scented air, our eyes feast upon the scene of ru-
ral beauty and our souls swell with gratitude to
the Giver of our lovely mountain home. Man's
injustice to the weaker sex is laid aside for the
time—we are in sight of the little cottage on
the hill side, nestled in a forest of apple trees.
The snowy mountain peaks tower far above in
majestic protection, sending forth rushing, bub-
bling streamlets to dance and sing through
velvet meadows and thriving farms, to be

[Column 2]
hushed into silence on the bosom of the fa
mous Salt Lake, shimmering in the sunlight
like a huge sheet of silver.
	The sound of carriage wheels brings a bevy
of curious expectant faces to the door. We
are most cordially greeted and gracefully
waited upon by the elder girls, while two or
three younger ones run excitedly in search of
"mother;" after a smiling welcome from our
aunt we begin to make ourselves acquainted
with the little country cousins, who have
sprung up like daisies in the meadow since we
made our last visit, when a blooming lass we
tripped over the hills which years of suffering
have robbed us of power to do more than look
upon and admire.
	In this secluded spot in the suburbs of our
propserous town, is a family to be proud of—
twelve children, sex equally divided, with no
vacant chair in the household, except as it was
left to fill that of another home. Here was a
family chain without one missing or ill-favored
link—the sons handsome, intelligent, and of
splendid physique, and the daughters, without
exception, healthy, rosy-cheeked and pretty,
each head crowned with an abundance of wom-
an's glory, a fair forehead, beneath which spark-
led large, beautiful eyes. How graceful and
modest, and such housekeepers! such perfection
of neatness everywhere, while every meal re-
vealed their knowledge of wholesome, tasty
cooking; the sweet, golden butter was perfec-
tion. Vases of tastefully arranged wild flow-
ers here and there, with books and paper, many
of them our own home literature, gave evi-
dence of refinement and intelligence. And
how refreshing the spirit of kindness manifest-
ed from the eldest down to the prattling house-
hold pet. We witnessed nothing rough, bois-
terous or uncouth, nor had they been trained
for the occasion, for we were unexpected visi-
tors.
	And this was a patriarchal family in the or-
der of plural marriage! All clinging to the
faith of their parents, and proud of their birth-
right and the principle which gave them an
honorable existence among the peaceful vales
of Utah. Truly such a household is a monu-
ment of praise to the mother, a plural wife of
a noble, generous-souled man, devoted to his
family and his religion; she has lived in the or-
der of celestial marriage twenty-eight years,
and testified to me that she had never regretted
her choice, nor once felt that she had done
wrong in accepting that principle.
	This quiet, steadfast woman had followed
the dictates of a conscience born of a mighty
faith in God; found honorable wifehood and
motherhood with a man she loved and honored
—has proven herself a faithful, devoted wife
through long years of toil and trial such as
must necessarily be the lot of a woman who
bears twelve children, in moderate circum-
stances. These she has trained to strict moral-
ity, taught them to honor and uphold the Con-
stitution of their country, only to see them
robbed of all rights and privileges of Ameri-
can citizens, unless they violate that conscience
which they have been trained from birth to
hold sacred; and she, this virtuous, God-fearing
woman, must sit with hands fettered by uncon-
stitutional law, untried, unproven guilty of any
offense, while any brazen courtesan, whose steps
lead down to shame and death, may pass tri-
umphantly by her and cast her vote, while she
smiles at the men who yield her patronage.

[Column 3]
	But we leave the discussion of such weighty
subjects to abler pens, we can feel the injustice,
better than discuss it.
	Our pleasant visit is over. We're homeward
bound with a huge bouquet of sweet wild roses,
a deserted bird's nest, and a bucket full of wil-
low whistles to charm the little ones, and with
a higher, truer appreciation in our hearts for
the women who quietly, patiently tread the
ceaseless round of home duties, uninspired by
the praise of the multitude, scarcely apprecia-
ted by those for whom they daily toil and
sacrifice, until the household machinery is
brought to a solemn stop; the main spring is
worn out, the tired hands are folded in a peace-
ful rest, and the weary heart has laid down its
burden of care and sorrow. These our our Mo-
thers! God bless them! May their children
rise up and call them blessed. While the
grandest, most enduring monument, shall be an
honorable remembrance among the Saints of
God forever.					SUN FLOWER.


﻿NOTES AND NEWS.

	Dr. Lizzie Sargent, daughter of our minis-
ter to Germany, and Miss Flora Kelly, daugh-
ter of Judge Kelly, of Pennsylvania, are
among the eleven American girls studying at
Zurich.
	Dr. Mary Putman Jacobi has lately given
the New York Telegram some very interesting
facts concerning the status of women in the
medical profession. The census of 1870 showed
625 women physicians in the United States.
The census of 1880 shows 2432. This includes
irregular practitioners of various kinds; but,
irrespective of such, the increase has been very
marked.
	Antipathies.-The celebrated Erasmus, though
a native of Rotterdam, had such an aversion
to fish, that the smell of it threw him into a fev-
er. Ambrose Pare had a patient who could
never see an eel without fainting; and another
who would fall into convulsions at the sight of
a carp. What would have been the effect of
an electric eel on these gentlemen? Joseph
Scaliger and others could never drink milk.
Gardan was disgusted at the sight of eggs. A
King of Poland and a Secretary of France
bled at the nose when they looked at apples.
Henry III., of France, and many others had
a great aversion to cats, mice, spiders, etc. A
great huntsman in Hanover, who would attack
a wild boar valiantly, always fainted at the
sight of a roasted pig, if he had not time to run
away. These antipathies have been humor-
ously accounted for by the doctrine of the
transmigration of souls. Those who had been
flies in a former state were horribly afraid of
spiders; those who had been mice, did not like
cats; and those who had been cats did not love.
dogs, etc. Amatus Lusitanus knew a person
who fainted whenever he saw a rose, and al-
ways kept his house when they were in bloom.
Scaliger mentions the same about lilies, and
Bayle about honey. Bayle himself turned
pale at the sight of water-cresses; Tycho-Brahe
fainted at the sight of a fox; Henry III, of
France, at that of a cat, Marshal d'Albert at a
pig. A lady, wonderful enough, could not en-
dure the feel of silk or satin. A man, not so
strangely, was known to faint whenever he
heard a servant sweeping. Nicanor swooned
whenever he heard a bagpipe; Bayle fainted
when he heard the splashing of water.


18	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
TRAVELS BEYOND THE MISSISSIPPI.

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

(Continued.)

	On the 27th Horace mentions the boys and
girls going fishing. "At the same time," he
says, "we saw a number of teams crossing the
bridge on their way back. There were ten
wagons, which proved to be the property of
men who had left the Pawnee village about 120
miles above there, in consequence of the hostil-
ity of the Indians, where they had lived a
number of years. They were going to the vil-
lage below on the west side of the river, where
a number of their company had preceded them.
Among those we saw were four wagons and six
men belonging to Bishop Miller, who are tak-
ing a load back for them, and receive in pay-
ment for the same three hundred bushels of
grain, mostly wheat.	*	*	The occa-
sion of their leaving is this—a hostile tribe of
Indians called the Punkaws, who are constant-
ly roving about and are much addicted to
thieving, have lately made their appearance at
the Pawnee village, and molested them so
much with their thieving operations and other-
wise, that they are obliged to leave, the Indi-
ans having previously stolen all their horses.
The settlement of men who have thus left, for-
merly consisted of eight families, viz.: three
missionaries, three farmers, one blacksmith, and
a school teacher commissioned by government;
the school teacher had under his direction some
twenty Pawnee children, who have left with
him to go down to the said village, and whom
he is educating at the expense of the govern-
ment. Some of Bishop Miller's men told me
that the Pawnee village was totally deserted,
they having gone out on a hunting expedition.
They told me furthermore that they are living
on the "fat of the land," there being plenty of
garden sauce, such as potatoes, onions, peas,
etc., which the settlers had left behind, not be-
ing able to bring them away."
	We had been deprived of vegetables from
the time that we had left the states, and it had
already begun to tell upon us, though we had
no idea then of the scourge and suffering that
were lying before us; but we will not "cross
the bridge till we come to it." There was a
piece of unexpected good fortune happened to
Bishop Whitney which Horace relates:
	"When father's boys went down into the
country some time after provisions he sent $89
for the same, but through the mismanagement
of the agent he did not get more than half he
wanted, and consequently would have been un-
able to prosecute the journey had it not been that
the Lord, seemingly on purpose to repair this
misfortune or injury, put forth His hand in his
behalf. The circumstances were these: Bro.
Hyde being about to depart for England, just
before crossing the river again with his fami-
ly, let him have two yoke of oxen, ten barrels
of flour, and a bushel and a half of beans,
which to use his own phrase, "has completely
set me on my feet again," and he is now fitted
out to his heart's content.	*	*	At
the same time Bro. Parley P. Pratt gave him
$30 in gold for the same amount of money."	*
	"Tuesday, 28th. Beautiful morning.
About 2 p.m. it commenced raining, and con-
tinued showering through the day and night,
accompanied by considerable thunder and
lightning—it seemed to pour down almost per-
pendicularly and in perfect torrents, so that
there was not a wagon but what the rain pene-
trated through the cover thereof and wet all
the bed clothing, etc."
	This was one of the trials calculated to test
one's patience, a virtue which had been consid-
erably exercised during the five months that
we had spent picking and plodding our way
under difficulties which no language can des-
cribe, therefore must leave it for imagination

[Column 2]
to do, though Horace gives no slight idea:
"Wednesday, 29th. This morning fine and
pleasant, the atmosphere sweet and pure, in
contrast to the sultry heat of yesterday; almost
every wagon in camp was unloaded, in order
to dry the things therein.	*	*	Bros.
Kimball and Brigham went over the river to-
day to make some arrangements for the breth-
ren starting for England.	*	*	They
intend starting to-morrow. Since we have
been lying still our cattle have suffered from
the "footale." caused either by the mud or grass
getting between their hoofs; while traveling we
have never been troubled with it."
	The remedy made use of was to pour melted
rosin into the hoof, afterwards pulling a rope
backwards and forward through it to get out
the filth. Rather a rough remedy this. We
had showers at intervals for two or three days
and nights, and on the 30th Horace wrote:
	"During the storm on Tuesday evening an ox
belonging to Bro. Eldredge was struck by light-
ning and instantly killed. Father Lott came
up to-day, bringing considerable church prop-
erty, with some cattle and sheep, etc." and that
my father and mother went over the river to
visit a farm the same day, returning at even-
ing and bringing quite a quantity of green corn
—first we had seen that was fit to eat. He
wrote:
	"Another meeting was held this morning,
and a number of teams sent down to help those
who are crossing the river all the time, and are
constantly swelling the size of our camp with
their numbers."
	Next morning another meeting was called
and a number of men who had not yet been
were sent to the river to work. Father and
President Young went also. While they were
there the steamboat "General Brooks" came
down the river and stopped at the village on our
side about two miles below the ferry' 'Bros.
Parley P. Pratt, Orson Hyde, John Taylor, R.
Pierce and Little came over in a skiff and took
in Bros. Brigham and Kimball and went down
the river to overtake the boat, but when they
got to the village the steamboat had left, conse-
quently Bros. Taylor, Hyde and Pratt were
obliged to go down in a flat-boat in company
with some missionaries till they can overtake a
steamboat."
	"Saturday, August 1st.	*	*	*	*
Another lot of men were sent down to the river
this morning with teams.	*	*	To-
day a number of the Otoe Indians were here,
they had a lot of corn with them which they
had stolen from the cornfields of the Mohawks
close by here. The Otoes are a mean, thieving
race, and are also very cunning, which they
showed particularly in this case, knowing that
the taking of the corn would be laid on to us
by the Mohawks. Father, mother and little
brother are very sick to-day. Father took
cold sitting up and watching with mother, who
was very sick during last night, but is a little
better to-day"	*
	"Sunday, 2nd. Brothers Brigham and Kim-
ball and some others took a trip up the river
on this side to-day to find out a nearer road to
the place where they want to go, they returned
and held a council.	*	*	It is con-
templated to start on to-morrow or next day
and to go to some place to settle down and pre-
pare for winter.
	To-day, by Bro. K.'s advice and arrangement,
the family was divided into three messes, mak-
ing it more agreeable and convenient for all.
*	*	To-day put up my little tent again
*	*	*	Sarah Ann, Helen, Mary
Houston, George Billings and myself are to
occupy it."
	"Monday, 3rd. The weather still fine.	*
Brother Kimball sent ten yoke of oxen down
to the river to help up Bro. Clayton and fami-
ly.	*	*	Bro. Brigham let Bro. Kim-
ball have a quantity of beef to-day, an ox of

[Column 3]
his having jumped off a bridge and broken his
neck this forenoon.	*	The same
day he called the people of the camp together
and told them the intention of moving in the
morning, and received their vote and acquies-
cence in the same. Among other things he in-
formed them that they had found a good road
on the "divide" between the Missouri and Elk
Horn Rivers, and they had no doubt of finding
some good location. We started next morning
and traveled eight miles and encamped on the
prairie."


UNION.

	Union is something that ought to be cultiva-
ted and exist in every settlement of the Latter-
day Saints. And without it we cannot accom-
plish much good. We ought to try and be
united with each other as much as possible.
The Lord says, "Unless ye are one ye are not
mine." By that it seems that we must unite
or He will reject us. And without union our
meetings cannot be interesting. Then let us be
united in our meetings as well as in other
things. It seems that nothing worthy can be
accomplished without union. Without united
effort this stream of water that flows close by
cannot be controlled; one man's efforts would
be useless to control it, but the united efforts of
twenty or thirty have once controlled it, and
they will do it again.
	One grain of powder is harmless, but when a
barrel explodes it will tear the rocks to pieces.
Think of the great damage that was caused
by the united effect of the explosion of many
tons of powder in the magazine at Salt Lake
City. Who has not heard of the Indian chief
who, when on his dying bed, took a single ar-
row and broke it easily, and then said to his
sons, This is the way you will be broken after
your father is dead if you single out and di-
vide yourselves! And then he took a bunch of
arrows and told one of his sons to break them,
but be conld not; the father then said, This is
the way you will be if you all stay together
and remain as one; you cannot be broken.
Take a single match and light it and it is no-
thing, but unite it with other flames and its
power is wonderful. It will destroy property
of every kind. And where there is union al-
most anything can be accomplished.
						NORA SAVAGE.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	Henry Carey, a cousin to Queen Elizabeth,
after having enjoyed her majesty’s favor for
several years, lost it in this manner: As he was
walking in the garden of the palace, under the
queen’s window, she asked him, in a jocular
manner, "What does a man think when he is
thinking of nothing?" The answer was a very
brief one. "Upon a woman’s promise," he re-
plied. "Well done, cousin," said Elizabeth,
"excellent!" Some time after he solicited the
honor of a peerage, and reminded the queen
that she had promised it to him. "True," said
her majesty, "but that was a woman’s prom-
ise."—Ex.

	Maud—Isn't it dreadful, Julia, about the
banks, and Wall Street, and stocks, and every-
thing of that sort? Julia—Just shocking!
Politics are such dreadful things. Maud—And
don’t you know I have been worried to death
about pa! Julia—Why? Does your pa own
a bank? Maud—No: but he’s got lots to do
with mortgages and speculations and that sort
of things, and I just know that when I ask him
to get a new gold-plated set of harness for
Kitty, he’s going to look up with that awfully
solemn look and say, "My daughter, do you
read the papers?"—Washington Hatchet.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 19


HELEN AND VIRGINIA.

"Wait thou, my spirit, till he calls
In answer to thine earnest prayer;
Do thy full duty, guard thy steps,
	For oh, I feel a presence near
That almost parts the burdened air;
	I lean; I wait, the voice to hear."

And once, unconscious who was near, 
She held the boy and sang to him;	
The hearer’s eyes drank in the scene,
    	And knew what most his love had missed—
The mother-heart. His eyes grew dim
	As she the drowsy eyelids kissed.

More thoughtful daily grew his face;
If she had ever sought his eyes
She might have caught a shadow there
	Rising o’er her own peaceful day;
But In her own pure soul the skies
	Were blue and clear, and plain the way.

And his fair wife, like humming bird,
Flitted for pleasure here and there;
He would not check her radiant smile,
	Could not reprove her thoughtless life
But lover still, he strove to bear
	The fears with which his peace was rife

Restless she grew ’neath home’s dull claims,
Insatiate grew her vain desires;
The tender quiet of his face
	Passed o’er her like a shadow cold;
A thought, that leaped like mocking fires—
	“Was he not growing selfish, old?”

“Well, if he chooses to be tied down		"
To dull home circle, tame home songs, 
Why, there’s Virginia and the child,
	Safe company, at any rate.”  
For her the zest-inspiring throngs,
	In part, his fault should expiate.

What wonder then that pity grew 
Slowly within Virginia’s heart?
With gentle care she strove to make
	Home brighter for the fair one’s eyes,
Kept ev’ry troublous thing apart,
	As though her own peace were the prize;

In vain—except the young wife thanked
Virginia in her inmost-heart
For taking charge of dull affaire,
	Grateful indeed that some there were	
Contented to perform a part 		
	Long since distasteful grown to her.

To him, home grew the dearest place,
Full well he strove to weave its charm
Around the lovely wanderer;
	But stronger still the contrast grew—
Lilian, who loosed his guardian arm,
	Virginia, unto duty true.

“Come, Lilian, let us [--]ng to-night.”
Then answered she with weary air,
“I’m going out, and I must dress;
	Banish that cloud that shades your brow;
Sing with Virginia, I don’t care
	To sing that kind of music now.”

What perill for what power lies
In music, richer far than speech;
Who does not know some blessed strain
	That lives, a sacred monitor,
Recalling oft to the heart's reach
	Life's sweetest memories again?

What wonder that in time there grew
Love, pity's sister, in her soul?
Yet like a hidden prisoner,
	No eyes discerned his presence there, 
Her secret lived 'neath her control,
	Faithful ti duty, pure as prayer. 

Yet oft within her mind arose 
The ill-adjusted laws which bind 
The wothy and unworthy, one, 
	While neither happiness secure:
Nor either other bliss may find,
	But life long burden mest endure.

But for the child grown dear to her,
Virginia long ere this had gone,
But something in his clinging love
	Plead to her heart, not yet--not yet--
It held her fast, it led her on,
	Till the release she prayed was met.

Whence came the shaft that laid him low?
Was it the mother’s heart to wake?
Frightened and helpless as a child
	She stood apart—“Virginia, come,
He calls for you, that you might take
	Him in your arms.”  Pallid and dumb.

She raised him in her arms once more;
“Rock me,” he said, then looking ’round,
“Kiss me good night, papa, mamma;
	Virginia, I am going where
I dreamed, and told you—hold me tight—
	I dreamed ’twas you who took me there.”
He laid his check upon her heart,
They hushed their sobs, nor touched his hand:
Virginia’s eyes beamed down in his
	Love, courage, faith; he smiled, and then,
Half dreaming, whispered, “there !” and
	Drew one deep sigh, nor breathed again.
With her life's secret, and its loss,
With empty arms, no longer now
Did duty plead a double cause,
	Nor felt her sacred sorrow yield
License to meet the hallowed love
	Beside the precious dead revealed.

For him, life spread like ocean vast,
False, shining waves that dancing leapt
To changing music ev’ry hour
	Above the treacherous, hungry dcep;
Rude winds in shreds the sails had swept,
	And left him all alone to keep

Watch o’er the frail, dismantled bark,
or Lilian’s soul was like her face,
And both like April’s fleeting day,
	With sudden bursts of shower, then
Its sunshine, carols, and its flowers.
	All beauty, life and warmth again.

He knew that soon her soul would rise
Freer and lighter than before,
And that the sphere whose shining dome
	His hope and love had gilded bright
Was but a hollow, glittering toy,
	A bubble, broken in his sight.

His name—through generations old
Sire unto son had, dying, left
The ancestral home and honored name;
	With him that name would die away,
His household of his son bereft,
	No hope illumed his darkened day.

And as a bird by chill winds driven
Seeks refuge till the storm is spent
In e’en the gnarled and hollow bough,
	So to her kindred, distant, cold,
Virginia for a brief time went,
	Nor dreamed the new life to unfold.

Some idle words that caught her ear,
Disdain, contempt, for some new creed,
Woke a desire, an inquiry—
	“What is this faith so strangely new
And so delusive, that indeed
	Divines fear ’twill their work undo?”


[Column 1]
SOME OF THE REASONS WHY.

	The following is in answer to a letter from a
Gentile lady in Cincinnati, making some in-
quiries in regard to our principles and institu-
tions:
		SALT LAKE CITY, March 23rd, 1884.
DEAR MRS. ELKINS:
	I am sorry your inquiry has been so long neg-
lected, perhaps the fact of my having a fam-
ily of five children and no servant may be a
satisfactory explanation.
	I do not wonder at strangers puzzling over
our state of life, because it is something new,
practically; its history in the Bible is like a
story of the past, respected because within the
sacred pages that have been the only light and
hope of the Christian world.  But what God
has once established can He not establish again,
even though men had lain aside the ancient
customs and even made laws against it?  The
reason that so many are unsatisfied, and some-
times even disgusted with the monogamic sys-

[Column 2]
tem of marriage, is because it is like a house
built upon the sand, with no foundation of di-
vine principle, authority, or promise of hope
of its perpetuity beyond the grave.  The world
has lived for self not God, the present not the
future. I do not feel to be an instructor in so
great a matter, but will try and reply to your
different inquiries according to my own expe-
rience and observance, for I would feel con-
demned to remain silent.
	I was a plural wife two years, and then the
first wife of my husband died, after a brief ill-
ness, being of apoplectic habit. It was with
her full consent (or I would never have entered
her family) also that of her children, that I
became her husband's and their father's
second wife.  During this two years we
never exchanged unkind words; she
was many years my senior, and perhaps
looked on me something as she would have
done on a younger sister.  I always bore in
mind that she had made a great sacrifice of
her personal feelings, through zealous devotion
and conscientious noble principle, that she had
risked all on the altar of her faith, trusting in
God and the honor of her husband and myself
to do our part in this most solemn coven-
ant.
	The woman heart and nature, as well as the
man heart and nature, are the result of genera-
tions of training and a selfish looking out for
self.  Take the Bible from the earth and in a
few centuries, or less, what would the Chris-
tian world become?  Women and men can be
perfectly happy in this order of marriage if 
they are guided by principle, and not by frivol-
ous fancy, or mercenary or ambitious mo-
tives.
	Now, when the evening comes, aud the hus-
band is away?  Well, if the husband is a real
Latter-day Saint his wife or wives,who do not that
evening have his society, know just where he is,
that he is not down town in a saloon,or anywhere
else practicing infidelity to his marriage vows,
and trying to deceive them. They know that
he stands more in awe of his Creator and the
guardian angels that we believe are continually
beside us keeping a faithful record of our
words and deeds, than of all the earthly courts
and laws the libertine fears.  That the loss of
the confidence of his brethren and family
would be his sorest penalty.  This wife would
gather her children around her in order, tran-
quility and happiness, and in quiet industry
employ their time and instill into their minds
that they are the most blessed, most hopeful,
and surest of present and eternal happiness, of
all God's creatures upon the earth. She feels to
give herself and her children to the service of
His plan, the strengthening, enduring and up-
building of His Kingdom, by their influence,
labors, prayers and aspirations.  These themes,
and not the trivial carnal crosses, occupy their
minds.  Not repinings—more knowledge is the
prayer.  Love of man is bondage.  Love of
God and His principles, with a just man, as
God's representative, for companionship, both
with the same mutual aim, righteousness here,
the courts of God hereafter, fill their minds
too well for them to stoop to or mourn for the
phantom joys of changeful, weak humanity,
bestowing or withdrawing.
	I fear I have not answered you after all.
Write again.		Yours truly,
			AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


Some readers are like the hour-glass (their
reading is as the sand; it runs in and runs out
but leaves not a vestige behind); some like a
sponge, which imbibes everything, and returns
it in the same state only a little dirtier; some
like a jelly-bag which allows all that is good
to pass away, and retains only the refuse and
dregs. The fourth class may be compared to
the slave of Golconda, who casting away all
that is worthless,preserves only the pure gems.
—Coleridge.

SALT LAKE CITY, - JULY 1, 1884.


THE HOAR AMENDMENT.

	It is even more than "passing strange" that
such a measure as the Hoar amendment to
the Edmunds bill, could by any means have
passed the Senate.  And one cannot help feel-
ing somewhat horror-stricken when considering
to what base ends the law has been made to
band. The fact stands recorded in Congress
that this most extraordinary document did posi-
tively pass the Senate on the 18th of June, 1884.
It is, however, worthy of recognition that there
were certain members of that august body of
men, who had the nobility and the courage to
vote against this most unjust and unscrupulous
measure.  One cannot help admiring and re-
specting such men; who will not for popularity,
influence, or any sinister motive, vote for what
they consider an illegal and unconstitutional
law; who are determined to main [-]in and honor
the principles of the Constitution, framed by the
honorable "fathers of our country" to protect
the people in all the rights and privileges that
pertain to l[-]fe, liberty and the pursuit of happi-
ness, notwiths anding they are opposed to the
patriarchal order of marriage (approved of God
and sanctioned by His prophets in olden times),
which is a religious principle of the Latter-day
Saints who inhabit Utah, and for which they
are called in question in the highest courts in
the nation in which they reside, censured and
dis[-]ranchised, and this not being thought suffi-
cient punishment, they are to receive not on y
further indignities, but to be forced, if possible,
into a condition of abject serfdom, taxed to
maintain laws they have no part in making,
and rulers they can have no right in choosing.
All this and more in a country which professes
religious liberty and offers a shelter for the op-
pressed of all nations, and in a Territory which
they themselves have rendered habitable by
toil, industry and diligence, driven as they
were by the exigencies of their situation,
brought about by mobs and violence, into a
wilderness where only wild beasts and savage
hordes roamed at will, yet glad to escape from
persecution and find a haven of peace an[-] rest
far from civilization, home and friends, that they
might worship God according to the dictates of
their own conscience, and obey the principles
revealed to them without fear or molestation.
The blessing of God having descended upon this
people in the desert land, and we having greatly
increased and multiplied in numbers, the anger,
indignation and jealousy of many of the over-
pious and unprincipled men and women of this
great Republic has been kindled into fever heat,
and as year after year rolled by and all measures
and schemes formed against this "despised peo-
ple" failed in checking the growth and develop-
ment of "Mormon" interest, it began to becon-

[Column 2]
sidered a difficult matter, a problem, a knotty
question, and one of the utmost importance to
get rid of.  Great, wise and learned men have
wrestled with this matter in vain, and still the
little grain of mustard seed sown in obscurity,
and insignificant, has grown and become a great
tree, and its branches have spread far and wide,
and have given shelter to many people, and it
is alarming in the eyes of the world, who know
not the marvellous work and wonder which is be-
ing brought to pass in this the last dispensation
of the fulness of times.
	But these are themes one can go on elaborat-
ing to almost any extent, for they are illimitable,
but suffice it to say, the tree cannot be rooted
up, or if it is, the scattering of innumberable in-
finitesimalseeds will "take root and flourish,"
and it will be found impossible to crush it out of
existence.
	When o e considers that this nation in dealing
with the momentous question of so termed
"Mormonism" is measuring arms with the
Almighty, it seems absurd to notice what is be-
ing done, but when one contemplates the doings
of men in these matters, and considers the bear-
ing it has upon men and women, causing suffer-
ing mental and physical, one is forced to deal
with the matter of aggression in defense of
rights, civil, political and religious.  When the
strong oppress the weak, when the nation
makes specific laws for certain classes of its
actual citizens, is it not time for rational
men and woman to resort to all just ex-
pedients to ward off and avert the calamity?
for it can be termed nothing else.  Every
woman interested in her country and her
own individual rights must be roused to
serious thought upon the action of the Sen-
ate in the passing of this amendment.  It
is perhaps true that the House will never go
so far as to commit the same error, but that
does not lessen the heavy responsibility taken
upon themselves by the men who voted for this
prohibitory law.
	Section 7 of this bill abolishes woman suffrage
in this Territory.  This is the little insect that
will sting sharply the men who voted for this
amendment, for although there are many suffra-
gists that hate "Mormonism" and the "Mor-
mons" as much as these same Senators, yet labor-
ing as they are and have been to obtain the bal-
lot, they will fight to the last any measure which
proposed to disfranchise women who have had
the right of franchise bestowed upon them, es
pecially as they are well aware all women are
already deprived of this right, who have ever at
any time been plural wives.  Senator Hoar has
made a great blunder in allowing that section
to come before the Senate, and he will lose cas[-]e
with his own party, and be censured and re-
proached for this egrigious folly of thus over-
shooting the mark; in other words he has acted
against his own avowed principles of right and
justice.


EDITORIAL NOTES

	THE Woman's Journal published in Boston,
dated June 21st, in reference to the vote upon the
Utah Bill in the Senate, after giving particulars
and names of men who voted yeas and nays,
says:
	"It now goes to the House. If this bill, with
the anti suffrage clause in it, should become law,
it will be a shameful injustice. Righteous
and equitable legislation directed against, poly-
gamy should be welcomed by all who have the
good of women at heart; but a law like this,
manifestly and glaringly unjust, does not de-
serve to succeed."


WOMEN'S HOME MISSIONARY
WORK.

	Sisters M. Isabella Horne, Elmina Taylor, and
Louie Felt left this city Wednesday morning,
June 12th, to make a tour through Millard
Stake, and hold meetings with the Relief So-
cieties, Y. L. M. I. A. and Primary Associations.
Traveled 400 miles and held twenty-two meet-
ings. At Leamington Sister Felt organized a
Primary Association on the 12th inst., with
Mrs. Mary Pay, President, and at Fillmore a re-
organization of the Y. L. M. I. A. was effected,
and Mrs. Delilah K. Olsen was appointed Presi-
dent.
	The sisters visited and held three meetings in
each settlement in the Stake except Leaming-
ton and Oak City, where only two meetings
were held. Sister Elizabeth Yates, of Scipio,
met the sisters at Leamington, and accompan-
ied them in their visit through the Stake. The
bishops everywhere were interested in the work
they were laboring to accomplish, and gave
them a hearty welcome, rendering them all the
assistance in their power. President Hinckley
was also much gratified at meeting with them
at Deseret, and also at Filmore, and urged them
to come again.
	Sisters Eliza R. Snow Smith; Margaret P.
Young and Elizabeth Howard went to Grants-
ville on Friday, June 13, to attend the Sister's
Conference of Tooele Stake, which commenced
on the 14th and closed on the 15th. There was
a Primary Fair held also, which was a very suc-
cessful affair, and the little children's work,
much of it, was very artistic and particularly
well executed.
	On Sunday, June 15th, a special meeting
was held at Granger Ward, for the purpose of
organizing a Relief Society in the ward, which
has only recently been set off and fully organ-
ized. Bishop D. McCrae presided over the
meating. His Counselor, John Banden, acted
as secretary. Opening prayer by Counselor
Abram Sorenson. The Bishop proceeded to or-
ganize by placing before the meeting the names
of Sister Mary E. Porter as President; Sister
Elizabeth Park, First Counselor; Sister Mary A.
Bess, Second Counselor; Sister Catharine Mak-
ay, Secretary; Sister Betsey Hall, Assistant
Secretary; Sister Phyldes Warr, Treasurer. The
nominations were unanimously approved. The
officers were set apart by the Bishop and his
Counselors. The meeting was afterwards ad-
dressed by Sisters B. W. Smith, M. W. Wilcox
and E. B. Wells, who were present by invita-
tion. The sisters who had just been appointed
to office each expressed a willingness to perform
the duty assigned, and try to magnify the office
to which they had been called.
	The Seventeenth annual meeting of the Re-
lief Society at South Cottonwood was held June
17th, at the Ward meeting house at 10
a.m., which was well filled with sisters; a
few brethren were also in the audience.
	Mrs. Jeanette B. Cahoon, a very able and effi-
cient president, who has filled that office from
its organization, presided, and great credit is due
her for the active as well as the spiritual labor
she has accomplished during that time.
	The minutes and reports were very good; Sis-
ter Harriet Ann Walker is a competent Secre-
tary, though she has not been long in the office.
Sisters E. R. S. Smith, E. B. Wells and Dr. Ro-
mania B Pratt, who had been invited to attend,
each addressed the meeting, though Sister Eliza
occupied most of the time, speaking upon
union, the duties of teachers, the importance of
their work, the necessity of progression the de-
sire we have to know more of our Father and
mother in heaven; referred to our consenting to
come here and take up our abode, forgetting the


past, in order, to prove our integrity to our hea-
venly parents, and exhorted the sisters to study
the word of God and seek to draw near to Him;
and gave much good instruction on various mat-
ters.
	Sister M. A. Rawlins occupied a few mo-
ments and entreated the sisters to attend their
meetings.
	At 2 p.m. the Y. L. M. I. A. convened at the
same place, and after the usual exercises of sing-
ing and prayer, Sister Eliza addressed the young
ladies. She urged them to lay a foundation for
future life and happiness, by becoming good
house keepers; entreated them to be practically
useful, as well as to learn accomplishments, and
to prepare themselves for the great work that
lay before them; attend to their meetings, and
seek to attain to excellence in all things truly
noble and elevating.
	Sister E. B. Wells was the next speaker, her
remarks were mostly in reference to the all im-
portant matter of understanding their own or-
ganization, and what was essential to life and
health, and the best methods of attaining to the
full development of every faculty with which
God had endowed them for the fulfilment of
their probation here on earth.
	Sister Jane Freeze made a few remarks very
suggestive to the young ladies, and was followed
by Sister Maggie C. Shipp, who bore a faithful
testimony to what she had received in regard to
the truth. Her words were earnest and her
manner most emphatic.
	After these sisters had finished speaking, Sis-
ter Eliza R. S. Smith made some explanations
in regard to a reorganization of the Y. L. M. I.
A., and presented the resignation of the Presi-
dent, Mrs. Annie Thompson, to be voted upon.
The resignation was accepted, and the following
officers were proposed, and a unanimous vote
was carried, Sister Eliza officiating in reorganiz-
ing:
	Isabella Benbow Evison, President; Annie E.
Labun and Annie Thompson, Counselors; Rosa
A. Kelch, Secretary; M. Antoinette Cahoon,
Assistant Secretary; Anna Smith, Treasurer.
	Bishop Rawlins addressed the young ladies;
told them those who preside must never tire if
they expect to be successful. The Bishop and
his Counselor set the officers apart. Meeting
adjourned for one year. Benediction by Coun-
selor Wheeler.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 21


[Column 1]
LETTER XIII.

LONDON AND PARIS.

	We arrived in London last night, after a long
and somewhat tiresome ride from Paris, which,
however, was much more pleasant than we ex-
pected it would be, for we had only two hours on
the English Channel, and that without one
touch of sea-sickness. Some of our compan-
ions were not nearly so well blessed as we, and
the slight unsteady motion of the boat quite up-
set them.
	You, I know, are anxious to hear more of our
visit in Paris, so I wont trouble you with any-
thing further about our journey to, or arrival in
London, but will try to go back to our first few
days in the glorious French capital, if my mem-
ory will serve me well enough.
	When I last wrote it was a rainy, disagreeable
day (excuse reference to the weather), but the
next day, Tuesday, was sunny and bright, and
we started out early for the Louvre, notwith-
standing our guide book recommends a prelim-
inary drive the first day, and the rainy days to
be reserved for the picture galleries. As we
were not weather prophets, and could not tell
when the rainy days would come, if come they
must, and as we took a preliminary walk the

[Column 2]
first day and located ourselves and the sights we
were most anxious to see, we decided to follow
our own inclinations, and visit those places
which held most interest for us first, in case
something should occur to shorten our stay, so,
as I before stated, we started first for the Louvre.
This is the most elegant palace in France, and
I think in the world. It was commenced some
few centuries ago, and each succeeding monarch
has endeavored to complete it, but it still re-
mains unfinished. At length when the original
design was almost carried out the Communists
attempted to destroy the whole edifice, but suc-
ceeded in their dreadful design only to burn one
wing; that, however, contained the valuable
library, and was an irreparable loss. After a
brief survey of the outside of the palace we en-
tered the picture gallery, and wandered from
one hall to another in admiration of this most
valuable collection.
	The Italian masters are exceedingly well re-
presented here, but so [---] I have seen, Dres-
den possesses the gem of Italian art, in Raphael's
Sistine Madonna. I cannot begin to enumerate
or describe the beautiful pictures of this gallery,
but can only say they all are most wonderful
and rare gems of art, and gave me the most ex-
quisite pleasure to have the privilege of seeing
them. The principal pride of the Louvre, how-
ever, is the collection of statuary, and the price-
less treasure is the Venus of Milo. It is a work
of art one wants to see once, only to long to see
again. You would hardly believe such power
of expression, such beauty of form, could be
wrought from a piece of marble. Our first day
in the Louvre we occupied in seeing all that was
possible, and locating the different objects, so
that our next visit we could spend in the rooms
containing the principal treasures. It took us a
long time the first day, and we left much fati-
gued. The place is so large that they say it
takes two hours to walk through the rooms with-
out stopping, so you can fancy how long it would
take one to look at the innumerable pictures,
statues, jewels, models, etc., that the palace con-
tains.
	Much of our time, and I believe our pleasant-
est moments, were spent in this gallery, but we
also visited the other art museums; among the
first the Luxembourg, with the gardens laid out
in the old Renaissance style. With these we were
particularly delighted, but with the art collec-
tion most dreadfully disappointed. The muse-
um contains only works of modern masters, and
they seemed to me wholly unworthy a place in
any museum. However, as I don't profess to b
an art critic, and people nowadays seem to ad-
mire these glaring sketches, perhaps I had bet-
ter not attempt to say anything further on the
subject. The palace was filled with students,
copying and studying the style of the modern
masters. One of our last days we went to Ver-
sailles. Oh! how I wish you could have been
with us. It was a most delightful day, and as
we walked round the old magnificent garden,
with its lakes, forests, fountains, statues,
orangery, terraces and flowers, I thought how
much you would enjoy it all, and said we could
never get mother back to the city if she once
were in this garden.
	The palace itself from the outside is not very
imposing, but the gardens are the most beauti-
ful in France, and the palace and grounds to-
gether cost the court of Louis XIV the fabulous
sum of a thousand million francs. The palace
is decorated almost entirely with portraits of the
different French rulers, and pictures illustrating
the history of the glories of France, so here, for
the first time, after our arrival in Paris, we saw
some honor shown to Napoleon; room after
room was hung with pictures of his battles and
scenes of his life. Many pictures of himself

[Column 3]
and of Maria Louise, but only one of the Em
press Josephine. Little honor, it seems to me,
do the French show to those to whom honor is
due.
	The most magnificent hall in the palace is one
extending along the east side and looking over
the garden from seventeen different windows.
In this hall King Wilhelm, of Prussia, was made
Emperor of Germany, when the Germans were
in possession of the palace in '71. Opening from
this room towards the south side is a beautiful
suite of rooms, which have been used as the
chambers and drawing rooms of Marie Antion-
ette, Marie Theresa and Marie Leczinska. We
wandered, rather accidentally, into a hall which
led into the apartments of Marie Antoinette.
This is the only part of the palace not open free
to visitors, and shown by guides. The guide in
waiting hailed us and ushered us in. Such lit-
tle gems of rooms they were; the whole suite
scarcely larger than one of the other rooms of
the palace, and filled with rare little treasures
of this unhappy queen. They were draped in
pale blue, orange and red brocaded silks. Every-
thing, even the bath-room, unique, elegant and
beautiful; but in one was a mirror where one
could see herself without a head, thus showing
the fate of the fair and beautiful Marie Antoin-
ette. The pretty little rooms remind one of the
exquisite taste and elegance of their possessor;
but also bring a feeling of sadness as we think
of her dreadful fate, and then the other horrors
of the revolution. Not a palace or a beautiful
place in Paris but recalls either some horror of
the revolution, or of the Commune of '71, and
their monuments and fountains, with all their
sparkling beauty, fail to hide the terrible scenes
of these times, or to wash from history the
stains of blood.
	Of course we visited the tomb of Napoleon,
and the Hotel des Invalides. This was one of
our most important days. The church of the In-
valides really consists of two parts; the Dome, and
the church of St. Louis. The sarcophagus is placed
in the former in a deep made crypt, and the
pale blue light issuing from the stained windows
above gives a most solemn aspect to the place.
We stayed here some time, thinking of the vic-
tories of Napoleon as they were recalled by the
sixty faded flags, which are placed around his
tomb, and left rather reluctantly for the Jardin
des Plan'e, where we spent the remainder of the
day.
	Our visit was a most delightful one, and we
enjoyed every minute of it, still I did not
leave Paris reluctantly.
	Yesterday I stopped writing to go and hear
the Rev. Dr. Parker, in Holborn Church, and
after that we visited Westminster Abbey. Of
this, however, I will say no more at present.
This morning I am going to the Tower. John
Q. is well acquainted with London, and
shows me around like one who had always lived
here. A week from to-morrow we expect to
sail for home, and then only think how short a
time and we shall all be together again. I for-
got to mention that we saw Longfellow's bust
in Westminster, in the Poet's Corner just to
the left of Dryden's monument. It is a very fine
piece of work, and says, "Placed here by the
English admirers of an American poet."
	Much love for all, father, mother, sisters,
brothers, and precious little Q.
								ANNIE.


Y. L. M. I. A. BRIGHAM CITY.

	On the 11th of June, with Mrs. Elizabeth
Howard, left this city to attend the Young Ladies'
Conference and Fair of Box Elder Stake, in
Brigham City. The weather was delightful and
the orchards and farms all along the route pre-


sented a most charming picture. After leaving
the city with its bustle and noise we enjoyed
the ride through the beautiful farms and green
fields along the way.
	Upon arrive we were received and enter-
tained by Mrs. Minnie J. Snow, President, and
were soon refreshed and ready to attend the
meeting in the afternoon. After the usu-
al exercises very interesting and beneficial
remarks were made by Sister Howard, fol-
lowed by a sister from Willard and many of the
young ladies.
	It indeed filled one with amazement to hear
these beautiful young ladies bear such testimon-
ies, and exhibit such knowledge, intelligence
and self control. I feel sure it would encourage
some of the young ladies, who never take part
in these associations, if they could have heard
the many beautiful thoughts that were express-
ed that day. It at once proves the benefits that
can be obtained from these meetings. They
enable us to build each other up, and develop
those faculties within us, which we knew not of
ourselves.
	Closing remarks were made by the President
of the Y. L. M. I. A., after which the meeting
was adjourned.
	In the evening we had the pleasure of attend-
ing the fair. I may truly say I was surprised
to see the excellent taste displayed in the ar-
rangement and in the articles exhibited, and the
willingness of the young ladies to assist in all
good works. Some of the articles were really
works of art, and all showed the industry and
wonderful energy of the young ladies. Home-
made hats just as pretty as could be purchased
at any of our millinery shops, trimmed in ex-
quisite taste, so[-]a cushions, baskets, netting,
bead work, embroidery of the finest kind on
homemade linen mats, tidles of all descriptions,
slippers, wax work, all kinds of beautiful needle
work, artificial flowers, collars, drawn work,
[---], bed spreads of drawn and netted work,
carved wood, quilts, netted curtains, pillow
s[-]ams, laces and collars, sketches in penci[-], and
oil paintings, and so many beautiful and use[-]ul
articles that it would be impossible to mention
or do justice in describing them. The Indians
from Thistle Valley sent some very nice articles,
showing that the work among them has not
been in vain. One of the young Indian women
sent some needle work; a baby's white dress
[-]ucked and trimmed with embroidery. Other ar-
ticles, such as woollen stockings, aprons and
everything in the way of bead embroidery,
purses, cushions, hand satchel, moccasins, and
all were quite handsome; also copy books filled
with their writing, and it would do well for
some of us to practice until we could form our
letters as well, for they were excellent.
	The fair was conducted by the young ladies,
and the contributors were all from the stake.
The lower end of the room was fitted up for re-
freshments, where they served the most delicious
strawberries, ice cream, cake and fruits. Alto-
gether the fair was a thorough success, and re-
flects great credit on the President and young
ladies
	May we have many more such meetings and
fairs, and may God bless all those who are seek-
ing and working for the good of these societies
and associations. I enjoyed my visit very much
indeed, and have a great admiration for those
young ladies who have shown such nobility and
courage to meet together for mutual improve-
ment.						LOUIE WELLS.


22	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


FORTY-EIGHTH CONGRESS.

SENATE.

	Washington, 18.—Consideration of the Utah
bill was resumed. Hoar offered an amend-


ment to strike out the clause abolishing woman
suffrage in Utah; rejected, 17 ayes, 34 noes.
Those voting in the affirmative were Blair,
Bower, Brown, Conger, Cullom, Dawes, Dolph,
George, Hoar, Lapham, Manderson, Mitchell,
Palmer, Pike, Sherman, Vance and Wilson.
	Brown explained his vote by saying he was
personally altogether opposed to woman suf-
frage, but regarding it as an entirely local mat-
ter: and as it had been established in a territory
he would vote against the United States gov-
ernment interfering with it. The bill then
passed. 33 yeas, 15 nays, as follows, yeas: Al-
lison, Beck, Bower, Cameron (Wisconsin), Col-
quist, Conger, Cullom, Dawes, Dolph, Ed-
munds, Fair, Frye, Garland, Harrison, Ingalls,
Jackson, Jones (Fla.), Jones (Nev.), Lapham,
McMillan, McPherson, Mahone, Manderson,
Miller (Cal.), Mitchell, Merrill, Palmer, Pike,
Pugh, Riddleberger, Sawyer, Voorhees and
Wilson, 33. Nays, Bayard, Brown, Coke,
George, Hampton, Jones, Kenna, Lamar,Max-
ey, Morgan, Pendleton, Ransom, Vance, Vest
and Walker, 15.


[Column 1]
LETTER TO MRS. B. A. EVANS,
LEHI.

A CONSOLATORY COMMUNICATION.

POSTGASSE 36, Bern, Switzerland,
			April 29, 1884.

	Dear Sister—I trust you will excuse my pre-
sumption in addressing you, with whom I have
not the pleasure of an acquaintance at all; and
though it is far from my desire at the present
time to recall the sad and harrowing circum-
stances of the past few weeks in your grief-
stricken feelings, yet I consider it no less a
duty than a pleasure to make a brief report to
you of the manner in which your lamented son
and our sincerely-mourned brother conducted
himself during the short mission he was per-
mitted to fill in our midst.
	In obedience to the call made of him at the
October Conference, 1883, he left his home and
beloved ones for a mission in Switzerland and
Germany, and reached this city, after short
visits with his fellow Elders in London and
Paris, on Sunday morning the 11th of Novem-
ber. In our evening meeting of that day he
bore his testimony to the truth of the work he
had traveled so far to proclaim, and expressed
the buoyant hope that he would soon be able to
talk to the people in their own language.
	The next day I gave the Elders their ap-
pointments to the different fields of labor, also
such instructions as I thought appropriate. At
the close of the meeting Brother Evans re-
quested that we administer to him, as he felt a
lassitude and weariness, also a cold and other
symptoms of an approaching fever. This we
did unitedly. Next morning, Tuesday the 13th,
had been selected for the departure of the
brethren, but as his journey was so long—to
Berlin—I endeavored to persuade him to stay
a few days longer with us until he had com-
pletely recovered. He preferred to go, how-
ever, his ambition to begin work being so great
that he could not feel comfortable in remain-
ing after having received his appointment. Be-
fore leaving them that night (the brethren all
slept in the office) I got him to promise me
that if he did not feel better in the morning
he would not undertake the journey, and so
bade him good night. When I came next
morning he was not present, and I was inform-
ed that he felt better and had decided to go
with the others. After he reached Berlin I
heard once or twice from him personally, also
from his companions who came to Conference
at Christmas and reported him then as getting
better. Ten days later I was shocked to hear
from the Elder who went there to join him,
that his fever was still unbroken and that a
change was imperatively necessary.

[Column 2]
	I wrote to Prest. Smith at Liverpool at once
and he summoned him to that place, whither
he proceeded, accompanied by one of our El-
ders, by the next steamer. Prest. Smith wrote
me twice that he was improving, and then I
read in the Star that he had gone home. The
next news was the mournful and wholly unex-
pected intelligence that he was no more. This
was a great blow to us all, particularly those
who had traveled here with him and learned to
know him.
	He showed himself a remarkably quick and
intellectual spirit, the possessor of unusual
ability and energy, and, had he been spared,
would undoubtedly have proven himself one of
the best missionaries ever sent out. His heart
seemed to be all in the work placed upon him,
and he had no rest, even when sick, unless he
could be studying his books. He would have
grasped the language in a very short time, and
what was still better he was always able to give
a scriptural and reasonable ground for the
hope he had within him. But He who rules
the universe felt to call him hence, and we,
his sorrowing friends, have to subject ourselves
to the decree.
	Happy is he who left us, for his race though
short was well and nobly run; and happy may
we be, who remain, if we are able to say from
the depths of our bruised yet willing and obe-
dient hearts: "Thy will be done; the Lord giv-
eth, the Lord taketh away, blessed be the name
of the Lord."
	I feel myself incompetent to offer any words
of consolation; but I assure you, dear Sister
Evans, that my prayers have been offered up
and will still be that the comforting spirit, of
whom it is written that it robs death of its
sting and the grave of its victory, may be be-
stowed upon you, and that you and your be-
reaved family may feel the near and hallowed
presence of Him who has promised to wipe
away all tears from the eyes of His people. And
may the bright example of him who is gone to
join his departed loved ones and his brethren,
ever remain as a shining beacon for the young
men of Israel, that they may ever be found
ready warriors for the cause of truth to assist
in putting its opposing hordes to fight and its
enemies under their feet.
	My wife and my associates here join me in
expressing our condolence, and our sincere wish
that in this hour of trial the friend of the
afflicted may be near to comfort and sustain
you. With the kindest regards, I have the hon-
or to subscribe myself,
		Your Brother in the Gospel,
						JOHN Q. CANNON.


FROM A GENTILE.

		APPLE GROVE, Ala., Feb. 15, 1884.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I am a regular reader of your valuable pa-
per. Although I live away out here in Ala-
bama, I know something of the doctrine and
teaching and practices of that persecuted peo-
ple called Mormons. I have never been among
them, but have read several books and papers
that give me a pretty correct knowledge of
the Mormon character, and I admire their
magnanimity, self-denial, industry, and for-
bearance towards their enemies. They have
built up a great State in the barren regions of
the Rocky Mountains, under the most unfavor-
able circumstances, and suffered many priva-
tions and hardships for the sake of religious
liberty; and still they can't be let alone. And
I find from reading newspapers from the other
way that their worst enemies are of that class
spoken of in the Bible as the salt of the earth
that has lost its savor, and said to be worthless
and good for nothing but to be trampled under
the feet of men. And God in His wisdom
may do that way for them yet, for they seem to
have lost the copy and standard of righteous-


ness, and the spirit of the Gospel as taught by
Jesus Christ. This is the way it appears to one
outside who is not biased by creeds. But from
my knowledge of the Bible and the Mormons,
they have more of the true spirit of Christiani-
ty than any order of people I know. Why
not let them alone? Is it on account of their
plural marriage system? It does look like the
ladies ought to be allowed to contract for them-
selves in the all-important matter of marriage,
and let the laws of God as revealed in the book
of human nature regulate things.
	Now, Mrs. Editor, if this is worth printing
in the EXPONENT, let it come, I am not
ashamed to own the opinion I have formed
about the Mormons.
					JOHN M. RUSSELL.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT 23


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	We live in a somewhat out-of-the-way place
and I suppose you do not hear from us very
often. Our Relief Society is in a prosperous
condition; it was organized in January, 1883,
and has continued to improve since that time.
Our worthy President, Sister Catherine Hatch,
is doing all she can to instruct us in the great
latter-day work, which is still rolling on. The
Lord says that man cannot stop its progress,
and it seems that His words are being fulfilled
every day of our lives. The whole world is
arrayed against us as a people, and they are
always planning something for our overthrow,
but we still continue to grow and improve. It
seems to me we ought to be very thankful
to think that the Lord has enlightened our
minds enough to comprehend the truth of His
work. We are blest above all other people, if
the world is against us. I feel that the Lord
is mightier than all, and if we are faithful He
will bring us off conquerors.
	We have a Y. L. M. I. A. here; it was or-
ganized in Feb., 1884, and it is doing very
well, considering the short time it has been or-
ganized. The young ladies deserve credit for
punctual attendance.
	Our Sunday School and Primary is in a
prosperous condition, and the young are pre-
paring themselves for a noble and great work.
Their privileges of learning are far ahead of
what their parents had. My prayers are that we
may all continue to improve and be thankful
for the blessings that we enjoy. I wish the EX-
PONENT success.
			Your sister in the Gospel,
									L. L. S.
	Woodruff, Arizona, May 1st, 1884.


[Column 1]
R. S. Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
REPORTS.

RICHFIELD.

	Minutes of the Eighteenth Quarterly Con-
ference of the Relief Society of Sevier Stake,
held at Richfield, March 14th, 1884, President
Elizabeth Bean presiding. Usual opening ex-
ercises. President Bean said she was pleased
to feel that a good spirit had met with us, even
though none of the leading sisters were with
us.
	Minutes of the previous conference read and
accepted, also the statistical and financial re-
port. Five societies were verbally reported to
be in a prospering condition, and doing what
they could towards assisting in rolling on the
latter-day work, and other good instructions of-
fered by the Presidents of the Societies.
	Sister Thirza Thurber felt to encourage the
sisters to live near to God, to thoroughly study
the principles of the Gospel and learn all we
could, to set a good example before our child-
ren; bore testimony to the good spirit one felt
when working in the temple. Referred to Bro.

[Column 2]
Grant's vision; hoped God would bless and aid
us.
	Sister Jane Spencer felt well to meet in con-
ference; thought we should store our minds with
useful knowledge, that we might be enabled to
converse intelligently, and teach our children
to be good and praiseworthy.
	Prest. Bean was glad to hear of the societies
being so willing to perform the duties assigned
them; desired the sisters to be united and not
allow nationality to separate them into classes;
desired them to visit the Y. L. M. I. A. and P.
A.; appointed some to go as missionaries to
these associations. Said the Lord would help
us if we would try to help ourselves; we should
work together and love one another.
	Meeting adjourned until 2 o'clock. Singing.
Benediction.
	2 p.m. Singing, prayer offered by Bishop
G. W. Bean, singing. Two other societies re-
ported as doing well, being united and anxious
to perform the missions assigned them.
	Sisters E. Bean and C. A. Hunt spoke upon
the subject of reports, desiring the secretaries
to study the subject; gave some instructions and
illustrations upon the subject.
	Bros. Powell, Poulson and Bean encouraged
and instructed us in regard to our duties and
the course we should pursue. Bore testimony
to the noble purpose for which the societies
were organized, and the great good they had
done; commended their labors, energy and
faithfulness.
	Bro. Marsing encouraged the societies to go
forward in their labors, train their children in
the principles of the Gospel; explained some
good points in Apostle Grant's vision.
	Bishop Joseph S. Horne felt highly pleased
with what he had heard; could see a great im-
provement; we had no time to idle. Discour-
aged back-biting—by indulging in it we do
wrong to ourselves and others.
	Sister Bean made a few closing and interest-
ing remarks. Conference adjourned until the
12th of June; 1884. Singing, Benediction by
Joseph S. Horne.
					ANNIE L. BEAN,
						Asst. Secretary.

GENTILE VALLEY.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We were organized as a Relief Society of
Mormon Ward on September 3rd, 1881, Sister
Sarah S. Richards, President; Ann Hale and
Anna Folkmann, Counselors. Since that time
we have done our best offering our mite wher-
ever it was most needed; we have donated to
the Logan Temple, also to the Deseret Hospi-
tal. We have some wheat on hand, and the
sisters all have a good spirit and try to go to
meeting as often as possible, though the people
are so scattered, and the men at present are so
busy with their summer's work, it is impossible
for the sisters to attend regularly. Apostle Mo-
ses Thatcher, Bro. Wm. Hendricks and Bro.
Parkinson held two meetings here last week,
giving the people some very good advice; they
appointed Bro. John B. Thatcher, Bishop, Alma
Hale and Arthur D. Young, Counselors, our
former Bishop, Solomon H. Hale being promo-
ted to the office of First Counselor to the Pres-
ident of the Idaho Stake.
	Our crops are looking well, and although
times are unusually dull, and money scarce, yet
we anticipate more prosperous times in the fu-
ture, and hope for the blessings that seem to
follow the saints generally.
	I will close, wishing you every success.
				MELLIE PECK, Secretary.

MONTPELIER.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The fifth anniversary of the organization of
our Primary Association took place to-day,

[Column 3]
May 31st, 1884, in the following order: Meet-
ing of the Association at 10 a.m., with a large
attendance of children, and a goodly number
of the parents, also the ward authorities.
	Meeting called to order by President Ann
Bunney, singing by the children: prayer by
Counselors David Osborne, singing; the Secre-
tary then read the report of the association for
the year ending May 21st, 1884, which was
very creditable, showing the number of meet-
ings, number of members enrolled, average at-
tendance, etc.; the time was then occupied for
one hour with recitations, songs, dialogues, etc.;
when Counselor F. M. Winters, D. Osborne,
and C. H. Bridges each occupied a short time
in making such remarks as were calculated to
encourage the members of the association.
	President Bunney then announced to the
children that we would have a half hour re-
cess for the purpose of arranging a bounteous
picnic.
	At 2 p.m. the benches were carried out and
the little folks were permitted to participate in
the merry dance, which was a great source of
enjoyment to them. After dancing a couple of
hours President Bunney and her counselors, as-
sisted by others of the sisters, treated the
children to candy, cakes and nuts, which the
children of course enjoyed.
	At 6 p.m. our amusements were brought to
a close, when all went home rejoicing.
	Our association numbers 132 enrolled mem-
bers, and the attendance was on this occasion
136. We have excellent meetings, and our
President and counselors are doing all in thier
power to advance our association in the scale
of intelligence.
				MARY M. OSBORNE,
							Secretary.

INVERARY.

	Our Primary Association here in this small
settlement numbers 115 members, and there is
generally a full attendance. We had a very
enjoyable time on the 7th of June. In the
forenoon there were speeches, recitations and
songs; all acquitted themselves well. The fixed
attention of both people and children during
all the exercises was pleasing.
	In the afternoon the little feet kept time in
the merry dance. Perfect order prevailed, and
all went home satisfied that the rising genera-
tion of Inverary would be an honor to Zion.
		EMMA E. JENSON, President,
		FLORA A. RUSSELL,
		ANNIE HYATT,		Counselors.

CEDAR BUTTE, ONEIDA COUNTY, IDAHO.

DEAR EDITOR:

	Owing to our isolated situation we see but
few communications from this part of the
country in your valuable paper, hence the lib-
erty I take in penning a few lines to you, hop-
ing you will be kind enough to give them space
in your paper.
	We are near 250 miles north of Salt Lake
City, and notwithstanding the visits of the
Apostles are few and far between, the Latter-
day Saints here are doing as well as in many
of the older settlements, where they have
much better chances. On the 2nd of April
the sisters of this place met and completed the
organization of our Relief Society, with Sister
Mary A. Green. President; Jeanette Pool and
Amina A. Stephens, Counselors; Mary A.
Shippeu, Secretary; and Harriet Bybee, Assis-
tant Secretary. Our Sunday Schools are well
attended and ably managed, the Improvement
Associations are up to the times.
	On the evening of the 21st the young folks
had a grand jollification under the auspices of
the Y. L. M. I. A. of this place, consisting of
dialogues, recitations, songs, duets, trios, quar-
tettes, etc.; the little ones performed their parts
splendidly and in a manner which did credit


to the management and showed great care in
training. It would be useless to attempt to tell
all that took place and describe how all per-
formed and who did best, for all did excellent-
ly; it might not be out of the way to make men-
tion of the song, "I'll be a little Mormon,"
sung by Miss Sadie Stephens, aged nine years,
and accompanied by her brother, Wm. W.,
aged thirteen, with the violin. The piece was
rendered in splendid style and elicited great
applause. Rhoda Bybee gave a recitation
which produced much merriment, as it was
rendered in a style entirely peculiar to herself.
"Kind words can never die" was rendered by
seven little girls aged from six to eleven years,
and their sweet voices blending with the notes
of the organ produced strains that were truly
akin to heaven.
	The evening passed agreeably, pleasantly,
and profitably, and all went to their homes
wiser if not better. Wishing the EXPONENT
every success,
	I remain, your sister in the Gospel,
				EVA OLSEN, Secretary.

FIRST WARD, SALT LAKE CITY.

	The young ladies of the First Ward held
their yearly meeting on Wednesday, April
16th, 1884. Some very interesting remarks
were made by Sisters Zina Young, Elmina Tay-
lor, Mary Freeze, Elizabeth Howard, Sarah
Graham, Margaret Steel and Annie Brown.
About forty sisters met at the house of Presi-
dent Graham and partook of a bounteous re-
past, preparatory to starting to meeting.
				FLORA MUSSER, Secretary.

FIRST WARD, PROVO.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Y. L. M. I. A. of the First Ward of
Provo is in a flourishing condition. There are
seventy names enrolled as members. Average
attendance between thirty and forty. We have
a paper called the Diadem once in two weeks,
which the young ladies write pieces for. Our
President, Pauline Hardy, is always punctual
at her meetings, and tries to make them inter-
esting as well as instructive; in fact all the
young ladies are up and awake to their duties,
and bear faithful testimonies to this great work
in which we, as children of God, are engaged.
	In financial affairs we are not behind the
other associations; we have donated $50 to the
Stake Tabernacle, also some to the Salt Lake
Temple and Deseret Hospital, and are now go-
ing to help build the Brigham Young Acad-
emy by donation. The EXPONENT comes regu-
larly to hand, and we are always happy to re-
ceive it.
	Ever praying for the success of the EXPON-
ENT and the best interests of Zion, I remain,
		Your sister in the Gospel,
			MARY STEWART, Secretary.


24	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SISTER EVELINE MATTHEWS
SHORT.

	Sister Eveline Matthews Short, beloved wife of Au-
gustus Short, died May 17th, 1884, after giving birth to
a daughter which was still-born. Sister Short was
born about the year 1852, somewhere between Utah and
California. She belonged to one of the tribes west of
Utah, as her parents were Lamanites. She was stolen
from her parents by the Walker Utes, when about three
years old and taken into the southern part of Utah,
where she was adopted into the family of Bishop Grover,
of Kanarrah, and received a good education. She was a
faithful and true Latter-day Saint, and died with the as-
surance of a glorious resurrection with the redeemed of
the Lord.
	The funeral services were held at the North Ogden
meeting house, on the 19th of May, 1884, in the presence
of a large number of friends who had come to pay the
last tribute of respect to their departed sister. The ser-
vices were conducted under the direction of Elder Robt.
G. Berrett. After the usual opening exercises Elde


Amos Maycock delivered an appropriate address. The
speaker rejoiced in the Gospel which placed all on an
equality. There is no distinction made and no such
thing as a special privileged class in the Gospel of Jesus
Christ. Elder Maycock further referred to circumstances
surrounding the early life of our departed sister, who
was a descendant of the Lamanites, and spoke at length
upon the promises that were contained in the Book of
Mormon concerning the remnant of people who once
inhabited this land, and of the important part they are
to take in this great work when a nation will be born
in a day. They will not waver in their faith as we do.
	Elder R. G. Berrett had only a short acquaintance with
Sister Short, but he was satisfied she was a good, true
and faithful Latter-day Saint. No one could have more
joy and satisfaction in the Gospel. She did not covet
the wealth of the world; her whole soul was wrapped
up in her religion, her greatest desire being to know
whether the Book of Mormon was a true record. In
answer to prayer and fasting the Lord gave her a testi-
mony of its truth. Her wish was to raise a family, but
in the providence of the Lord she has been called away
in the bloom of womanhood.

	Many more good things were said suggestive of the
regard in which our dead sister was held, and also
words of condolence were spoken to the husband and
relatives. The cortege was then formed and the re-
mains of the departed followed to their last resting-
place, where the coffin, which was very tastefully decor-
ated with wreathes of flowers, was carefully deposited.
—OGDEN HERALD.

RESOLUTIONS.

	The following Resolutions of Respect to our beloved
sister were unanimously adopted and sustained by the
Young Ladies' Mutual Improvement Association of Vir-
gen City:
	Whereas, God, in His infinite wisdom, has called from
our midst our sister, who was greatly beloved and res-
pected by us all for her kind and gentle disposition, her
pure, virtuous and exemplary life, faithful labors in our
Society, and the many good qualities she possessed,
	Therefore, Be it Resolved, That we, the members of
the Y. L. M. I. A. of Virgen City, do deeply mourn the
loss of our beloved sister, Eveline Short, and that we
condole with her husband and friends in their great loss;
but while we mingle our tears with theirs, we would
point with joy to the hope of a glorious resurrection
which all true Latter-day Saints possess.
	And, Be it Resolved, That we present a copy of these
Resolutions to her husband.
					ELEANOR OWENS President,
					BETSY A. PARKER,
					AGNES RICHARDS, Counselors.

﻿SPENCER HOUSE.

	We are so often spoken to in reference to a
good house to stop at by strangers visiting our
city, as also by our people from the settlements,
that we take pleasure in recommending the
"Spencer House" as thoroughly respectable,
clean and pleasant. The table has long borne
the reputation of not being excelled; the
grounds are the largest of any hotel grounds in
the city, which, with its extensive lawns and
abundant shade trees, make it pre-eminently a
most inviting resort for tourists—prices are
reasonable.
	This house is situated on Post Office Block,
1st East, between 2nd and 3rd South, Nos. 251
and 252.
