WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 13.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 1, 1885	NO. 17.


BRIDAL TRIBUTE. 

RESPECTFULLY INSCRIBED TO——THEY KNOW
WHOM.

We wish you all the happiness
	That in a wedded life can be.
Obtained through constant faithfulness
	For time and all eternity.

Within a Temple's sacred shrine,
	When knelt beside an altar there,
By rite performed as God enjoins,
	You two became a wedded pair. 

Commenced aright:—now step by step
	The upward leading track pursue;
Should obstacles arise, look up
	And keep the royal end in view. 

Let God be first in all your thoughts—
	To do His will your highest aim; 
And if obedient to His laws,
	His special blessings you may claim.

New obligations wait you now,
	As wedded souls two joined as one;
Your separate int'rests all combine,
	Though in two lives your duties run.

To form connubial happiness,
	Let love commingle with respect;
And undissembling honesty
	A mutual confidence protect.

And now our blessing we extend
	In wishing you the rich behest
That noble deeds and efforts bring,
	And say, "In blessing be you blest."

	E. R. SNOW SMITH.
Dec. 20th, 1884.


LINES ON THE DEATH OF ELDER
ENOS LIONEL STOOKEY.

COMPOSED ON THE EVENING OF HIS BURIAL, AND
RESPECTFULLY DEDICATED TO HIS MOTHER.

In the spring the flowers will bloom again,
	The air be laden with perfume sweet
That the south winds bring of the bursting buds
	That are springing to life 'neath the zephyrs' feet;
The sun will smile on the ice-bound streams
	And send them dancing along their way;
And wonderful treasures of fragrant life
	Come out with the light of each opening day.

<i:'oome out="" with="" the="" light="" of="" each'opening="" day.="" batalaa!="" my="" flower="" that="" t="" loved="" and="" watchd="" "‘tlint="" grew="" to="" d="" prond,="" o’erstiadowlng="" tree,="" fs'jead="" by-="" touch="" spoiler's="" hand—'="" spring="" cannot="" bring="" backtts="" life="" te="" mel:;="" .="" v="" i="" bad="" thought="" hide="" ’neath="" its="" branches="" strong="" cta.="" vifhen="" withering="" heat="" day="" was="" high,="" rest="" in="" bcantlfnl="" shelter="" long,="" as="" bun="" crepf="" down="" way.="" but="" it="" fell,="" untouched="" by="" a="" slow="" decay—="" sweet="" strength="" crashed="" single="" hour,="" 'whilf="" stately="" beauty="" fair="" see,="" 6eemed,frill="" nnirieasnred="" power;="" fre="" who="" trained="" itb="" growth="" j'="" 8tand'here.in="" storm="" onr="" hearts'="" must="" .brave="" -and="" seo="" nothing="" above-="" bat="" btormy="" sky,’="" nothirig="" below="" but-this="" precious="" grave.="" '="" -="" ‘ifd'.l="" lionel—my="" boy—the="" hopes="" are="" hot="" vain—="" arise="" from="" thy="" tear-stained="" bed—="" purer="" an="" endless="" spring,="" ’="" 1="" (="" now="" only="" remember—iriy="" boyll="" dead.,="" v‘="" sirlve="" look="" np="" “father's="" face="" f.="" andread="" frownlngs="" blessing="" hld.,1="" brit,’ah="" cari="" be="" given1="" again="" ,r="" (j="" -like="" lost="" one="" rindcr="" coflln="" lid?="" 0gp,="" make="" us="" patient,wo="" two="" walk'="" l’"with="" weary="" feet="" toward="" gathering="" nighty="" <="" div="">But, alas! my flower that I loved and watched
	Till it grew to a proud, o'ershadowing tree,
Is dead by the touch of the spoiler's hand—
	Spring cannot bring back its life to me.
I had thought to hide 'neath its branches strong
	When the withering heat of my day was high,
And to rest in its beautiful shelter long,
	As the sun of my life crept down the way.

But it fell, untouched by a slow decay—
	Its sweet strength crushed in a single hour,
While its stately beauty was fair to see,
	And its life seemed full of unmeasured power;
And we who trained it and loved its growth
	Stand here in the storm our hearts must brave
And see nothing above but a stormy sky,
	And nothing below but this precious grave.

Lionel—my boy—the hopes are not vain—
	The hopes that arise from thy tear-stained bed—
Of a purer life in an endless spring,
	But now I only remember—my boy is dead.
I strive to look up in our Father's face
	And read in its frownings a blessing hid.
But ah! can a blessing be given again
	Like that lost one under the coffin lid!

O, God make us patient, we two who walk
	With weary feet toward the gathering night,


With this strange, deep grief like a shadowy cloud
	Shutting away all of earth's sweet light.
We would ask no greater boon than this
	Of the Father who pitied our anguish deep,
Than to walk in the path His life has marked,
	Then lie down at His side and forget to weep.

		LAUREL B. 
Sunnyside, Dec. 21, 1884.


[Column 1]

AUTOBIOGRAPHY

OF EMILY D. P. YOUNG. 

Continued

	"The same night the prisoners, Gilbert, Mor-
ley and Corrill, were liberated from jail that
they might confer with their brethren and try
to negotiate some measure for peace, and on
their return to jail about two o'clock Tuesday
morning in the custody of the sheriff an armed
force of six or seven men stood near the jail
and hailed them; they were answered by the
sheriff, who gave his name and the names of
his prisoners, crying, "Don't fire, don't fire; the
prisoners are in my charge," etc. They, how-
ever, fired one or two guns, when Morley and
Corrill retreated, but Gilbert stood, with sever-
al guns presented at him, firmly held by the
sheriff. Two, more desperate than the rest, at-
tempted to shoot, but one of their guns flashed
and the other missed fire. Gilbert was then
knocked down by Thomas Wilson, a grocer in
the village. About this time a few of the in-
habitants arrived and Gilbert entered jail, from
which he, with three of his brethren, were lib-
erated about sunrise, without further prosecu-
tion of the trial.
	"On the morning of the 5th of Nov. the vil-
lage began to be crowded with individuals
from different parts of the county with guns, etc.
And report said the militia had been called out
under the sanction or instigation of Lieut. Gov.
Boggs; and that one Col. Pitcher had the com-
mand. Among this militia (so called) were
embodied the most conspicuous characters of
the mob; and it may truly be said that the ap-
pearance of the ranks of the body was well
calculated to excite suspicion of their horrible
designs. Very early on the same morning
several branches of the Church received intelli-
gence that a number of their brethren were in
prison and the determination of the mob was
to kill them; and that the branch of the church
near the village of Independence was in immi-
nent danger, as the main body of the mob was
gathered at that place. In this critical situa-
tion about one hundred of the Saints from dif-
ferent branches volunteered for the protection
of their brethren near Independence and pro-
ceeded on the road toward Independence and
halted about a mile west of the village, where
they awaited further information concerning
the movements of the mob.
	"They soon learned that the prisoners were
not massacred, and that the mob had not fallen
upon the branch of the church near Indepen-
dence as was expected. They were also in-
formed that the militia had been called out for
their protection, but in this they placed but lit-
tle confidence, for the body congregated had
every appearance of a county mob; which sub-
sequent events fully verified in a majority of
said body. On application to Col. Pitcher it
was found that there was no alternative but for
the church to leave the county forthwith, and
to deliver into his hands certain men to be

[Column 2]

tried for murder, said to have been committed
by them in the battle the evening before. The
arms of the Saints were also demanded by Col.
Pitcher. Among the committee appointed to
receive the arms of the church were several of
the most unrelenting of the July committee,
who had directed in the demolishing of the
printing office, and the personal injuries of that
day; namely, Henry Chiles, Abner Staples and
Lewis Franklin, who have not ceased to pursue
the saints from the first to the last with feelings
of the most hostile kind. These unexpected
requisitions of the Col. made him appear like
one standing at the head of civil and military
law, taking a stretch beyond the constitutional
limits of our Republic.
	"Rather than have submitted to these unrea-
sonable requirements the saints would have
shed their blood in the defense of their rights,
the liberties of their country and their wives
and children; but the fear of violating law in
resisting this pretended militia, and the flatter-
ing assurances of protection and honorable
usage promised by Lieut. Gov. Boggs, in whom
they had reposed confidence up to this period.
induced them to submit, believing that he did
not tolerate so gross a violation of all law as
has been practiced in Jackson Co. But the
great change that may appear to some in the
views, designs and craft of this man, to rob an
innocent people of their arms by stratagem,and
leave more than one thousand defenceless men,
women and children to be driven from their
homes among strangers in a strange land of, to
appearances, barbarians, to seek shelter from
the stormy blasts of winter's cold embrace, is
so glaringly exposed in the sequel that all
earth and hell cannot deny that a baser knave,
a greater traitor, and a more wholesale butch-
er or murderer of mankind never went untried,
etc. 
	"The conduct of Col. Lucas and Pitcher had
long proven them to be open and avowed ene-
mies. Both of these men had their names at-
tached to the mob circular as early as July,
the object of which was to drive the saints
from Jackson Co. With assurances from the
Lieut. Gov. and others that the object was to
disarm the combatants on both sides, and that
peace would be the result, the brethren surren-
dered their arms to the number of fifty or up-
wards, and the men present who were accused
of being in the battle the evening before gave
themselves up for trial. After detaining them
one day and night on a pretended trial for
murder, in which time they were threatened,
brickbatted, etc. Col. Pitcher, after receiving
a watch from one of the prisoners to satisfy
costs, etc., took them into a cornfield and said
to them, "clear."
	"After the surrender of their arms, which
were used only in self-defense; the neighboring
tribes of Indians in times of war, let loose
upon the women and children could not have
appeared more hideous and terrific than did
the companies of ruffians who went in various
directions, well armed, on foot and on horse-
back, bursting into houses without fear, know-
ing their arms were secured, frightening dis-
tracted women with what they would do to
their husbands if they could catch them, warn-
ing women and children to flee immediately or
they would tear their houses down over their
heads and massacre them before night. At the
head of one of these companies appeared the
Rev. Isaac Mc.Coy, with a gun upon his shoul-
der, ordering the saints to leave the county


forthwith and surrender what arms they had.
Other pretended preachers of the Gospel took
a conspicuous part in the persecution, calling
the Mormons the common enemy of mankind,
and exulting in their afflictions.
	"On the 6th of Nov. women and children 
fled in every direction from the merciless mob.
One party of about one hundred and fifty
women and children fled to the prairie, where
they wandered for several days, under the
broad canopy of heaven, with about six men
to protect them. Other parties fled to the
Missouri River, and to lodgings for the night
where they could find it. One Mr. Bennett
opened his house for a night's shelter to a wan-
dering company of distressed women and chil-
dren who were fleeing to the river. During
this dispersion of the women and children, par-
ties of the mob were hunting the men, firing
upon some, tying up and whipping others, and
some they pursued upon horses several miles."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

THE GRANGER FAMILY.

FROM THE "PHELPS CITIZEN"

	We are indebted to our former townsman,
Gamaliel Case, of Sodus, for the following cor-
respondence. The lady, Mrs. Sarah M. Kim-
ball, was the daughter of Oliver Granger and
grand daughter of Pierce Granger, both of
whom were born in Phelps:—Ed.
	LOGAN, Cache Co., Utah, Aug. 24, 1884.
MR. GAMALIEL CASE—DEAR COUSIN:
	I realize that this will be a strange message
to you, from a strange messenger.
	I learned by letter from our departed cousin,
Mrs. Pomeroy, that you knew of my visit to
Phelps and Newark last year.
	Uncle Elihu of Michigan told me of your
being in Sodus. I much desired to see you, but
all seemed so strange that I failed to accom-
plish it.
	One important object that I had in visiting
my native state, was to gather records of my
kindred dead. I could better explain to you
in person than on paper why I wish these re-
cords. I hardly hope to be able to make you
comprehend the importance this matter has for
me. You must know something of the pecul-
iar people with whom I have chosen to cast my
lot. One of our peculiarities is, we build Tem-
ples to the name of the God of heaven and
earth. They are built at great expense and
dedicated to the Most High. We enter there-
in and attend to ordinances most sacred, for
ourselves and such as we consider our worthy
dead. For instance, last Tuesday I enjoyed
the to me blessed privilege of entering the
Logan Temple font and being baptized by one
having authority, for your mother, my great
aunt, Sally Granger Case. Wednesday I re-
ceived endowments and blessings in the Tem-
ple for and in her behalf.
	At a future time I hope to officiate in seal-
ings and further ordinances for her. The seal-
ing ordinance is the eternal order of marriage,
or marriage for eternity.
	To complete the record of my work I want
you to send me the date and place of your
mother's birth and death. We are so peculiar
as to believe that what is properly done and
recorded in the Temples of our God will be re-
corded in heaven, and these are some of the
books from which we shall be judged.
	However absurd this may appear to you,
please gratify me by sending such names and
dates of your branch of the Granger family,
who are dead, as you can gather.
	I learned in Phelps that you had a pamph-
let containing an account of the early settlers
of that town. If you would be kind enough
to send it to me I will return it if you wish.
	If there is any one interested in me, my re-
gards to them.

[Column 2]

	I write at a venture not knowing whether it
will reach you. Logan is 100 miles north of
my home. I expect to remain here only one
week more for the present. This is the nearest
Temple to my home that is finished. Six years
ago I went to St. George Temple 300 miles
south and worked for my dead four months.
	I shall feel anxious to know whether you re-
ceive this so please drop me a line at once.
	Very respectfully your cousin,
		S. M. KIMBALL.

REPLY
MRS. SARAH M. KIMBALL—DEAR COUSIN:
	Yours of the 24th of August last, came to
my home while I was on a visit to Old Ply-
mouth Bay, where the Pilgrim Fathers first
landed, where I remained some time with rela-
tives. After my return home your letter was
handed me, and indeed it was a strange message
and from a strange messenger.
	I did not write you immediately, for the
reason I had not quite all the dates you re-
quested. You ask to know of the honored
dead of the Granger family, of their birth,
death, etc. I cheerfully comply so far as I have
it, for indeed we sprung from a noble ancestry.
I copy the following from Elisha Granger's old
Bible as it lies before me in his own hand writ-
ing:

	Births of the children of Elisha Granger by Sarah
his wife.
	Elisha, born Feb. 23, 1768.
	Pierce, born Aug. 23, 1769.
	Elihu, born April 30, 1771.
	Sarah, born March 26, 1773.
	Eliphaz, born Feb. 9, 1775.
	Sarah my wife departed this life Feb. 15, 1775.

	Next, the death of my grandfather's second
wife, but I have no account of the death of his
third wife. And then follows his own death
who was your own great grandfather, as taken
from the tombstones in the old burial ground
in Vienna (Phelps), that he bought and gave
to the Methodists for a church lot and burial
ground and the Methodist church stands on
the same site to-day:

In Memory of
Hannah
the wife of Elisha Granger
 Who departed this life
June 26, 1807, in the
73d year of her age.

In Memory of 
Mr. Elisha Granger
who died April 12, 1821,
aged 77 years, 9 months and 9 days.

	Next I give the epitaph of your own dear
grandmother:

Sacred to the Memory of
Mrs. Clarisa Granger
the wife of Pierce Granger, Esq.,
who died Aug., 1813, in the
40th year of her age.

	Next, that of your Aunt Apamy, a noble
Christian woman:

Apamy,
wife of Elihu Granger
who died July 21, 1853
aged 8l years.

	This brings me to notice the death of your
Aunt Sarah Granger Case, as you call her, my
own dear departed mother. She died at my
house, the old homestead, Oct. 6, 1843, aged
70 years and six months. She was named
after my noble grandmother and you bear not
only the name of my mother but of that noble
grandmother that gave birth to our Granger
family, but died as you see by the above rec-
ord, leaving her young family and a babe six
days old.
	We turn next to our noble Christian grand-
father, your great grandfather, and note what

[Column 3]

he done with all those motherless children. In
a proper time he looked out a mother for
them, and did not let the animal part of man
predominate by running after some young girl,
but procured a mother that was ten years older
than himself to help bring up his children.
This was a noble act on his part. He was con-
verted under Jesse Lee, of Conn., and Lee
gave him license to preach. I have often seen
the license in his old family Bible. They
brought up their family in a Christian way
under all the disadvantages of a new country,
he being with his three oldest boys, the second
settler in the town of Phelps. That first sum-
mer in one of the settlements a person died and
our grandfather preached the funeral sermon,
the first one ever preached in Phelps, which
was in 1789. But in the fall they went back
to Connecticut, and did not move on with
their families until the next year.
	Our grandfather bought 1000 acres in
Phelps and 1000 acres in Sodus and placed
his children on his lands, but this was not
enough for him to do. He and his wife would
get up meetings in private houses, and camp
meetings, for at that time there were no
churches, and in answer to the prayers of their
father and mother, their children were all con-
verted, and your grandfather, my uncle Pierce,
who was also a local preacher in the Methodist
church, besides he was a Justice of the Peace,
and no person of his time was more favorably
spoken of, even to this day by all that knew
him.
	I do not find any record of my much loved
uncle Pierce Granger, he died in the west and
I hope you may be able to find it. I feel under
great obligation to my beloved grandfather and
my uncle Pierce and I regard them our worthy
dead. Have you any Temple font and endow-
ment blessing that could be secured in their
behalf as well? They were both married here,
one having three wives and the other two, but
not but one at a time.
	Of course, I am not aware of any celestial
marriage, or whether there is any for men, if
they are equally good. But so far as I have
been permitted in my limited experience to ob-
serve, I have noticed that those who took to
themselves more wives than one were not con-
sidered to be the best type of men; this holds
equally true among the ministry in the differ-
ent evangelical denominations. But you may
have become acquainted with a better class of
ministers among your "peculiar people," who
are trying to obey the teaching that a Bishop
should be the husband of one wife, etc.
	I have not the pamphlet of which you speak.
My grandfather's family were all born in old
Simsbury, Conn. If any further information
is desired please ask, and if you ever come this
way again I will be glad to see you,
	Yours, etc.
		GAMALIEL CASE.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	"Those interested in fair play for woman
ought to give the best attention for a few mo-
ments to Mr. King, the new president of the
Erie Railway. In very properly reducing the
enormous expenses of that corporation he cut
down all salaries, or nearly all;but in doing so,
instead of making the scale of reduction uni-
form in the same line of service, he reduced
the salaries of women employees 30 per cent.,
while those of men doing corresponding duty
and receiving about the same rates of pay were
reduced only 15 per cent. It would be inter-
esting to know what Mrs. King—if there be a
Mrs. King—thinks of this sort of discrimina-
tion against the sex to which she and the
mother of her husband belong."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WELCOME HOME!

ADDRESSED TO BROTHER BEN. BENNETT,
BY HIS WIFE.

Welcome, oh, welcome, to home and to me,
And thy children’s fond hearts have a welcome for
	thee!
Thy friends and thy neighbors will gather around,
And from brothers and sisters the welcome resound;
Thy mother will meet thee with welcome most kind
And from nephews and nieces a welcome thou'lt find.
My heart has been lonely while waiting to hear
The signal to tell me thy coming was near,
And, oh, how it beat when I saw by the press
That thou wert returning thy children to bless;
With thy precepts to guide them, thy fond love to
	cheer,
Thy presence to shield them from terror,or fear;
They will tell how they missed you when prayer time
	came 'round,
And they yearned for a look, or a smile, or a sound
Of thy voice to encourage, to soothe, or restrain.
To share their young joys and their faith to sustain;
Even baby has seemed for her father to mourn,
And daily looked forward to papa's return—
She was "going to Salt Lake to meet him," she said—
Twas her first thought at morn and her last going to
	bed;
And now she will see him—I scarce can believe
That thou art returning my cares to relieve;
To share in our pleasures, to chase our weak fears,
To brighten our smiles, and to dry up our tears,
To pray with us, talk with us, tell where thou hast
	been,
And give us an outline of what thou hast seen.
In the long winter evenings how good it will be
To feel I have round me my children and thee:
To see thy kind smile, feel the press of thy hand,
See thee mingle again with thy little home band
When their voices in praise to their Father on high
Are lifted in thanks that their papa is nigh,
And they ask to be granted this one prayer more,
That father’s long absence for some time is o'er,
And. across the broad ocean no more he may roam,
But his mission in future be nearer to home.


[Column 1]

SCENES AND INCIDENTS AT
WINTER QUARTERS.

BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.

(Continued.)

	The next inportant event was the arrival
of Almon W. Babbet and wife from Nauvoo,
This was on the evening of the 6th. He brought
considerable news; said the mob had complete
possession of the place, the Temple and all.
They had several mock ceremonies with differ-
ent individuals—had baptized Daniel Davis
three times. He saw Bishop Whitney, who
barely escaped, and was obliged to leave Nau-
voo secretly and speedily for St. Louis. He
stated that the mob had plundered numbers of
buildings, among which was the house of Bro.
Joseph Heywood, who was stripped of nearly
everything he possessed. They had defaced
the Temple considerably, both inside and out.
The shore of the city and all the approaches to
the city were strictly guarded to prevent the
ingress of "Mormons," and when any man was
found they immediately baptized him and sent
him over into Iowa. Mr. Babbet was advised
by the council, which had met to arrange some
difficulty between some of the brethren, to re-
main with us, but he seemed not very anxious
to do it.
	On the morning of the 9th he and wife
started back. Little John Smith, who had
come from Nauvoo with my father's family,
went with them to meet his mother, who was
about 150 miles back, near Mount Pisgah.
My husband mentions the death of Miss Eliza
A. Peirson, a niece of Dr. Richards, at his tent,
on the evening of the 10th.
	He says:  "She had left father and mother
far behind to join the Saints of God, in the
wilderness, to suffer and die among them for

[Column 2]

the Gospel's sake. She was a worthy, exem-
plary young woman, and the remembrance of
the many happy hours I have spent in her so-
ciety, together with her sisters and friends, will
never be obliterated while time shall last. She
was from Richmond, Massachusetts, where I
became acquainted with her during my sojourn
in the Eastern States."
	Under the same date is written the following:
"I. Redding returned from the Pawnee village
this evening, whither he had been by appoint-
ment to assist some families to remove back
here, whom Bishop Miller had left sick and
destitute." Bishop Miller had now become a
confirmed apostate. He had drawn away a
few weak and disaffected persons and some,
who, like himself, were corrupt at heart. They
were afterwards distinguished as Millerites.
Many of those who had traveled in his camp
returned to head quarters.
	On the 13th Horace speaks of a large camp
of Omahaws making considerable noise and
tumult in the village below, having received
their payment from the United States. "The
amount of money paid to them is $50,000,
which was brought up by land accompanied by
a guard of twelve men.
It is said that the Indians are killing our cat-
tle in great numbers below here, and they have
been here with beef to sell; no doubt the relics
of some of our cattle."
	On the evening of the 14th my uncle, Wm.
Murray, Bro. Harvey Green and Mr. Hovey
arrived from Nauvoo with their families, some
of them being very sick. They had been
three weeks on the road. They brought addi-
tional news, and according to their statements
the mob was pretty busy plundering houses,
ripping open feather beds and scattering the
contents in the street, knocking the horns from
the oxen on which the font was built, and run-
ning about the streets imitating the blowing of
horns with them and doing other acts of sac-
rilege. They had also torn down the altars
and pulpits in the Temple, and converted the
edifice into a meat market. The same spirit is
rampant to-day, and threats of a similar
character have been made by our enemies for
years past, but have been restrained by Him
who has said: "Thus far shalt thou go and no
further."
	But to return: On the 15th Horace wrote,
"Bro. W. Woodruff was brought into the
camp this afternoon from the woods severely
injured by the fall of a tree. Prest. Young,
Bro. Kimball, O. Pratt, Dr. Richards and
Joseph Young laid hands on him, after which
he appeared much relieved." He speaks of
Bro. Pond and family arriving on the 16th,
and all being quite sick with the ague. Con-
cerning the Omahaws he says, "They are lo-
cated about ten miles below here, where they
intend to remain this winter. They have had
for some time in contemplation a grand buf-
falo hunt, which they have abandoned in ex-
pectation of living and sustaining themselves
by the killing of our cattle instead, which they
are frequently detected in driving off."
	Sunday, the 18th, the weathar being toler-
able meeting was held at the stand. "The sub-
ject of our molestation by the Indians was
taken into consideration. It was thought to
be necessary to build our houses in a more
compact body and form our wagons in a circle,
that we might be better able to defend our-
selves against their encroachments. This was
the advice given by the interpreter who at-
tended meeting with some Indians at the stand."
It was further deemed proper to send out dif-
ferent companies in different directions after
the cattle. The companies were organized,
each consisting of twenty-one men. Brother
Winslow Farr, Z. Pulsipher and J. Redding
were appointed to go on the same business, as
captains of companies.

[Column 3]

	On Tuesday morning a general turnout was
made to go and drive in the cattle, in conform-
ity with the arrangements made at the meet-
ing. They spent a number of days hunting
them, and all that were found were put into
Father Cutler's yard, which was appointed as
the place of rendezvous, where the brethren
might come and each select his own cattle. In
the afternoon of the same day, the men who
were left in camp were summoned to go down
on the bottom and put out a fire, which was
raging greatly, and had already burnt a num-
ber of hay stacks. "But," Horace says "not-
withstanding all our efforts, we could not en-
tirely subdue it.	*	*	Brother
Kimball went and burnt round our stack in
order to sa ve it. The brethren at last suc-
ceeded in turning the fire in such a direction
that it should not do much injury."


A GENTILE DR.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I think it is high time that I express my
thanks for your very good little paper, the Ex-
ponent, which I have received since I had the
pleasure of meeting Mrs. Richards and her son
at Washington.
	I have often thought I would pen a few
words from this way down East; whether it will
encourage or discourage is not for me to say.
I am often called upon to express an opinion
in regard to the condition of the Mormons, as
it is well known that I have been with them a
short time, and have frequently expressed an
opinion, especially in regard to polygamy as I
understand the same. As you probably are
aware that women are the greatest defamers of
women, such are those I meet and present to
them the better elements of the practice of
polygamy than the surroundings throughout
the world among all other so-called religionists.
The moral principles, to all intents and pur-
poses, seem to have passed away; there are few
exceptions. We find among the clergy, priests
and deacons of all sects, as well as the general-
ity of men and women, care only for self-grati-
fication, leaving their offspring to be murdered
or picked up by the police and sent to the
foundling asylums, and the mother (whom the
laws do not recognize) men have decoyed and
destroyed. These women, girls,are driven into
suicide, or a worse life while they live; the man
is free, legally irresponsible,while the girl is an
outcast.
	Bigamy is better than seduction. Those who
make laws are those who defile them; we need
laws enacted that will hold all classes, and all
sects, religionists and non-religionists. That
all men can have one wife; if they desire more
than one they must be equally responsible to
each and all, provide for them and the chil-
dren, and all equally recognized and legiti-
mate. This is the only plan to create morality
in the world.
		Dr. C. A. Von Cort.
New York.

SALT LAKE CITY, FEBRUARY 1, 1885.


[Column 1]

MARVELOUS TIMES.

	Who ran say that these are not marvelous
times and big with wonderful events? Every
day some startling development is made that
makes one conscious of the fact that these are
the times that try men's souls to the very cen-
tre. With all the accidents of the nineteenth
century nothiug is so effectually calculated to
prove a destructive agency for the wicked and
ungodly as the invention and manufacture of
dynamite. What may not be accomplished by
this terrible agent of destruction when manipu-
lated by cruel, designing and unscrupulous men
or women, though we instinctively shrink from
the acknowledgement, yet women have been
proven equally cruel with men when occasion
required urgent action. For instance, in the
French Revolution, and in the case of "Shake-
speare's Lady Macbeth."
	The recent explosion in London has opened
the eyes of many who sat in darkness on this
subject, and that was no doubt only an experi-
ment, and ere long the nations will awaken to
the coneciousness of the astounding fact that
when there was seeming security, they were
treading upon dangerous ground that might at
any moment explode and swallow them up.
Secret societies, combinations and organizations
are banding themselves together for certain pur-
poses, and it would seem the time is fast ap
proaching when every man's hand will be
against his neighbor, and he who will not take
up his sword must needs flee to Zion.
	The war in Egypt has carried off its thousands
and tens of thousands already, and still it is in
progress. The terrible avalanches in Italy have
carried terror and desolation through a large por-
tion of that beautiful country, and left thou-
sands homeless and destitute, as well as burying
an unnumbered multitude alive. In our own
land cyclones in the south have frightened
many and destroyed much property; and storms,
shipwrecks and accidents and the like by
sea and by land are of every-day occurrence.
	In the mean time, how is it with the Latter-
day Saints? They are being persecuted by their
enemies, wickedness waxeth strong in high
places and in the hearts of the rulers of the na-
tion, the evil one is on the alert, knowing that
his time is short. The servants of the Most
High are being persecuted and driven to ex-
tremities, and the very principles of heaven are
being used as a weapon against the Saints to
banishment and imprisonment. Men who have
laid their all upon the altar for Christ's sake,
men who have endured much persecution here-
tofore, are taken up on the slightest pretext, and
their liberties jeopardized, their families intimi-
dated if possible and every indignity heaped
upon them until it seems more than human na-
ture can bear,

[Column 2]

	Ths recent cases that have been before the
courts here have been treated in such a way that
it makes one feel there is no such thing as jus-
tice and equity to be obtained, but only a deter-
mination to push to the very extreme those who
by some unforeseen circumstance or incident
have fallen into the hands of their enemies, who
are like ravenous wolves ready to devour all that
comes in their way and are ever on the alert for
more. They seem to forget the slippery places
on which they themselves stand, and the glass
houses in which they live; and while their eyes
have enormous beams in them, they are probing
for the mote that is in their neighbor's.
	But swift destruction awaits all such and they
will be overtaken as a thief in the night, and
will cry for help and none will hear.
	The way in which the officials, Prosecuting
Attorney and his assistants, have questioned
young women in the examinations that have
been made before the Commissioners is a dis-
grace. The most indelicate and obnoxious ques-
tions are put to innocent, pure-minded young
girls. How much longer this sort of thing can
be endured it is impossible to say. At present a
vigorous effort is being made by those in power
here to afflict and annoy peaceable citizens.
Next week will be a busy one for these con-
temptible deputies,and every woman who values
her honor wllf no doubt do her best to protect
herself from being confronted by one of them.
On the slightest pretext they serve a subpoena
and no one knows then, what his or her fate
may be.
	The statement or decision, if it can be so
called, made in the case of President Angus M.
Cannon is the weakest article one could imag-
ine. The Commissioner does not base his as-
sertion upon the evidence at all, but upon sup-
position, which it seems to any one possessing
ordinary common sense to be contrary to all law;
all deductions drawn are, or should be, accord-
ing to evidence and not supposition. But as
this is more persecution than prosecution, it will
of course pass muster, even though men’s liber-
ties are at stake; they are "Mormons," and that
accounts for it—these civilizers, regenerators,
purifiers, who are so anxious the laws should be
enforced, care no more for purity of morals than
they do for a pure atmosphere to breathe in,the
fumes of tobacco and whiskey in and around
theFederal court room are positively nauseating.
One cannot help thinking of the remark of Rev.
Olympia Brown, who said the aristocracy of
power was that of the moustache, whose insig-
nia was the spittoon, whose home was the smok-
ing car, and whose outward sign was the curling
tobacco smoke, etc., and with all propriety one
might add the fumes of whiskey.


[Column 1]
	EDITORIAL NOTES.

	MRS JOHN T. CAINE and Mrs. Franklin S.
Richards attended the recent Convention of
the National Woman’s Suffrage Association in
Washington.
	THE Secretaries of the Relief Society, Y. L.
M I. A. and P. A. should bear in mind that the
semi-annual reports will soon be wanted now
for the Stake Conference.
	MRS. M. O. Kelting of California writes—
, 'Continue to sen[-] your dear little messenger to
my address. I find it like a little white-winged
comforter in my lonely moments and I pray that
[-]he angels of heaven and earth may continue to
bless and encourage the EXPONENT.”
	WE have had the pleasure of seeing a bust of
President Brigham Young, executed by one of
Utah’s young sculptors, Harry B[-]own of Logan.
It is life-like indeed. The peculiar expression
of the mouth and nose, and indeed of every fea

[Column 2]
ture; the very pose of the face is perfect, and the
smallest detail is perceptible. We congratulate
Bro. Brown on the wonderful success he has
made in developing his subject. It is an encour-
aging fact that Utah is making some considera-
ble progress in the direction of art cultivation.
	WE have received a sample copy of a new
magazine, entitled "Babyhood,”   edited by
Marion Harland, which is sufficient in itself to
recommend it. But as we have examined and
read it carefully we feel sure it is a work of mer
it and one that will assuredly recommend itself
to all who read it. It gives pleasant and happy
advice to mothers in reference to little ones and
makes timely and appropriate suggestions on
the topics connected with babyhood and mother-
hood.  Price:$1.50 per year, published in New
York City.
	THE news of the musical success of young
Edwards of Willard City, in competition
with so many professionals and compos-
ers from various parts of the world, is
quite startling. It must be a source of im 
mense satisfaction to the youthful author, and
It certainly should be considered an honor to all
who love Utah and are proud of her sons to
know that there is so much real musical talent
beginning to be developed, It proves the falsity
of the statements that Mormoniam chokes out
progress in every direction.
	WE have received from Washington the
“Brief and Argument” of George G. Vess,
Wayne Mac Veagh, Franklin S. Richards and
Charles W. Bennett for Appellant, Mary Ann
M. Pratt vs. Alexander Ramsey, A. S.  Paddock,
G. L Godfrey, A B. Carleton, J. R. Pettigrew,
E D. Hoge and John S. Lindsay, Appellees.
Supreme Court of the United States, October
Term, 1884. We have not yet had time to read
it carefully but from a casual perusal judge it to
be a very able and conclusive argument in favor
of the lady. It would be well for the sisters to
take pains to read up on theese subjects that are
of such importance to this people, that they
maybe thoroughly posted in matters pertaining
to the possession of the franchise, also property
rights, etc
	WE have also received in pamphlet form the
"Legislative Commission Scheme," the title of
the address of Hon. John T. Caine, delivered
in the People’s Mass Meeting, held in the Thea-
tre Nov. 3, 1884. It is a well-written and logi-
cal statement of facts, and eve y woman inter
ested in the condition of affairs should read it
carefully.
	A very pleasant ladies' tea party was given
at the Davereux House on Thursday, Jan 29,
by Mrs. Priscilla Jennings, who is a most enter-
taining and hospitable hostess. There were be-
tween thirty and forty la[--]es present, most of
them widows and missionaries’ wives. The
supper was very handsomely served, the table
being very beautifully decorated with fragrant
flowers. Mr and Mrs. Jennings as host and
hostess cannot be excelled. The afternoon and
evening were spent by the ladles in social con-
versation, and every one seemed to intensely
enjoy the visit. Davereux House is a beautiful
modern mansion, and with its large, and ele-
gant parlors, commodious dining apartments,
and handsome and complete library, conserva-
tory and other luxuries, possesses all the advan-
tages of a refined and luxurious home. We
heartily wish its inmates long life and health
to enjoy the blessings with which they are sur-
rounded.
	SISTER ZINA D. H. YOUNG'S birthday was
yesterady, Ja[-]. 31st. She was sixty-four years
of age. We congratulate her and wish her
many happy returns of the day and hope she
may enjoy life as fully and completely as she can
wish. Our readers are doubtless aware that she


has for some time past been officiating in the
Logan Temple. She expects shortly to take a
trip to Cascade Falls, Oregon, where her son,
Lieut. Willard Young, is at present stationed.
	SISTER ELIZA R. SNOW SMITH'S birthday
passed off very quietly. She received those who
called to congratulate her in her parlor at the
Lion House. The honored lady has now reached
the advanced age of 81 years. Her health is ex-
cellent, her faculties unimpaired; indeed her
memory seems as bright and perfect as ever,and
her mind as clear and active as it ever has been;
she still continues to write and works more
hours in the day than most young people. We
congratulate Sister Eliza on reaching her eighty-
first year and hope she may have many happy
returns of the day, with health and prosperity
and happiness.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


IMPORTANT DECISION.

THE JUDGMENT OF THE TERRITORIAL SUPREME
COURT AFFIRMED.

	WASHINGTON, January 19.—The Supreme
Court has rendered a decision in the
polygamy case of Rudger Clawson, ap-
pellant vs. the United States, an appeal
from the Supreme Court of Utah. Clawson
appellant, having been found guilty by a
jury of the crime of polygamy, and unlawful
cohabitation, was duly sentenced to pay a fine
aggregating $800 and imprisonment for four
years. He appealed to the Supreme Court of
the Territory, and pending judgment on such
appeal, applied to the Court that sentenced him
for release upon suitable b[-]nds. The judge de-
nied application, on the ground that the defend-
ant ought not to be admitted to bail after con-
viction and sentence, unless some extraordinary
reason had been given. Clawson thereupon
sued out a writ of habeas corpus in the Supreme
Court of the Territory, and prayed for release,
upon the ground that the Court below had un-
lawfully refused to admit him to bail. The Su-
preme Court overruled the petition and remand-
ed the prisoner to the custody of the Marshal,
whereupon he took the present appeal to this
court. This court holds that inasmuch as the
judgment of the Third Judicial Court, in which
the prisoner was tried, did not impose upon him
a fine only, but also imprisonment, his admission
to bail, pending appeal from that judgment, is
distinctly committed by the laws of Utah to the
discretion of the court or judge to whom appli-
cation for bail may be made. The Supreme
Court of Utah was, therefore, right in overrul-
ing the petition and remanding the prisoner to
the custody of the Marshal, and its judgment is
affirmed.
	Justices Miller and Field dissented.


[Column 1]

WATCHERS, WHAT OF THE HOUR?

	"The Lord, He is God!" for myself I acknowll
edge no other power; He has His agents—oppos-
ing elements there must be—and we see them
at work, The Savior said: "It must needs be
that offences come, but woe be to them by whom
they come." Some rejoice to do evil, rejoice to
afflict the righteous; such are ready instruments
for the offences named—hence we see there is no
injustice, for all may choose their own path,
therefore in the day of judgment they will stand
with their hand on their mouth, they preferred
works of darkness rather than light, therefore
they have their reward; their own conscience-
will be their judge.
	We live in a day and an hour when the most
light-minded must have their moments of seri-

[Column 2]

ous thought, I refer to L. D. S. especially, though
the thinking minds of the world are also at this
time posed to their full tension; but we have
done with the world. The Church and King-
dom of God is our world, and in that is full oc-
cupation for a true L. D. S.—first to look to
themselves, that their own door step is clean
and their own hearth bright and shining, made
so by a good heart, an eye single and a con
science void of offense towards God and towards
His creatures. The path of Saints was never
in any age a path of roses, only such as
the sunshine of the Holy Spirit can cause them
to produce and strew day by day on their indi-
vidual pathway, and this they have the tact and
the power to accomplish so that it is the warmth
and the sunshine and the fragrance of the same
that beautifies their life and makes even the
shades to be illumined. One would imagine such
a people would be beloved, would be admired as
worthy of imitation, especially by those calling
themselves Christians. Strange, passing strange
that such should excite "envy, hatred, malice
and all uncharitableness" in these "orthodox
Christians."
	Stay—are such Christians? Let me take the
New Testament and see how Christ organized
His church on earth during His brief mission on
the same. I read and realize that modern
Christianity is a libel, almost a burlesque; yea,
quite a burlesque, when I consider it in all its
phases, a mere ghost of that pure, simple, un-
adorned church of which Christ laid the foun-
dation, and then in His own person established
its living principles. All who read its records
intelligently must know that "comparisons are
odious" in such a review.
	The problem of the 19th century is simply a
restoration of this identical church, with all its
rights and ordinances and endowments estab-
lished again upon the earth by the Lord Him-
self calling out a youth as His Prophet, and
teaching him by His own mouth and Spirit how
again to re-organize His church upon the earth.
This God's truth was preached—thousaads glad
ly embraced it, souls that were hungering and
thirsting for the word of God. But there and
then rose up the opposing spirits and drove out
the embryo church again into the wilderness.
They traveled to a desert of sagebrush far away
from civilization and there they laid the founda-
tion of a city and of a territory without gold or-
silver, simply by the "sweat of the brow" and
the intelligence that God had freely given them;
and by the aid of His Spirit upholding them for
years they continued building homes, a place of
worship, and it was for years a home of saints,
as far as human nature can attain that title.
No saloon, no house of ill-fame, no place for sin
to establish itself contaminated its soil; peace,
union and sobriety reigned within its borders,
it grew, it increased in knowledge, wisdom and
understanding; missionaries were sent abroad;
the honest poor, and some rich rushed "as doves
to the window." Utah became a name—God
smiled upon her and blest the labors of the
honest, who left their homes like Abraham of
old to serve and worship Him exactly and pre-
cisely as the Savior laid down while on His
brief mission to this earth.
	Thousands from every land and clime flocked
to the standard erected in the mountains, ac-
cording to ancient prophecy. Utah, the one
word floating upon her banner; when behold
they were invaded by those who smelt gold and
riches of all kinds in this wonderful Utah. Mer-
chants rushed in with their merchandise of
every kind; soon they established the saloon as
a necessary appendage, and soon, behold the
demi monde flaunting through our streets, and
then came the railroad with its appendages, and
the very face of nature was changed—and so

[Column 3]

they get possession of homes, the land of which
had been prepared by the L. D. S., and instead
of stretching out the hand of neighborhood they
from the first laid plans to "oust the Mormons"
and usurp their homes and their possessions; and
not only so, but abuse, malign, traduce, and
even besiege Congress to raise the iron hand
upon this industrious, faithful, loyal people, and
strip them of those rights the glorious Constitu-
tion guarantees to all beneath her flag. Why is
this? The innocent, honest-minded ask. Read
the history of Christ while on the earth and
you will see "history repeats itself." Yet it
gives a solution nothing else can give of the
wherefore and why that this people are now so
cruelly persecuted. If infidels require a test of
the truth of ancient Scripture, let them read the
history of the latter day work—Christ's Church
re-established upon the earth, and their minds
must be opaque indeed if they do not see history
repeating itself, even like the voice of revelation.
Like those of old the saints cry out, Lord, how
long? Give us strength to endure to the end.
	HANNAH T. KING.


"THE WAY OF THE TRANSGRESSOR
IS HARD."

	They say, "Who seeth us and who knoweth
us?" God knows and sees all things, and all
things that we do he takes cogn zance of, and we
"are rewarded for the deeds done in the body,
whether they be good or evil."
	How necessary, then, that we do right and
not transgress God's laws and commandments,
for He cannot look upon sin with the least de-
gree of allowance. If we go into the secret
chamber to commit sin the word of the Lord
says, "It shall be revealed upon the house tops,
for the Lord loveth judgment and forsaketh not
His Saints; they are preserved forever: but the
seed of the wicked shall be cut off" "The
righteous shall inherit the land, and dwell there-
in forever." "The wicked watcheth the right-
eous and seeketh to slay him." Psalms 37th
chapter, 29th and 32nd verses.
	Oh! how great are the promises of the Lord to
the righteous. The Psalmist again says, "I have
not seen them forsaken nor their seed begging
bread," These are the days of repentance and
turning away from sin, that we may become a
sanctified people, waiting for the Lord's appear-
ing.
	Acts, 3rd chapter, 20th verse: "And He shall
send Jesus Christ, which before was preached
unto you." "Whom the heavens must receive
until the time of the restitution of all things,
which God hath spoken by the mouth of His
Holy Prophets since the world began." And as
the men of Galilee were looking up into heaven
the angels said, "This same Jesus which is
taken up from you into heaven shall so come in
like manner as ye have seen Him go into hea-
ven."
	Now, the Lord has been very merciful to the
inhabitants of the earth, and sent His holy
angels to warn them of judgments that are now
coming thick and fast, to repent of their sins,
accept of the true Gospel and prepare for His
coming again to the earth. It seems, indeed,
strange to me that people will persecute the
Latter-day Saints, because they promulgate the
true Gospel of the former-day Saints. The
Priesthood had to be restored by revelation, and
was—to Joseph Smith. He received the plates
on which the Book of Mormon was written, and
translated it by the gift and power of God. The
Urim and Thummin he received also, which
cons[-]itut[-]d him a seer and revelator, and a great
prophet to the world of mankind. God gave
him the greatest knowledge of doctrine, of prin-
ciples and history, of any man on the earth. He


gazed into the past and knew how to arrange
prophecy, he was active in the present to estab-
lish the Church of Christ, with that unity of
faith, which is the world's wonder, without
flaws, cross, or clashing of sentiment. His
knowledge of the future led his mind beyond
the grave to comprehend the immortal destiny
of God's creation. The work that be did could
not have been done without the aid of our
Father above. Joseph Smith was rejected be-
cause he was poor and because he testified to
the truth as God showed it unto him; and as
God's ways are not like man's ways, to which
the world cannot conform, no more than the un-
godly could to Christ's teachings,when He came
to the Jews. The Lord says He will do a strange
work and a wonder; and again, He says, He will
do nothing except He reve[-]leth Himself to His
servants the prophets. Then how can He do
His work on the earth without a prophet? Says
the unbeliever, "We need no more of these
things." If this be true, then God will be silent
forever and infidelity reign triumphant. We
must no more peruse the sacred book and believe
in its inspired pages. Its unfailing prophecies
in the past mark definitely its fulfilment in the
future, and as God is an unchangable being, He
will bring about His purposes in the same plain
and comprehensive manner as He has done in
all ages of the past.
	MARY ANN M. PRATT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

WASHINGTON CONVENTION.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The 16th annual convention of the National
Woman's Association has just terminated, and
in the main has been a success, though there
was imminent danger of disruption, to an ex-
tent, at least for a time. The convention was
held in the "Church of our Father"—Universal-
ist. The attendance throughout does not seem
to have bean as large generally as the year pre-
ceding—that is, attendance of people not actual-
ly members. This decrease, however, is proba-
bly due to the fact that the place ot meeting was
not so centrally located as is Lincoln Hall,
where the meetings had been held heretofore,
and could not be reached within several squares
by the street cars, the cold weather also may
have had the effect of lessening the number
present. However, in the evenings there were
generally as many as could comfortably be
seated, and the meetings were spirited and cal-
culated in the main to awaken a greater interest
in the movement and to furnish convincing
proof of its justice to those who may still enter-
tain doubts on the subject.
	Six sessions were held, two each day, begin-
ning at 2:30 and 7:30 p.m.,beginning on Tuesday
and continuing through Wednesday and Thurs-
day. The afternoons were devoted to reports
speeches and addresses; the evenings to ad-
dresses only, and while some were inclined to
weakness, there were many noticeable for depth
and breadth.
	The first meeting of the convention was
opened by invocation by Rev. Alex. Kent. On
the stand, platform or pulpit were seated Eliza-
beth Cady Stanton, Susan B. Anthony, Matilda
J. Gage, Ellen S.Fray, Caroline G. Rogers, El-
len H. Sheldon, Lillie D. Blake, and others,
while close about the platform were ladies iden-
tified with the movement who were more or less
prominent. The opening address was delivered
by Mrs. Stanton. She spoke in congratulatory
terms of the assemblage and the progress of the
work, ard said if men were not capable of mak-
ing cities and towns safe for youths to live in,
then they had better give way and see what
women could do. The women of the Republic

[Column 2]

had not done their duty so far in not taking
pains to see that their husbands were interested
in the subject and had formed an opinion on it.
Mrs. Stanton asked them to make up for lost
time. The members of each profession were
willing that woman should enter all others but
their own, though she had shown her ability to
shine in them all.
	Lillie D Blake gave a report of the work in
New York and made some extended remarks.
The way to stop opposition was to kill off those
who opposed the work. She told how she and
others had worked to defeat the election of as-
semblymen who had opposed the suffrage move-
ment as members of the State Legislature; and
called attention to the success with which their
efforts had met in killing these men politically
who worked against them and condemned them
to that death from which there was no res-
urrection. Every woman, she said, could
control at least one vote. Borne were
married, others would, she hoped, be
so soon, but if no such influences were
possible, then she should go to her groceryman
and others with whom she dealt and see that
he was in favor of the movement and would
support it with his vote, or she would give her
custom to some one that was in sympathy with
the cause.
	Sarah M. Perkins made a very able report of
the work done in Ohio, and she told graphically
how the Woman's Christian Temperance Union
had by a prayer been brought to espouse the
cause of woman's suffrage and make that cause its
own ,for the reason that the Temperance could
never hope to accomplish any permanent good
until woman, by the ballot, could say that the
dram shop 'round the corner, which was des-
troying her darling boys, should be forever
closed.
	Mrs. Perkins was followed by Frances E. Barr,
who reported work in Connecticut. The report,
while mainly encouaaging, was not as satifac-
tory as could be desired.
	Mrs. Harriet Shattuck of Malden, Mass.,gave
an address on "What Woman's Suffrage
Means." She went on to show that responsi-
bilities multiplied upon men and they were the
better for it, and that year by year duty upon
duty have been added to women and she had
ever qualified herself for it. The whole subject
was centred in the word—responsibility, and
while women already had many she wanted one
more—that of suffrage. She ridiculed, and ably
too, the idea that disqualification should be in-
sisted upon because of sex, and argued such a
conclusion to a logical conclusion, showing the
utter absurdity of such a position.
	Resolutions were then read, and it was stated
they would be open for discussion on the follow-
ing afternoon.


NEW YEAR'S RECEPTION.

	Sister E. R. S. Smith spent the Holidays iu
Brigham City. During her visit here she at-
tended several of our meetings and social gath-
erings and contributed much to their profit and
social enjoyment.
	On New Year's day a reception was held in
Prest. Lorenzo Snow's residence in honor of our
esteemed sister, under the supervision of Prest.
Minnie J. Snow, assisted by her counselor, Miss
Freddie Widerborg. One hundred ladies from
different wards of the city, members of the R.
S. and Y. L. M. I. Associations, in groups of
twenty five arrived at various intervals during
the day. As each party of guests appeared they
were cordially received, introduced and seated
in the parlor, where Sister E. R. S. Smith gave
expression to her surprise and appreciation of


this unexpected honor. Indulging a short time
in social conversation, many reminiscences of
by-gone days were related. At the expiration
of the time thus pleasantly employed, all re-
paired to the dining hall, surrounded the taste-
fully spread tables to partake of refreshments.
Here toasts, sentiments, etc., enlivened the
scene. Every succeeding company took Sister
E. R. S. S. unawares, as she supposed each de-
parture completed the day's surprises.
	One feature of the occasion was of peculiar in-
terest; the last party were Scandinavians, many
of whom were advanced in years, their counte-
nances radiant with joy as with faltering step
they advanced, to greet their esteemed friend.
At the table the kind wishes and pleasing senti-
ments offered in their native tongue, were inter-
preted by Sister Mary Stars, their President
and el[-]cted hearty and appropriate responses
from Sister E. R. S. S. and our generous hostess.
So passed the day: the occasion cheered and
made happy many hearts, and we all realized
the truthfulness of the proverb—"In blessing we
are blest."	M.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE.

	GARDEN CITY, Rich Co., Jan. 15,1883.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Thinking perhaps a few items from our north-
ern country would be welcome, I will try to pen
a few lines.
	I can truly say that your valuable little paper
is an ever welcome guest; we read it and enjoy
the instructions therein. And while we are en-
joying peace here, for which we feel thankful,
we do not know for how long a time. When I
read of what is termed legal proceedings with
our brethren in different places, many of them
born legal heirs to the Constitution for which
our forefathers fought and bled, I can but ex-
claim, Where, oh, where has liberty fled? They,
for the exercise of their religious faith, are put
into prison and fined; whereas, our glorious
Constitution guarantees unto all religions to en-
joy that right. It was written by inspiration,
and has prepared the way for the bringing to
pass the purposes of the Lord in establishing our
holy religion upon the earth in these the last
days. Then let us all, sisters, pray earnestly
unto the Lord to hasten the time when our
brethren may be removed from prison and re-
turned to their families and the bosom of the
Church, which may God grant.
	Our Relief Society in this place is in good
condition; we hold our meetings regularly, and
have the Spirit of God with us. The Young
Ladies' and Primary Associations are in success-
ful operation.
	Praying the Lord to bless you and give you
strength for your great labor, and for the differ-
ent societies and associations, I am your sister
in the Gospel,
	ANN E. COOK.


﻿PATTY SESSIONS.

[Continued.]
	Sister Sessions' notes in her diary are most
of them of a personal character; still we find
so many familiar names, it is pleasant to men-
tion one or more incidents connected with her
occupation in those days.
	On the 22nd of February she speaks of hav-
ing attended Sister Lightner, who had a son
born at that time. On the 7th of June Newel
Knight's wife had a daughter born. On the
14th of June her son Peregrine returned from
his mission East. On the 26th of July Orson
Spencer's wife had a daughter. September 27
she ays sthe Governor's troops were there.


[Column 1]

	We pass over the trying scenes of the people
in Nauvoo before and after the death of the
Prophet and his brother. Suffice it to say that
Sister Sessions suffered with the people in sac-
rificing her home, etc., and also mourned much
in her own breast, for she had the most pro-
found admiration and respect for him as the
Prophet of God.
	The Temple meantime was finished and dedi-
cated, and Bro. and Sister Sessions received
their endowments therein. They had assisted
in building it all in their power,and those were
days when the people were poor and persecut-
ed and had much to endure.
	The exodus from Nauvoo was an important
event, not only in the history of this Church,
but in the annals of the American nation, and
it will ever be a disgrace in the records of the
United States, where so much boast has been
made of freedom and religious liberty. We
pass on now to Sister Sessions' description of
leaving that beautiful city, and give it in her
own words:
	"Feb. 10, 1846, Nauvoo, or the City of
Joseph. My things are now packed ready for
the West, this afternoon I attended to the ac-
couchement of Mrs. Harriet Young; she had a
son. On the 11th I went to the Hall to see the
panorama of the massacre, as shown by Bro.
Dibble. 12th. This morning we bade our
children good-bye, camping at Sugar Creek,
where the exiles had made a sort of gathering
place for teams and wagons previous to start-
ing. Sister Sessions, writes on the 16th: "Pres-
ident Young came into camp this morning; it
is very cold and the wind blows hard and I
can scarcely get near the fire for smoke and we
have no tent. 17th. The brethren have begun
to organize and we go in the first hundred,sec-
ond ten, Prest. Young's company, Capt. Hol-
man."
	The weather continued severe; it was the
month of February and the people had just
left warm, comfortable homes. On the 22nd
Sister Sessions speaks of having visited Louisa
Beamen and Eliza R. Snow. On the 23rd Bro.
Sessions got canvas for a tent and Sister Ses-
sions made it herself. During the few days
they were in camp several children were born,
Sister Sessions attending some of the sisters in
their confinements.
	The last day of February the camp began to
move on slowly; the weather was inclement and
there were various difficulties and hindrances.
Sister Sessions speaks of having to drive the
team every now and then, because the cow gets
lost and her husband goes to hunt for it. On
the 16th Sister Sessions says she visited Sister
Zina D. with Sister Eliza R., and on the 22nd
she writes they cross the Shariton River; here
everybody had to double teams to get up the
bank and all the wagons had to have ropes at-
tached to them to drag them. It was at that
place she speaks of Sister Zina D. having a
son born. The weather was cold and wet and
the roads were so bad it was almost impossible
to travel.
	On the 6th of April she writes: "President
Young came into camp driving his own team,
then lighted a fire as happy as a king." That
night there came up a fearful thunder storm,
and at 2 o'clock in the morning Sister Sessions
was called up, a brother had come two miles
on horseback to fetch her, she rode back be-
hind him on the horse through the storm, the
mud and the water sometimes belly deep to the
horse; the child was born before the husband
and midwife arrived. The sister had traveled
13 miles after her sickness came on,had crossed
a creek on a log after dark, the storm had
carried the bridge away, and camped in an old
deserted log cabin. In such places as these
by the wayside many little ones were born that
are now fathers and mothers of families, though
many died as well as many of the mothers who
could not endure the hardships and fatigue.

[Column 2]

Sister Sessions' journal gives many items in re-
gard to building the bridges and difficulties
which hindered the onward progress of the
camp of Israel. Sometimes the teams would
get lost and the brethren would have to go out
hunting for them. Sister Sessions often men-
tions in her journal of dividing the little she
had with others who had still less, also of walk-
ing miles and miles, day after day, because the
teams were weak and they were short of food
for them. On the 19th there was a public
meeting, the first one called after leaving Nau-
voo.
	"Saturday, May 2. It has rained continu-
ously for six days and my bed has been wet
all the time; to-day I got my bed out to dry for
the first time." Sunday she writes it was rain-
ing hard again. Friday, 8th, she writes: "over-
hauled our wagons and sent off a saddle and
harness to sell; weighed our load, the weight
was 1,321 pounds, 551 is provisions, and we
have been dividing out ever since we started."
On the 10th she said they had a meeting and
the sacrament administered. "Thursday, 12th,
visited Sister Horne, who was sick, and Sister
Taylor, who was lame."
	The Saints at this time were stopping and
making a settlement; they fenced in some land
into a sort of big field and built some houses,
left some families there to take care of it and
put in a crop to make a sort of resting place
by the way, where those coming later might
find shelter and comfort in their journeyings.
On the 15th she writes in her journal: "Some
brethren from Nauvoo overtook us, they have
only been three weeks on the way and we have
been three months. They bring letters for
the brethren and sisters from relatives and
friends left behind."
	Monday, 25th, Sister Sessions says she was
feeling very bad and had been crying, think-
ing of her children she had left in Nauvoo, and
her mother in the State of Maine, and many
little things she had to contend with in her
travels, and Sister Eliza R. Snow and Sister
Markham came up to her wagon and it gave
her great joy to see these sisters. "28th, visited
Eliza Snow and Parley Pratt's folks. 29th.
Sister Rockwood gave me some tallow and I
run seventeen candles, thought it quite a pres-
ent. Lucy Walker arrived here to-day from
Nauvoo. Sunday, 31st, I was called to Parley
Pratt's, Mary had a son."
	At this time the Saints had reached another
stopping place, which was named Mt. Pisgah,
and Sister Sessions on leaving that place says:
"We leave many good brethren and sisters
here, Sisters Horne, E. R. Snow, Zina D. and
Emily P—— and many more. Wednesday,
3rd, she says, "Sister Horne sent on for me, I
went back to Mt. Pisgah and attended to her;
she had a daughter. I rode on horseback
seven miles each way." So she writes from
day to day how they journeyed on, and of the
various hindrances and stopping to make
bridges, etc. On the 28th of June a meeting
was held on the banks of the Missouri river or
the "Bluffs" near, where Dr. Willard Richards
preached to the people and spoke very feeling-
ly of its being the anniversary two years since
of his coming into Nauvoo with the bodies of
the Prophet Joseph and Hyrum. "Wednes-
day, July 1st, Brigham Young has moved
down to the river, the boat is done ready to
cross."


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	CORRESPONDENCE
	PROVIDENCE, Cache Co., Jan. 20, 1885.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	As it is seldom that an article appears in
your valuable little paper from Providence, if
you can spare the space I will inform the read-
﻿
[Column 2]
ers of the EXPONENT how we are progressing
in the work of God.
	The people are trying to live their religion,
and are enjoying a goodly portion of the Spirit
of God. All our associations are in a flour-
ishing condition,and a greater interest is taken
in them now than at any previous time. Our
Primary Association cannot be surpassed.
Every year about a week before Christmas
they have a fair and display all the work they
have done during the past year. All kind of
work is to be seen there, and some of it is real-
ly beautiful. In the morning there is an ex-
amination of all they have learned, and one
cannot help but mark the progress they have
made. It is a lovely sight to see the little ones
clinging to their parents and pointing to some
article they have made and say, “See, mother
I made that! isn’t it pretty?” And a very good
feature of the fair is: parents come and see
what their children have done and do not feel
satisfied until they have purchased it. By do-
ing this the association is supplied with funds
to pay its expenses.
	The Relief Society and Y. L. are progress-
ing favorably. They hold their meetings reg-
ularly, and they are well attended.
	There has been much sickness this winter,
but the health of the people is now good. There
have been a few deaths, however, and among
those who have passed from the troubles and
sorrows of this life are Sisters Lovisa Miller
Hammond and Mary Morris Gibbs. Sister
Hammond was the daughter of Daniel and
Clarissa Pond Miller. She was born Oct. 1st,
1834, in Hancock Co., Illinois, and removed
with her parents to this Territory when only a
girl. She was married to M. D. Hammond on
the 11th of Dec., 1853, and was the mother of
eleven children, all of whom are living, except
one daughter aged fifteen years, who died two
weeks before her mother. She died Dec. 9th,
1884, and leaves a large family and a wide cir-
cle of friends to mourn her loss. Sister Ham-
mond had been President of the R. S. of this
place for two years, and during that time she
did her utmost to make a good President. All
the members of the society respected and sus-
tained her in her position.
	Sister Gibbs, wife of Richard W. Gibbs, was
born July 15,1821, in the small town of Pon-
tyeats, Caernarthanshire, South Wales, and
was the mother of six children. She was bap-
tized into the Church of Jesus Christ of Lat-
ter-day Saints in 1845, and from that time to
the time of her emigration to Utah in 1869,her
house was a continual resort for the Elders,
and none ever came who were not welcome, or
did not feel her generous spirit. Sister Gibbs
died very suddenly on the morning of the 14th
of January, not having been sick a minute, to
any one’s knowledge. About seven o’clock
her husband heard her make an unusual noise,
but before he could light a lamp she was dead.
She died as the had lived, a true saint, and
many warm friends mourn her loss.
	The people are enjoying many blessings be-
stowed upon them by their Heavenly Father,
and many manifestations of His power and
goodness are shown  continually in our midst.
I can testify to many instances where He has
made manifest His power in healing my little
children and myself when we have been sick,
and in caring for us and comforting me in my
lonely hours while my husband is on his mis-
sion; and also in caring for my husband,raising
up friends for him, and giving him health and
strength that he may go forth and carry His
word to the honest in heart and teach them the
principles of the Gospel. For all His mercies
and blessings I am truly thaukful and grateful,
and desire to continue a faithful servant in
His Kingdom. 
	ANNE W. SMITH.


KOOSHAREM, Jan. 11, 1885.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Thinking that you would like to hear from
us in this remote valley, we will give you a
brief sketch of what we are doing.
	Our Society is in as good a condition as we
can wish for; we have a good substantial log
house and a granary attached to it, which will
accommodate us for several years, which has
been dedicated. The sisters all seem to be
alive to their duties and are willing and on
hand to help roll forth this great and mighty
work which we are engaged in,
	Wishing the EXPONENT success, for it is a
welcome visitor in our midst.
	Respectfully,
	MARGARET ANDERSON, Pres.
	ANNIE M. WRIGHT, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. ﻿


PRIMARY REPORT
		   	
	Minutes of Primary Association of Fifth
Ward, Jan. 14, 1885, Prest. Clark presiding.
Opened by singing“Gather up the Sunbeams;”
Prayer by Prest. Clark; Singing "Nay, speak
no ill.”	Roll called, 57 members present; min-
utes read and accepted. The programme was
then carried out as follows: Recitation by E. 
 Brimley; Recitation, R. Cowan; Song, Mor-
mon Boy,” Roy Palm[-]r; Essay, “Happiness,”
Amy Harper; Reading from Doc. & Cov., Lily
Pettit; Song, “Dare to be a Daniel,” E. Abbott;
Organ Solo, Lizzie Marsden, Duet, Lottie and
Ella Marcroft; Bible Reading, Ernest Brown;
Duet by Lily Appleby and Lizzie Brown.  An
account of the annual meeting was then read,
	Sister Clawson then said that she was
pleased to be present, and especially to see so
many boys, as this spoke well for the future.
Asked how many dismissed meeting, also who
were the Bishop and Counselors of the ward
and Superintendent of Sunday School, which
was answered promptly by members. Spoke
about the necessity of being obedient, polite
and unselfish; hoped all would remember to
pray and be ready to do whatever Sister Clark
desired.
	Sister Ferguson then said Sister Clawson
had praised the boys,but one thing she noticed
was, that their hands were very dirty (when
quite a number put them in their pockets).
Spoke about a story she had heard, where a
little boy whose mother was sick, had prayed
for her, and she recovered through his faith.
Said children’s prayers are sometimes heard
even more than ours, as their prayers are so in-
nocent and they have great faith. Spoke of
the consequences of disobedience. Said honor
thy father and mother was a revelation to
children, and if you are in the path of duty
the Lord will send His guardian angels to pro-
tect you; but if you do wrong the adversary
will tempt you. Asked all to pray for the
Lord to preserve them from every ill.
	Sister Clark then said she hoped all would
remember the instructions which had been
given, and would like  to  see visitors at any
time. 
	Programme was then read; closed by singing
“Love at Home;” Benediction by Sister Claw-
son.


﻿GOOD TIME AT WILLARD.

EDITOR EXPONENT:	
	Thinking that a few lines from our quiet
little town would not be uninteresting to the
many readers of your valuable paper, we en-
close the following:
	It is a general time of good health among
the people of this place. The holidays have
passed off quietly and pleasantly; especially so
did Christmas day, when a social party was


held under the auspices of the Y. L. M. I. A.
of Willard, which was a very enjoyable and
praiseworthy affair.
	At 1:30 o’clock p.m., the invited guests as-
sembled at the hall, which was tastefully de-
corated with mottoes, pictures, etc., for the oc-
casion. At 2 o’clock the afternoon exercises
began, and until 5 p.m those present were en-
tertained with speeches, songs, recitations and
instrumental music, all of which were credit-
ably rendered.	
	The afternoon exercises being over, all were
permitted to partake freely of the contents of
the baskets of the members of the. Y. L. M. I.
A., which were well filled with the bounties of
this earth, so tastefully prepared that none
could refuse partaking of the pleasant repast,
After picnic those present went forth and en-
joyed themselves in the dance until near mid-
night, when all wended their way home im-
pressed with the idea that the day was one not
soon to be forgotten.		
	Your sister in the Gospel,
	MARION.S. PERRY, Prest.,
	LOTTIE E. HUBBARD Cor. Sec.


OBITUARY

	DIED, at St. Johns, Arizona, Sister Harriet Zelnora
Chapman, beloved wife of Elder Welcome Chapham
Deceased was born May 13, 1854, in Sheffield, Yorkshire
England; emigrated in the William Tapscot, 1862, with
her mother and a younger sister. She has been the
mother of four children; only one, a little girl aged 13,
now living. Bro. and Sister Chapman were called to
Arizona on a mission at the last Spring Conference
Early in Octob[-]r Sister Chapman was hooked by a cow
and died ten weeks afterwards from the effects of the
injuries recelved.
	She was a loving daughter, wife and mother: we
mourn her loss, yet feel all is well, with those who have
been faithful and true and have endured unto the end.
May our Heavenly Father shed abroad His Holy Spirit
in the hearts of those who mourn,especially her mother,
Sister Sarah Mc.DonaId of this city, whose heart is
sorely stricken at the loss of her daughter; and may she
remember in this her hour of sorrow the comforting
words of our blessed Savior: “Blessed are they that
mourn, for they shall be comforted"—and the words of
the inspired Prophet of God who said, “Blessed are the
dead who die in the Lord; yea, they rest from th[-]ir la-
bors and from henceforth their, works shall follow
them.”—COM.
				
	DIED, at South Cottonwood Ward, Dec. 2nd, 1884,
of a chill, Floy, beloved daughter of Joseph H. and
Nancy E. Proctor; aged ten years and eleven months.
	We, The Primary Association of South Cottonwood
Ward, in view of-the loss we have sustained by the
decease of our friend and associate,
	Therefore, Be it resolved, that it is but a just tribute
to the memory of the departed, we offer to the parents
our sincere sympathy, and say, while we mourn the loss
of our dear Floy, we shall look forward with joy to the
time when we shall meet the dead. The angels called
and in ways of an all wise Providence it was that she
should go. Though the days are dark now springtime
will come once more. Thus we trust pleasant days will
come again for you and yours.
	"This lovely bud, so young, so fair,
		Called hence by early doom, 
	Just came to show how sweet a flower
		In paradise would bloom.”
		MRS. ELIZABETH DAVIS, Prest.
		MRS. MARGARET CAHOON,
		MRS. SARAH A. WOOTON,	Counselors. 
And members of the South Cottonwood Ward Primary
Association.
		EMILY E. BRINTON, Secretary.
