WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

	The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 13.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 15, 1885.


OUR TRUST IS IN GOD.

No one can come between me and my God;
	None can hide from me His glorious face;
But I may walk the path which Jesus trod,
	And unrestrained may seek His love and grace.

Oh, glad assurance! this but proves to me 
	Beyond a doubt my own divinity, 
For though I bend beneath the chastening rod,
	No one can come between me and my God.

No earthly power, no king nor potentate,
	No power of hell can dare to touch the soul 
Immortal, God-like; no one can dictate,
	Or bring the heart’s emotion ’neath control.

The body they may torture and afflict,
	Imprison, take the life which God has given, 
The soul its upward flight cannot restrict
	It will return again to God and heaven.

How vain the plans and schemes of mortal men
	To dare coerce our faith and trust in Him, 
To try to dictate what we shall believe, 
	Or say how much of truth we shall receive.

We neither tremble or defy their power,
	But bravely meet the issues of our cause;
We will not falter in the trying hour;
	Our God will help us to obey His laws.
	E. A. BENNION.
Feb. 4th, 1885.


[Column 1]
	THE PRESENT DAY.
			    	
	FOR THE YOUNG LADIES’ M. I. ASSOCIA-
				  TIONS
				  
	What is the present but the connecting link
between the past and the future?  Through 
and during the present alone we can make the
past improve the future.  All the improve-
ments of civilization have come to us from the
knowledge and proper criticism of the events
of the past, by right-minded and ambitious
men and women.  But for them and their la-
bors the world of to-day would be groaning un-
der the chains of cruel barbarism.  As great
as between noonday and midnight gloom is the
contrast between the Constitutional Govern-
ment of the United States of America and
that of the most enlightened nations of two
centuries ago.  The same can be said of arts
and sciences; we do not hear of a cathedral be-
ing a century in  process of erection.  In less
than a century the improvements, inventions
in all arts and sciences have been multitudin-
our; but how and by whom have they been
brought about?  Not by the drones, who said:
"Let us eat, drink and be merry, for to-morrow
we die.”  No, but by men and women whose
education at the school of endurance, hard-
ship and abnegation, has resulted in a stern
sense of duty to future generations, and an un-
quenchable thirst for knowledge. “Knowledge
is power!”  What a theme for reflection! But
I am addressing myself to the young ladies of
Utah on the subject of the present, and the du-
ties and privileges it holds out to them.
	I hope none of my young sisters are igno-
rant of the fact that it is their privilege and duty
to become wives and mothers, and thould there-
fore spend their youth in qualifying themselves
for these important callings.  Do they think
of these things as they meet in their Improve-
ment Associations?  Do they suggest means to
check evil and promote good?  The physical,
intellectual and moral improvement of the

[Column 2]
girls of to-day means the improvement of the
nation in the next generation.
	I do not think that the real object in organ-
izing these associations for young ladies is ever
mentioned in some places, and seldom referred
to in others.  They are for improvement, say
some.  Yes, so are the day schools and Sunday
Schools; but why are young ladies organized
into societies of their own?  That they may
exchange thoughts, make suggestions as to the
best means of retaining their health and vigor,
how to cultivate good manners without affec-
tation, and how to store their minds with use-
ful knowledge without neglecting the most
practical duties of house-wifery. 
	Do not deem me harsh if I make bold to re-
mind you that the children of a sulky, weak-
minded, self-indulgent mother can scarcely be
expected to become fine, intelligent, fearless
men and women.
	Young ladies, do you realize that the work
of regeneration and reform, for the next half
century, depends a great deal upon you, indi-
vidually and collectively?  It is so whether
you realize it or not.  It is for you to decide
whether the men and women of the near future
shall be of the fine physique, sturdy valor and
sterling integrity, or frivolous, slothful and ef-
feminate.  The past is open to you to read and
reflect upon; tha present is the field for you to
labor in, and the future anxiously awaits the
result.
	E. C. FRANCIS.


[Column 1]

PRAYER.

"Prayer is the soul's sincere desire."

	Our most inward thoughts and desires, wheth-
er good or evil, are known to the Lord; He
can read our hearts as we can read a printed
book. Then how necessary it is for us to be
pure in our thoughts as well as in our actions.
God is the Father of our spirits; His ears are
always open to the prayers of His children.
What a beautiful, consoling thought to know
that we have a Father in heaven whom we can
approach in our hour of trial and tribulation,
even if we are deserted by all we hold dear
upon earth, knowing that He will never forsake
His children nor leave them comfortless; for
our Savior says, "Ask, and ye shall receive;
knock, and it shall be opened unto you." What
is more easy than this, only to ask and we shall
receive. And we as Saints and daughters of
God know that He has not made this promise
in vain.
	I believe every one of us, if we live as we
should do, can bear testimony to the efficiency
of prayer. How many times has the Lord
heard our prayer in regard to our little ones
when upon a bed of suffering, and restored
them to health according to our prayers and
the desires of our hearts. The Lord says He
knows the desires before asking Him, but He
likes us to ask for what we want, and that in
faith. How many times have the sick been
healed, the deaf made to hear, the lame to
walk, and even the dead raised to life through
the prayer of faith?
	How is it that we are so abundantly blessed
and prospered in these valleys of the moun-
tains, being of so many nationalities. It is
through the prayers and faith of the Saints, for
the Lord loves a prayerful people, and will
protect and bless them. He loves to listen to

[Column 2]

them and to grant their requests. It is not
the length of our prayers that is most accepta-
ble to the Lord, but the earnest desires of a
honest heart. How simple, yet how beautiful,
is the prayer our Savior taught His Disciples,
"Our Father which art in heaven, etc." In
another instance our Savior says: "What things
soever ye desire when ye pray, believe that ye
receive them and you shall have them; and
when praying forgive if ye have aught against
any, that your Father which is in heaven may
also forgive you your trespasses." He also
says, if we are joyful, to praise Him with sing-
ing, as a song of the heart is a prayer unto the
Lord. We are also told to watch and pray,
lest we enter into temptation.
	What is a more beautiful sight than to see a
little child in pure simplicity kneeling at its
mother's knee offering up a prayer to our
Heavenly Father, and we have read of many
instances where their prayers have been an-
swered, and I believe the Lord hears the
prayer of a little child even quicker than He
does of older people, because they have not
committed any sin, and are therefore pure. But
our Savior tells us to ask and it shall be given;
seek, and ye shall find; and to every one that
knocketh it shall be opened. He also tells us
that the hairs of our head are all numbered,
and that not even a sparrow falls to the ground
without His notice. And in Him we live and
move and have our being.
	Men generally do not believe in these things,
but we as Latter-day Saints know it to be true,
and that there is no safety without prayer.
We read that our Savior Jesus Christ, before
His crucifixion, went into the Garden of Geth-
semane, which is the Mount of Olives, and in
agony He prayed to God that if it were possi-
ble, to let the cup pass; He also took some of
His Disciples with Him and asked them to
watch while He prayed; and while He was
offering His prayer to His Father to sustain
Him in His trying hour, they slumbered and
slept. We find that He did this three times,
and each time He found them asleep. The
third time He said: "Sleep on now and take
your rest, the Son of Man is betrayed into the
hands of sinners."
	If Jesus our Savior had occasion to continu-
ally plead with the Lord for His aid and assist-
ance,how much more necessary it is for us,poor,
weak fallen humanity to pray without ceasing
that the Spirit of God may be our constant
guide and companion through all the joys and
ills of life so that we may be able to stand firm
as the Rock of Ages, holding on to the Rod of
Iron, ever remembering that the Lord is our
friend, that He is a God that is near and not
afar off, that He knows our every thought and
desire, and that He is ever ready to hear and
answer our prayers; and may we ever be able
to say that in life or death we are the Lord's.
	I would then exhort you, my young sisters,
never to neglect or forget your prayers if you
would have the Lord for your friend and
guide, and by obedience to God's laws and
seeking to live nearer unto our Heavenly
Father we may hold communion with holy be-
ings, for there is no blessing the Lord will
withhold from His children if they are only
humble and prayerful.	A. N. B.
	Jan 28, 1885.


London has a Sunday paper, the Age, devot-
ed to gossip, with 50,000  circulation.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


AUTOBIOGRAPHY
		OF EMILY D. P. YOUNG.
				Continued.
	“On the 5th Elders Phelps, Gilbert and Mc-
Lellin went to Clay Co. and made affidavit
similar to the foregoing sketch and forwarded
the same to the Governor by express; and the
Governor immediately upon the reception
thereof ordered a court of inquiry to be held
in Clay Co. for the purpose of investigating the
whole affair, and meting out justice to all: but
alas!  corruption, wickedness and power have
left the wretches unwhipped of justice, and in-
nocence mourns in tears unwiped.
	“Thursday, Nov. 7th.  The shores began to
be lined on both sides of the ferry with men,
women and children, goods, wagons, boxes,
chests, provisions, etc., while the ferrymen
were busily employed crossing them over. And
when night again closed upon the Saints the
wilderness had much the appearance of a camp
meeting.  Hundreds of people were seen in
every direction, some in tents, and some in the
open air around their fires while the rain de-
scended in torrents.  Husbands were inquiring
for their wives and women for their husbands,
parents for children and children for parents.
Some had the good fortune to escape with
their family, household goods and some provis-
ions, while others knew not the fate of their
friends and had lost all their goods.  The scene
was indescribable, and would have melted the
heart of any people upon earth, except the
blind oppressor and ignorant bigot.  Next day
the company increased, and they were chiefly
engaged in felling small cottonwood trees and
erecting temporary cabins, so that when night
came on they had the appearance of a village
of wigwams, and the night being clear the occu-
pants began to enjoy some degree of comfort.
	“Lieut. Gov. Boggs presented a curious ex-
ternal appearance, yet he was evidently the
head and front of the mob;  for, as may easily
be seen by what follows, no important move
was made without his sanction.  He certainly
was the secret spring of the 20th and 23rd of
July, and, as will appear in the sequel, by his
authority the mob was moulded into militia to
affect by stratagem what he knew as well as
his hellish host could not he done by legal
force.  As Lieut. Gov. he had only to wink
and the mob went from maltreatment to mur-
der.  The horrid calculations of this second
Nero were often developed in away that could
not be mistaken.
	“Early in the morning of the 5th, say at one
o’clock a.m., he came to Phelps, Gilbert and
Partridge and told them to flee for their lives.
Now, unless he had given the order so to do no
one would have attempted to murder after the
Church had agreed to go away.  His con-
science vacillated on its rocky moorings and
gave the secret alarm to those men.  The
Saints who fled took refuge in the neighboring
counties, mostly in Clay Co., which received
them with some degree of kindness.  Those
who fled to the county of Van Buren were
again driven and compelled to flee, and those
who fled to Lafayette Co. were soon expelled,
or the most of them, and had to move wherever
they could find protection."
	It was in Nov., 1833, that my father moved
his family into Clay Co.	We crossed the Mis-
souri river at a ferry not far from Liberty
landing.  Most of father’s provisions were left
in his cellar, not being able to move it on such
short notice.  And thus two years of my child-
hood were spent in Jackson Co., Missouri.  The
brethren cut down, small trees and laid them
up cob fashion, and when they were five or six
feet high they stretched a tent over the top for
a roof.  And such was my home on the bank
of the Missouri river, in the month of Nov.,
1833. I was then in my tenth year.
	On the 13th of Nov., between three and
four o'clock in the morning the camp was
aroused from their slumbers to witness the
beautiful and grand sight of the falling stars.
The Saints beheld it with hearts of rejoicing.
Being persecuted and cast out from their homes
for the sake of their religion, and knowing it
to be one of the signs of the last days spoken
of by the Prophets, it was calculated to cheer
and comfort their hearts and strengthen their
faith in the Gospel, notwithstanding they were
in deep affliction.  Although I was but a child
at the time, I looked upon the scene with de-
light.  The heavens seemed wrapped in splen-
did fireworks.  The appearance was beautiful
and grand beyond description.
	I think it was several weeks that we were
camped on the bank of the river.  The wea-
ther began to be quite cold—too cold to remain
in tents with any degree of comfort.  The
Saints found homes as best they could, search-
ing out and making habitable all the old shan-
ties and hovels that could be found, endeavor-
ing to keep as near together as possible.  Father
and Elder John Corrill procured an old log
cabin that had been used for a stable and
cleaned it up as best they could  and moved
their families in.  There was one large room,
and a leanto, but that was not of much use, as
the floor was nearly all torn up, and the rats
and rattlesnakes were too thick for comfort.
There was a large fireplace in the one habita-
ble room, and blankets were hung up a few
feet back from the fire and the two families,
fifteen or sixteen in number, were gathered in-
side of those blankets to keep from freezing,
for the weather was extremely cold, so cold
that the ink would freeze in the pen as father
sat writing close to the fire.  Elder Corrill’s
family took one side of the fireplace and we
took the other.  Our beds were in the back
part of the room, which was cold enough for
the polar regions.
	The next summer the Saints procured a
small cabin in a paupau grove for a school and
one of our Mormon girls was installed as teach-
er.  And notwithstanding our deplorable cir-
cumstances I spent many happy hours with the
school children in that beautiful grove at hours
of intermission, swinging on the long wild
grapevine that hung from the tall trees, or
tearing down some of the long and slender
ones to jump-the-rope with.  We built houses
of the branches of the paupau tree, the wood
being very brittle we had no difficulty in
breaking as many as we wanted.  The fruit of
the paupau is about as long as the banana, and
about four or five times as large around, with a
smooth skin.  The inside, when ripe, is a yel-
low, thick, creamy pulp, with- large flat seeds,
and to the taste is very sweet; but oh ! such a
sweet lone taste will generally suffice, nobody
wants to taste it twice.
     Some of the old citizens of Clay Co. sent
their children to our school and of course were
better dressed than the Mormon children,
which caused them to sometimes sneer and
make fun of our shabby clothes, but generally
we got along very well.  The Saints were very
poor, and I sometimes wonder how they pro-
vided for their families the necessaries of life.
My father being Bishop made the times much
harder for him, for he not only had his family
to provide for, but he had the poor to look
after, and provide for their comfort also.
	I sometimes think that Bishops in these
days know but little what the office of Bishop
was in the early days of the Church—in the
days of its poverty and inexperience.  Some-
times the poor would grumble and complain
because there was not more for them.	 To
raise money in those days was almost like
wringing water out of a dry sponge.

[column 3]
A GIRL AUTHOR!
	Ellen Gibson was born in Winchendon, May
8,1821. When five years of age her parents
removed to Ringe.  She possessed an active,
nervous temperament; learned readily and was
at the head of her class.  Her first published
articles appeared in the miscellaneous depart-
ment of the “Boston Cultivator,’' to which she
contributed for several years.  She began school
teaching at the age of fifteen and met with de-
cided success in that vocation.  In 1848 she
was compelled to relinquish teaching on ac-
count of ill health.
	When the War of the Rebellion broke out
she was in the west lecturing; and she became
enthusiastic with the patriotic spirit, and gave
her services in raising soldiers and societies
under the patronage of Gov. Salmon of Wis-
consin.
	During the first N. W. Sanitary Fair held
in Chicago she sold thirteen hundred copies of
a little book she wrote, entitled “The Soldiers'
Gift,”or“The Dangers and Temptationsof Army
Life; giving the proceeds to the fair.' In 1864
she was recommended as chaplain by Governor
Lewis and General Fairfield of Wisconsin, and
assigned to the First Wis. Vol. Artillery, Pres-
dent Lincoln gave her his testimonial: “This
lady would be appointed chaplain of the First
Wis. Heavy Artillery, only she is a woman.
The President has not legally any thing to do
with such a question, but has no objection to
her appointment.	      A. LINCOLN.” Dated Nov.
10,1865.
	She finally served without being mustered
in.  She was known during the war as Ellen
E. I. Hobart.  She now resides in Barre, Mass.
From the New England Historical Genea-
logical (Society) Register. Voi. XXXYII, p.
392,


﻿MISCELLANEOUS.

	England had only 1,311 hours of bright sun-
shine last year.
	A Berlin doctor has been fined for not keep-
ing himself posted on modern methods of prac-
tice.
	There are 347 women in England who live
by blacksmithing and actually swing heavy
hammers.
	A sociable man is one who, when he has ten
minutes to spare, goes and bothers somebody
that hasn't:
	The snowfall on the Italian Alps is the
heaviest within the memory of man. It is cal-
culated that 300 lives have been lost through
the storm.
	Unprejudiced. Mrs. Newgold (in picture
grllery—This, Aunt Eunice; is a real old mas-
ter. Aunt Eunice—Well, I shouldn’t care if
it was; it’s just as good as some of the new ones.
—Life.
	Does any man wound thee? Not only for-
give, but work into thy thought intelligence of
the kind of pain, that thou mayest never in-
flict it on another spirit.—Margaret Fuller.
	The Magazine of Art for February is superb
in illustration as in letter press.. The frontis-
piece is a fac-simile in colors of Downman's
portrait of Lady Maria Waldergrave, with her
piquant beauty; the views of Artists' Homes
shows a prosperous age in art; some fine por-
traits are given in Editor Henley's paper, "In
Character." The tinted page of poetry and
picture this month is called "In Arcady." "The
Marvelous Madonna," "A Round in France," a
portrait of the eminent artist Elihu Vedder,
with reproductions from his drawings, are
among the brilliant features of the magazine.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


THE HEART DOOR.

There is a little grave, watered well with tears,
	Where I used to sit when winds were still,
Dreaming sad dreams of the might have been,
	Indulging in vain regrets until
The Savior entered, and shut the door,
And that old pain comes to my heart no more.

Did He not say, as He used to walk
	On the vine-clad hills of old Galilee,
The sweetest thing He said or taught
	Was, "Let the children come unto me."
So, when He entered in my heart's door,
Sorrow departed to come no more!

	MARY KNAPP. 
Selected.


TOO LATE.
BY NORA PERRY.
What silence we keep year after year
With those who are near to us and dear!
We live beside each other day by day
And speak of myriad things, but seldom say
The full, sweet word that lies just in our reach
Beneath the commonplace of  common speech.

Then out of sight and out of reach they go—
These close, familiar friends who loved us so;
And, sitting in the shadow they have left,
Alone with loneliness, and sore bereft,
We think, with vain regret, of some fond word
That once we might have said, and they have heard.

For weak and poor the love that we expressed
Now seems beside the vast, sweet unexpressed,
And slight the deeds we did to those undone,
And small the service spent to treasures won,
And undeserved the praise for word and deed
That should have overflowed the simple need.

This is the cruel cross of life, to be
Full visioned only when the ministry
Of death has been fulfilled, and in the place
Of some dear presence is but empty space.
What recollected services can then
Give consolation for the might have been?
INDEPENDENT.


SCENES AND INCIDENTS AT
WINTER QUARTERS.
BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY.
(Continued.)
	My husband wrote: “Wednesday, the 21st,
fine day—this morning, by request of Brother
Kimball, went down to the ferry, six miles
from here; found them just crossing the river.
Little John Smith and myself drove up the
loose cattle and arrived at the camp a little
after dusk.  The family all got here soon
after.”
	“Bishop Whitney,Bros. Woolley and Wright
arrived from St. Louis on the 26th.  The goods
—amounting to about sixty tons—had to be
brought up from St. Joseph by teams.  Brother
Woolley returned to take the charge.  Eight
teams were started the 2nd day of Nov.”
	On the 6th Horace speaks of Wm. Clayton
and George Grant “returning from the village
on the other side of the river, whither they
went on business for the Church.  Bro. Clay-
ton informs me that he never saw such a
swindling operation as that practised on the
Indians down there—they received their pay-
ments in money and were then followed by a gang
of sharpers, who watching their opportunity,get
them drunk and then gamble with them and
cheat them out of all their money.”
	Sunday, the 8th, he mentions the “Omahaws
going through our settlement, their horses
loaded down with tent poles, baggage, etc., on
their way north to the Grand Hunt."
     Saturday, the 7th,he speaks of being engaged
in roofing and sodding the house, into one
room of which we moved that evening. He
says: “We congratulate ourselves consider-
ably upon being able to live in a house again,
as we have got throughly tired of living in
tents.” We continued to use our wagon as a
bedroom till the 30th, when it was wanted to
bring produce from the country.  By this time
quite a number of houses had been put up.
This one contained a number of rooms, built
in a row, between my father’s and Bishop
Whitney’s main buildings.  One room was
used by father for storing provisions, etc.  My
sister-in-law, Sarah, with whom we had tented
so long, occupied a room adjoining ours, though
it was not yet ready for her to move into.  We
lived in ours one month before it was entirely
finished.
	On the 8th of Dec. Horace mentions being
engaged “mudding up his room,” his brother
Orson assisting him.  This, like the majority
of the houses, was covered with sod, and the
chimneys were built of the same.  Each room
had one door and a window with four panes
of glass, but no floor.  I was rather unfortun-
ate, first,in having a chimney that seldom drew
the smoke, particularly when the weather was
cold enough to need a roaring fire in front of
a good sized back-log, and then being prostrat-
ed on my bed from the 23rd of January till
along in March; it gave me the opportunity of
cultivating the qualities of patience and calm-
ness under new vicissitudes, from which there
was no alternative, only to endure them with
as good grace as possible, for many of the
Saints were still without a roof to cover them;
but I shed many unbidden tears during the
smoking period, lasting one month, when find-
ing that our fireplace built of sod was about to
tumble down,they had some brick brought from
the rubbish of the Old Fort of Council Bluffs,
and built a new one.  Thus ended our trouble
from that quarter.  We had been accustomed
to trials from smoke, heat, wind and dust, and
many other things of an unpleasant nature
during camp life, and we took considerable
pleasure in fixing up our little homes.  Our
floors we managed to cover with canvas or
pieces of carpeting, which had outlived the
storms and the wear and tear while journey-
ing from the States. We made curtains serve
as partitions to divide the bedrooms, reposi-
tories, etc., from the kitchen.  Most of our
furniture we had made to order—such as cup-
boards and bedsteads—they being attached to
the house, also tables, chairs and stools, and an
occasional rocking chair, relics of other days,
graced our ingleside.  I was fortunate in hav-
ing one of the latter, which I had brought
with me.  And here I received my “setting
out” in crockery ware, etc, which, though not
very extensive, was deemed quite immense for
those times.  Our marriage taking place just
as we were about starting from the states, the
presenting of these needful articles was post-
poned till a future time, expecting, as we then
did, to cross the Rocky Mountains before build-
ing houses to inhabit. Two or three pieces I
have still, which I keep in memory of the vari-
ous and peculiar scenes through which we have
passed together; as well as the loved ones who
have passed away.
	The larger houses were generally shingled
and had brick chimneys and puncheon floors,
with a six lighted window to each room.
Father’s largest house contained four good
sized rooms on the ground, and two up stairs.
My bro. William and family lived in one room,
my mother, her four little boys, three or four
young men and two young women who had
been adopted, and two of father’s wives occu-
pied the rest; the women assisting in sewing
and housekeeping.  My mother bad been for
some time in very feeble health, and her
youngest child, who was so sick with whooping
cough, when starting from Nauvoo, was taken
care of by Mary Ann Sheflin, one of father’s
wives who lived in the row.  She, having
charge of him most of the way on the journey,
she loved him as though he had been her own
child.
	Horace wrote on the 12t'n, “Bro. Kimball,
Bishop Miller and myself went to Brigham’s
house, which is just finished, in honor of which
we spent the evening in dancing.  Bro. John
Kay sung a few songs and the company dis-
persed, having had a real  good “house warm-
ing.” Bishop Miller, Peter Haw.es and L
Woodworth slept at my house to-night on the
floor.  The latter is quite sick with the fever.
*	*	*	*	The brethren, for
the last two or three days, have been in council
with Bishop Miller, trying to take action on
His case before he goes back to the Punkaw na-
tion.”
	The Bishop had just returned from the coun-
try, where he had been, with others of his
company, for produce.  He left for home with
part of his company on the 15th.  Many of the
brethren had gone down into Missouri to work
or to trade for provisions, which consisted,
mostly, of corn and bacon; the latter, with
corn meal cakes,was our main subsistence dur-
ing the winter.  Vegetables, and many of the
necessaries of life were not obtainable.  Indian
meal cake and puddings we considered very
nice when used as rarities, as we were accus-
tomed to doing in the east, but when we had
little or no change, they became somewhat
nauseous, particularly to the sick and delicate.


A PLEASANT GATHERING.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	In honor of Prest. E. G. MacDonald on her
54th birthday, her numerous friends inaugura-
ted a party, which proved a perfect success in
every respect, the lady not having the least
idea of the surprise awaiting her. When she
saw her domicile invaded by such a number,
loaded down with good things,she accepted the
situation and welcomed them with a hearty
good will, which made everybody feel at home.
After some pleasant conversation and a birth-
day address by one of the ladies of the party,
supper was served, and the tables fairly groaned
beneath the good things that were loaded upon
them; 47 persons partook of supper, and, as it
was remarked, it seemed like the "widow's
cruse" of oil, there was still plenty when all
was served.
	The evening passed pleasantly away; Bro.
Chas. Robison made a brief speech, expressing
a wish that the dark clouds that were now hov-
ering over her and us might soon vanish away
before the effulgent rays of the coming day,
and wished her "many happy returns of the
day," each one happier than the last. Sister
E. G. MacDonald expressed her gratitude to
the brethren and sisters for the kindly feelings
that had prompted their friendly greeting.
	There was but one thing to mar the pleasure,
of the evening, and that was the absence of
her husband, who was absent on a trip through 
Southern Arizona, laboring for the interest of
the mission, for which he is laboring incessant-
ly.
	Our Relief Society is in good working or-
der, as are also the different associations. Our
Society Hall is so far completed that social
parties and entertainments are being held in it.
Although dark clouds seem to be gathering,
and the prospects are that we shall have our
share of persecution; still, there is a deep abid-
ing faith with the people that the Lord lives
and rules and will overrule every thing for the
best if we obey His laws in every respect.
	God bless you and all connected with the
Exponent, which is carrying gratification and
pleasure into so many households, is the con-
stant prayer of your friend and sister in the
Gospel,	S. A. P.
	Mesa City, Arizona.

SALT LAKE CITY, FEBRUARY 15, 1885.


[Column 1]

RELIGIO-P0LITICAL CRUSADE.

	The crusade against the people of Utah, the
original settlers, the actual pioneers of this new
country, this sometime barren desert, has bagun
in dead earnest, judging from the events trans-
piring at the present time, and the bitterness
and utter lack of honor that is apparent on the
part of those unscrupulous men, who have un-
dertaken with zeal, worthy a better cause, to
manipulate the present religious political cam-
paign.
	These officers of the law and their emissaries
would fain make people believe they are anxious
to promote a better condition of society, and
make the country more popular; in fact would
elevate all who would be guided by them.
Would they not then take away the agency of
the individual? If you want to believe in Mor-
monism it is heresy in the eyes of these official
authorities; you have no right to believe in cer-
tain things, society condemns them,and we, the
people of the United States, will not submit to
it. Then men and women are not to have the
right of conscience? This is a free country in-
deed! The people of the United States are out-
raged by certain forms of marriage! Are not
the people of Utah the people of the United
States, a part of the body politic? One would
think not to hear these officials talk! Is not
every citizen of the United States entitled to
privileges of freedom and religious liberty, and
is any man or woman to be condemned, without
due process of law, to any punishment whatso-
ever?
	The laws of the United States so read, and we
have ever been so taught and we so understand
it, but the laws are changed when Mormons are
accused; anything is fair on the side of men who
administer the law, any testimony may be
taken as evidence, or no testimony at all accord-
ing to the circumstances, and it requires no
proof whatever to deprive a man or woman of
the right of franchise. Men and women are
punished without any evidence being brought of
the alleged crime, test oaths are applied to the
people in regard to their religion, actual religi-
ous rites and ceremonies, and if they will not
subscribe to them they are powerless as to any
claim upon their country. They are outcasts in
its broadest sense. They have no rights or
claims that the country will acknowledge, and
men and women, who are by every human right,
sacred or divine, entitled to the blessings be-
queathed, by that glorious instrument of free-
dom, the Constitution, are being deprived, little
by little, of every vestige of liberty, religious or
political.
	Every American citizen, whether native born
or naturalized, points with pride and confidence
to the Constitution, knowing its grand and sub-
lime principles and the religious liberty it guaran-
tees as a safeguard and protection. But it is

[Column 2]

being torn to shreds, figuratively speaking, by
members of Congress who pass unconstitutional
laws, and the executive and judiciary officials
who administer them,while they boast so vigor-
ously at the same time about having the laws
obeyed, and are such sticklers for morality that
their pure minds are exercised because more
wives than one live in the marriage relation
with one husband, and are protected, cared for
and supported, their children acknowledged, and
withal have marital rights and privileges. Is
the way of the world so much higher morally
that they can very well afford to boast of their
refined civilization.
	To be sure it is so widely different from the
order of plural marriage that comparisons are
odious, and should never at any time be allowed;
but in view of these very punctilious people
feeling outraged by the practice of plural mar-
riage away off in the heart of the American
derert, let us pause and consider the misery and
degradation in the City of Washington alone,
where these one-sided laws are enacted. We
give the testimony of a lady who is a resident of
that City, and has been for many years, who
moves in the best society, who was an intimate
friend of President Garfield, and in fact knows
Washington by heart, so to speak. Hear what
she says:
	"What is the danger that threatens our fire-
sides to-day? An invasion from Spain, or France
or England? An insult to our flag upon the
high seas? Who that has a son or a daughter
does not know that the moral pestilence that
walks abroad at noonday and poisons the air of
night is a more dangerous foe to his household
than hosts of armed men?
	"Gentlemen of the House of Congress have
asked me if I did not know that gathering in
these girls from the streets, even though we se-
cure their reformation, would only make room
for more; that this evil is a natural and inevit-
able necessity. May I ask in whose interest
this argument is used? Is it for our own sons
that this fearful supply must and will be de-
manded? Or is it for the sons of our neighbors?
Surely it is no the intention of the lawmakers,
by tacit consent, to deliberately provide for the
ruin and degradation of their own families. It
is folly to suppose that young and ignorant girls
and children can be bought and sold upon our
streets for the worst purposes, and your own
households only can be safe and high and pure.
*	*	*	It is not purely a local evil
for which the citizens only are responsible. In
the summer and autumn,when permanent resi-
dents only are here, this class of girls find
themselves on the verge of starvation, and large
numbers leave the city. But when Congress
assembles, the immense foreign and floating
population, that ever gathers around the capital
of a great nation, creates a demand for them,
and the keepers of the houses send out their
emissaries for new girls. The vagrant girls of
Washington are peculiarly national and not lo-
cal vagrants. Many of them are brought here
young and ignorant, from distant homes by em-
ployees of the Government. Large numbers
come in search of employment, and find, as the
twenty thousand war widows in New York
found, that there is but one employment for
women that men are willing to pay for."
	This is but a sentence or two from the strong
and able argument of this eminent lady in be-
half of a "Girl's Refore School." To read the
argument in full is enough to make one's blood
run cold, and she gives facts,for as she very apt-
ly says, "One fact is worth volumes of argu-
ment."
	But these people do not see the beam that is
in their own eye, but observe with alarm the
little mote they fancy they can see afar off in
Utah. And is it not true that in this very city,

[Column 3]

where men are employed to pry into domestic
affairs, to obtrude themselves into the sacred
home circle, that they are men of vile habits,
steeped in tobacco and whisky, men whom every
virtuous woman despises, and whom she would
shun as she would a deadly serpent? Yet these
men, under pretence of finding whether the
Edmunds law is being broken, obtrude them-
selves into the sacred precincts of homes where
the commandments of God are kept, where
prayers are offered, and where peace and happi-
ness abide; what do they care for morality, or
purity, or chastity; they are after money; they
are the tools employed to condemn innocent
men and women, and if possible throw them
into prison, break up happy homes and make
them desolate, ruin the lives and break the
hearts of women who are as far above these exe-
cutors of the Edmunds law as the heavens are
above the earth.
	A pertinent question to ask at the present
time would be, who supports the palaces of sin
in our midst? Who patronizes the saloons? If
these questions were answered truthfully it
would be, the men who have come here to civil-
ize and regenerate the "Mormons," but who are
many of them "like ravenous wolves seeking
whom they may devour." The real object is
money and political influence, and any means
will be resorted to that can be made available to
make capital out of the "Mormon" question.
	The persecution raging now against this peo-
ple can be styled nothing less than a crusade
against religion for political purposes. What will
be gained by it to further the ends of office seek-
ers towards place, power, or aggrandizement can
better be told in the future, but it will most like-
ly prove more disastrous to them in the end
than to the people whom they now oppress; it
was so in Mo., it was so in Nauvoo, and in our
opinion it will prove the same in this instance.
"Mormonism" will flourish, the people will
prosper, and those who fight against it will fall
into the pit they have dug for this people, and
be caught in the snares they have taken so much
pains to set for those who are trying to keep the
commandments of God.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
	The pen sketch of Patty Session will be
continued in our next issue.
	MINUTES of Ladies' Semi-monthly, meeting,
held in this city, Feb. 7th, left over for lack of
space, will also appear in our next number.
	THE Secretaries of the several organizations in
this Stake of Zion will please remember that re-
ports must be handed in early in order that the
Stake secretaries of R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. and
P. A. may have ample time to prepare them for
the Stake Conference of this Stake of Zion,
which will be held previous to the sister's Con-
ference. The 5th of March is the latest date
given for them.
	The comic opera of Patience will be presented
in the Salt Lake Theatre, on Monday evening,
Feb. 23rd. The performers are all selected
from home talent, and, there is every prospect
that the performance will be excellent of its
kind. Prof. Krouse, a thoroughly educated
musician, who is getting up the affair, is spar-
ing no pains to make the event a perfect success.
The names of those who are to take part in the
opera are a pretty good guarantee of an enjoyable
musical treat and entertainment.
“Cherry Lips" is the title of a piece of music,
a grand valse, composed by Prof. A. Andle and
published by D[-]ynes & Coalter of this city, who
are really "very enterprising young men in a
musical direction. It Is one of a series of musi-
cal sketches, which will be suitable for the
guitar, banjo, piano, organ, violin, mandolin


z[-]ther and harp.		Prof. Andre has a happy facul-
ty of adaptation, as well as good taste in the com-
position of his pieces for his scholars, and as
they are not diffi ult thay will no doubt have a
wide circulation in the home market.
	THE Stake Secretaries in the various Stakes of
Zion will please govern themselves according to
the instructions previously given, that all re.
ports for the Central Board may be in before the
5th of March. The Relief Society, the Young
Ladies' Associations and the Primary are all
requested to send their reports to the secretary
of Central Board. Relief Society, Sarah M.
Kimball; Box 629, S. L. City. Y. L. M I. A ,
Louie M. Wells, Box 555. S. L. City. Primary
Associations, Alice A. Jenkins, S	   L. City.
Please be prompt in sending, that a complete
and correct report may be made for the April
Conference.
	The conflicting reports and rumors in regard
to the condition, of the English troops in Egypt
render it impossible to decide whether or not
Gen. Gordon has been killed, or indeed make It
somewhat doubtful whether Khartoum has real-
ly fallen. However, it is true that the brave
and gallant Earle, and also Leiut. Col. Eyre and
Col. Coventry have been killed. The telegrams
of Feb,12th, stale that the English success was
complete. More troops are to be sent to Egypt
and the struggle will continue. A council of
war was held at the War Office at noon on
Thursday, Feb. 12th. The Duke of Cambridge
will review the troops before they leave for
Egypt. The outlook is not cheery for the
English when their best officers and many of
their soldiers must be sacrificed; but the gov-
ernment seems quite willing to risk them, and
such are the fortunes of war.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

O, JUSTICE! WHITHER HAST THOU
FLED?

	Let our eye look upon Zion. Let her be defiled.
	But they knew not the thoughts of the Lord.—Micah
IV, 11,12.
	Let us enter the private sanctuary of domes-
tic life, where, to the honor of this great re-
public, the divinity of the marriage tie is ac-
knowledged and held sacred; and where purity,
the crowning glory of the social circle, is bravely
and successfully protected.
	Let us there, with wanton cruelty, defy the
Constitution of our country, and by trampling
on the rights of conscience, sever the holy ties
of wedlock—separate husband and wife—pa-
rents and children, and ignore the finest affec-
tions of the human heart.
	Yes; let us cause thousands of honorable, lov-
ing wives to be stigmatized as prostitutes, and
their offspring, as bastards.
	Let us cause multitudes of innocent children,
that are now being tenderly cherished and care-
fully educated, to be branded with infamy, and
deprived of heirship.
	Let us desecrate their homes, and exterminate
the only people of whom this nation can truly
boast as protectors of purity and innocence; lest
their virtuous and honorable example shall, in
the present reign of corruption, rise up before us
as a burning reproof.
	Let us immure in loathsome prisons those
brave men, who, for the sake of worshiping God
according to the dictates of their own con-
sciences, left their homes and the graves of their
noble ancestors, and sought refuge in the sterile
American Desert; where, nerved by the power
of faith in the arm of the Great Jehovah, for a
while they battled with the elements for life,
and at length, with indomitable energy, over-
came the barrenness of the soil, and made the
"Deseret blossom as the rose."
	Those are the men, whe, with their stalwart

[Column 2]

sons, the noble offspring of plural, celestial
marriage, with stern, unyielding persevarance,
establishe a connecting link between the com-
mercial cities of the East, and the rich mining
districts of the West, and made practicable for
the nation, the continental transit of the "iron
horse."
	Let us erace from the book of remembrance
the countless deeds of hospitality and generosi-
ty bestowed by those early settlers of the wilder-
ness, on our emigrants,when their supplies were
exhausted, as they were wending their way
through to California.
	Let us plant the seeds of devastation in a
thriving, peaceful, honest, industrious commun-
ity—a Territory brought into existence and
made to flourish, without aid, protection, or en-
couragement from the government under which
it exists.
	Yes. let us abregate the rights of its founders,
insomuch that henceforth it shall be controlled
by gamblers and speculators—those who have
no interest in common with the people.
	Instead of the Territory of Utah as it had be-
come—a theme of boast, as a nucleus of peace,
good order and happiness; let us, through our
crushing policy, exhibit it to the nations abroad
as a spectacle of confusion, desolation and 
woe.
	Let us tear away that mighty shield of the
rights of concience, and protector of the rights
of citizenship, our glorious Constitution—let us
place our veto on the commands of the Al-
mighty, and presume to me sure arms with the
Great Ruler of the Universe.
	Thus saith the President, and thus saith the
Congress of the United States of America, by
passing unconstitutional bills, and in appointing
and forcing upon Utah a Commission composed
of men with undeveloped consciences, who as-
sume preogatives, powers,and authority beyond
their investment: who having inagurated an
obnoxious compulsory test oath to screen the
lecherous and punish the pure, complacently
smile when the inalienable rights of local citi-
zens are trampled upon, and the judicial sanc-
tum, which should be an emporium of justice
and equity, is transformed into a nest of conspira-
tors—an equisitorial caldron.
		E. R. SNOW SMITH.
	Salt Lake City, Feb. 7, 1885.


[Column 1]
﻿		A VIEW- FEBRUARY 18S5.
	It is interesting to note the different phases
presented at different periods of the world’s
history by those powers which have ever been
opposed to the truth. Alike in those dispensa-
tions in which Moses and Jesus were to figure
prominently, the Evil Powers seem to take into
consideration beforehand the necessity of doing
something to prevent the rise of such mighty
one, in the interest of that cause against which
they fought.
	They therefore “put it into the hearts of
wicked men" to have the lives of infants taken
which should be born in the times and places in
which they supposed those mighty ones were to
come forth. But notwithstanding all the great
crimes committed in the shedding of innocent
blood at those times the plans of the Almighty
were not frustrated. The very children whose
lives were sought being safely guarded and kept
alive. The Evil Powers were foiled and the
powers of light were triumphant.
	In this, “The dispensation of the Fulness of
Times,” the evil powers seem to take a some-
what different stand, though evidently working
with the same object in view as in former times.
It appears now that their business is to ‘put it
into the hearts of wicked men” to frame and
seek to enforce laws to the effect that children

[Column 2]
shall not be born. The world does not want
them, and the few people who do want them
must not have them if their enemies can pre-
vent it.
	This latter scheme of the Evil Powers is with-
out doubt concocted in consequence of a knowl-
edge which they must have obtained of the fact
that within a limited space of time, all the re-
maining spirits which belong to this earth, and
are to receive earthly tabernacles, wi1 be born
of the flesh. That among them are many mas-
ter spirits, which have been reserved for the
great work of the latterdays. And that as soon
as these spirits have received their fleshly taber-
nacles, and are brought forth, “The end of the
world, which is the destruction of the wicked,"
will c[-]me.
	Is it any wonder that the Evil Powers are
striving so madly and so vigorously at this time
to do something to prevent the birth of pure
spirits in pure bodie? What else should they
be striving for, knowing, as they do, that “their
time is short?” Only to be faithful and diligent
remains for those who are looking forward to the
time when the evil powers will be bound, and

		JESUS SHALL REIGN.
Oh! the vain glory and wild speculation
	Which we now witness on every hand!
Oh! the dread doom of this proud generation!
	Why will they perish! Why not understand?
		Keep in the narrow path,
		And thus escape the wrath
Which is now sweeping the nations amain;
		Stand with the meek and pure,
		 They only can endure,		"
		And righteous crowns secure;
			Jesus shall reign!

Th’ trumpets are sounding, th’ seal a being broken;
	The plagues are abroad of destruction and dearth;
The wicked repent not; they heed not the token,
	And soon they’ll be swept from the face of the earth.
		Cease not, ye saints, to call,
		Save, or we perish all!
Help us, our Father, Thy laws to maintain;
		Oh! God of truth and light,
		Strengthen us by Thy might,
		Thine is the power, the right;
			Jesus shall reign!

Soon will the morn of the first resurrection
	Gladden the valleys and brighten the waves;
Parents and children, the friends and connections,
	Sweetly embrace as they rise from their graves.
		Father, our hearts prepare,
		In the great bliss to share,
More than requiting all sorrow and pain;
		Joyous the anthem then,
		Angels and saints again
		Echo the glad amen,
			Jesus shall reign!			LULA.
	S. L. City.


A DELIGHTFUL PARTY.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On Thursday last Sister Julia Howe gave a
most enjoyable entertainment to the widows of
the 17th Ward.  Her parlors were thrown open
for their reception, and two long tables were set
in the dining-room, on which were placed the
substantials of the season, with many delicacies
abundantly garnishing the feast. Dinner was
served at four o'clock and the Sisters Reese,
Alder, Keetie Kimball and Miss Zina Hyde,
were the ministering spirits attendant upon the
tables. They greatly assisted, too, in the order
and satisfaction that every one preserved and
felt.
	Sister Hyde presided by appointment over the
assembly, and by her ever kind, affectionate, and
unassuming manners made all feel at home.
	After dinner, all adjourned to the large and
beautiful drawing room, where, after a time, the
guests were called upon to express their senti-
ments upon such subjects as they chose to


speak upon. Many arose and spoke in an inter-
esting and edifying manner of their experience
in life, of their happy associations and enjoy-
ments in social gatherings, of journeying across
the plains, etc. Songs and recitations were also
engaged in.
	All expressed their appreciation of Sister
Howe's kindness and benevolence in providing
search an entertainment for them, and called
down blessings upon her.
	At ten o'clock all returned to their homes re-
freshed and strengthened by "the communion
of Saints"
	Our Sister, in speaking, said President Taylor
o served at the last Conference that from that
time the widows should be blessed, and she be-
lieved that in this meeting that prophecy was
being realized.
				ONE OF THE WIDOWS.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PLEASANT TIME.
	On Monday, January 26th ult., a very enjoy-
able time was spent at the residence of Sister
Ware’s. Many sisters of the 19th and other
wards met together to celebrate her 79th birth-
day, it having been the custom to do so on that
occasion for the past ten years. The time spent
there together was profitable and instructive.
	The afternoon was spent in singing, prayer
and speaking of the goodness of God to His peo-
ple in days that are past and up to the present
time, when all bore a faithful testimony to the
truth of the Gospel. It was a time long to be
remembered.
	After the meeting was over all sat down to a
bountiful repast. The picnic was brought by
the sisters.
	I will just say I wrote a poem, the words of
which past through my mind the previous
evening, and I penned them down as they came
to me, and read them at the party.
						MARY CLARK.


R. S. AND Y. L. MEETING.

	The Ladies Semi-monthly meeting was held
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly rooms,
Satuaday, January 27, Mrs. M. I. Horne presid-
ing. Singing, "Lord, Thou wilt hear me when
I pray." Prayer by Counselor Phebe Woodruff.
Singing, "Glory to Thee, my God." Minutes of
previous meeting read and approved. Minutes
of Y. L. Associations from 20th 6th and 17th
wards, also minutes of P. A. from the 5th, 4th,
15th, 11th, 17th and 10th wards were read by
the reep[-]ctive secretaries.
	Prest. Horne then addressed the sisters.
Spoke of tha cultivation of our young people,
and thought we should all seek to learn those
things that would be for our best good and ad-
vancement. Said, we are living in a time that
requires us to be wise and humble, and when
we must live near to God and do His holy will,
if we would expect His blessings. The time
has come that our Prophet Joseph Smith spoke
of, when all the world is turned against us. Let
us try to be faithful and merit the Lord's pro-
tecting care, for we know not what may come
to us or ours. Let us also remember our breth-
ren who are being so tried, and offer up our
prayers in their behalf.
	Coun. Woodruff exhorted the sisters to be
faithful in the performance of every duty rest-
ing upon them. Wished those present to im-
prove the time in bearing testimony.
	Several of the sisters bore excellent testi-
monies.
	Sister Mc. Master spoke in the gift of tongues,
and Sister Lawson gave the interpretation.
Prest. Horne made a few closing remarks, in
which she gave a brief account of the trials the


Saints had to pass through in Missouri, and said
the Lord protected us then, and if we are faith-
ful He will protect us now.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Singing,
Doxology. Benediction by Sister E. S. Tay-
lor.


NOTES AND NEWS.
	Washington, 25.—Rev. Dr. Patten, Presi-
dent of Howard University, preached a sermon
in the Congregational Church of this city to-
day, on woman and skepticism, in the course
of which he spoke of the woman’s suffrage
convention held here recently, and expressed
the opinion that when women are given too
much liberty, they branch off into skepticism
and immorality. He said, among other things,
that the lives of such women as George Elliot,
Madame Roland and Harriet Martineau exem-
plified the truth of this assertion, and he re-
ferred to Victoria Woodhull as a representative
of the woman suffrage movement. Among his
audience were Miss Susan B. Anthony and
Mrs. Elizabeth Cady Stanton, and at the con-
clusion of the services they walked to the plat-
form and upbraided Dr. Patten for his utter-
ances. Miss Anthony said to him that if his
mother were living she would take him across
her knee and spank him, but Mrs Stanton in- 
terrupted her and said: "On the contrary, let
me congratulate Dr. patten. I've been trying
for years to make women understand the worst
enemies they have are in the pulpit. Now he
has illustrated it beyond question." Without
giving Dr. Patten time to reply the women has
tily left the church.
	To-night the Rev. Olpmpia Brown replied 
to Dr. Patten from the pulpit of the Univer-
salist Church.


WASHINGTON CONVENTION.
	The evening session  of Tuesday brought out
a larger attendance and there were several
members of Congress present. Matilda J.
Gage delivered an address on the political par-
ties of the country, showing they had been suc-
cessful or had failed according to their strict
adherence to or desertion of principle.
	Mrs. Stanton followed,speaking on "The
Disabilities and Limitations of the sex." She
glorified motherhood as the divinest function;
referred to Horace Greeley's limitation of
woman's usefulness, in which he said she could
neither navigate a ship across the sea, nor sing
bass in a choir, by stating that woman had
done both. She laid at the doctrine of celi-
bacy endless evils and ills, and asserted that
the religious devotees of the moral ages were
responsible for the darkness of that age. She
criticised St. Paul's strictures on marriage,and
condemned the practice and principle of celi-
bacy in pungent and unmeasured terms.
	Mrs. Laura de Force Gordon was the last
speaker. She gave a history of the work in
California together with an interesting account
of the contest over the admission of women to
practice at the bar. Women are now admitted
to all callings and professions in California, sex
being declared no disqualification by the Con-
stitution, by a clause which Mrs. Gordon had
herself drafted.
		SECOND DAY.
	The meeting was called to order by Miss An-
thony. She told her hearers not to be discour-
aged. There were but four bills in the Senate
ahead of the suffrage bill and that already the
friends of the measure were preparing their
speeches on it. She said if women were half
as sagacious as men they would note down
points in behalf of the bill and send them to
one of the senators favoring the measure, that
he might incorporate them in his speech.
	Dispatches were read from Helen M. Gou
gar and Mrs. Lockwood, and letters from Clara
Barton, Robert Purvis and Rachel Foster.
	Mrs. Perkins of Ohio then read a most able
and forcible address, quaint, touching and tell-
ing, showing full womanly and matronly in-
stincts, an unfaltering trust in the Creator, an
abhorence of intemperance and a resolute and
immovable conviction that the cause of woman
was just and should be secured.  “There had
been too many Jane Carlyles in the past,sitting
up all night to make bread  for a grumbling
husband. Victor Hugo had said the 19th cen-
tury belonged to women, and he, rather
than the bearish English philospher, should
be taken as the true representative of his
age."
	Caroline Gilkey Rogers then read a well-
prepared and interesting paper on the “Aris-
tocracy of Sex." In the course of her address
she gave a description of a speech made by
Old Sojourner Truth in a suffrage convention
years ago, by which the entire current of
thought and feeling was changed. "If de fust
woman that God eber made was strong ’nuff to
turn the worl" upside down," said the tall and
powerful ex-slave, "den dese heah women on
de platform oughter be able to set her right
agin."
	Miss Burr read an allegorical essay showing
the condition of a world where the distinction
of sex in the walks of life had been oblitera-
ted and that this world was surpassingly and 
painfully inferior.
		RESOLUTIONS.
	"Whereas a bill for an amendment to the fed-
eral constitution prohibiting the states from
disfranchising citizens on account of sex is now,
with reports for and against its passage, before
the Congress of the United States; therefore,
	Resolved, That we request our representa-
tives in congress to pass this bill, and submit it
to the legislatures of the several states.
	Whereas it is conceded to be the duty of
overnments to protect the persons and proper-
ty of their citizens against all aggressions by
land and sea; therefore,
	Resolved, That it is clearly the duty of the
United States, on this principle, to protect the
women of the several states, when robbed by
tyrannical custom or unjustlegislation of their
fundamental rights by extending to them the
rights of suffrage.
	Resolved,  That in the death of the Hon.
Henry Fawcett, Wendall Phillips, Senator An-
thony, Madam Mathilde Annekk, Frances D.
Gage, Sarah Pugh, Elizabeth T. Schenck,
Katherine Newell Doggett, Laura Giddings
Julian, Judge Folger, Bishop Simpson, and
William Heney Channing, the year 1884 has
been one of irreparable losses to our move-
ment, and as an association we would express
to their families and friends in England and
America our deep sympathy in their affliction
and our gratitude for their life long services for
the elevation of women.
	Resolved, That we note with great satisfac-
tion the growth of sentiment favorable to
woman suffrage in the W. C. T. U., and that
we thank them heartily for their recent memo-
rial to congress in behalf of the sixteenth
amendment.
	Whereas the dogmas incorporated in relig-
ious creeds derived from Judaism, teaching
that woman was an afterthought in creation,
her sex a misfortune, marriage a condition of
subordination, and maternity a curse, are con-


[Column 1]

trary to the law of God as revealed in nature
and the precepts of Christ; and
	Whereas these dogmas are an insidious poi-
son sapping the vitality of our civilization,
blighting woman, and laying their palsying
hand upon humanity; therefore,
	Resolved, That we denounce these dogmas
wherever they are enunciated, and we will
withdraw our personal support from any or-
ganization or person so holding and teaching,
and
	Resolved, That we call upon the Christian
ministry, as leaders of thought, to teach and
enforce the fundamental idea of creation, that
man was made in the image of God, male and
female, and, given equal dominion over the
earth, but none over each other. And fur-
ther, we invite their co-operation securing
the recognition of the cardinal point of our
creed that in true religion there is neither
male nor female, neither bond nor free, but all
are one."
	These resolutions, introduced the first day,
were read and adopted, save the last one, which
created an unusual sensation. Men and women
joined in the debate and the discussion took a
partly religious turn and threatened to result
disastrously. It was discussed pro and con
vigorously and was beginning to call forth
criminating attack upon special religious or-
ders, when Mrs. Stanton arose and spoke ear-
nestly, and conclusively in its behalf, and then
said further consideration would be postponed
till the morning following when a special ses-
sion would be held to consider it. The after-
noon meeting then adjourned.
	The evening session was devoted to ad-
dresses. Miss Hardman of Philadephia spoke
on the subject, "As the Rulers, so the People,"
stating that the only way to overcome mob
rule was to substitute virtue for vice, to give
the more moral half of the community—the
women—representation, and the result would
be attained. Women for the same work shoud
receive the same compensation, and yet the
only place where men did not cheat the women
was where women had the right to vote.
	Rev. Olympia Brown,for 20 years a preacher
in the Universalist Church, spoke to the text,
"All are created free and equal." The aris-
tocracy in power, she said, was that of the
moustache, whose insignia was the spittoon,
whose home was the smoking car, and whose
outward sign was the curling tobacco smoke,
and which was dependent upon an accident of
the body. 
	The concluding speaker of the evening was
Lillie-Devereux Blake. Her subject was the
"True Republic." She said the term "repub-
lic." was not fulfilled in this country. A young
woman here who desired to earn her living had
offered to her the alternative of starvation or
degradation. She hoped the Bartholdi statue
would never be erected until women had the
right to vote. 
	It was decided not to hold a morning session
to discuss the resolutions which created such a
tumult during the afternoon.

THIRD DAY.
	A fair sized audience assembled on the after-
noon of the third day. Miss Anthony again
called the meeting to order, calling attention
to the necessity of liberal contributions to ena-
ble the work successfully to be carried on, and
she was followed by a male member named E.
M. Davis, who made himself conspicuous all
through the convention for very bad taste. He
made a very painful attempt to coax and argue
those present into helping "us" in "our" work.
	Dr. Harriet B. Chapin, in a wavering voice,
reported the condition of the suffrage work at
Albany, New York.
	Mrs. Shattuck of Boston, a very womanly 
appearing and acting lady, reported the condi-
tion of the woman's movement in Massachu-

[Column 2]

setts. She read some very well compiled and
suggestive statistics, showing the virtue of
women in her state and the portion her sex
plays in the industrial and professional pursuits
of that State. Mrs. Shattuck is a very pleas-
ing, broad and reflective speaker, and must
prove a power in the cause because of her ap-
parent earnestness and conservativeness.
	Anna L. Diggs read a well-written, telling
and humorous account of the suffrage work in
Kansas. She was very attentively listened to.
	One of the most eloquent and powerful ap-
peals and addresses made was that of Mrs.
Clara B. Colby, Editor of the Woman's Tri-
bune, of Nebraska. She appealed to men to
make the cause of woman's emancipation their
own, as they had the cause of free government
in the early history of the nation, as they had
for the abolition of slavery. She told the early
Hebraic legend of creation: "Lillith was the
goddess and Adam the God; that they reigned
over all equally till Adam wished all control,
and Lillith, rather than npt enjoy peace, left
the garden. Then an Eve, subordinate and a
helpmeet only was created," and it was a fact
worthy of note that until this inferior and sub-
ordinate woman was made, no evil came into
the world. She wished Lillith to return, to
reign equal, and thus bring back the era when
sin should be unknown.
	Mrs. D. Stockman of Chicago made a report
for Illinois, while Rev. Olympia Brown report-
ed the movement in Wisconsin so active that
they had held two annual meetings within the
year in that State.
	The afternoon session closed by Mrs. Stan-
ton stating that it had been decided to lay the
last resolution over for one year, as the discus-
sion seemed to have given rise to some feeling;
but she wished women to read the Scriptu es
and take notes, so as to determine intelligently
whether there was not a grave discrepancy be-
tween the position assigned them by the minis-
ters and creeds of orthodox churches, and that
contemplated in the first two chapters of Gene-
sis and in the Gospel of Christ.
	In the evenng Miss Anthony made an ad-
dress, hitting hard those who were opposing
the work in Congress, while Mrs. Stanton
stated that in the forthcoming volume of the
"History of Woman Suffrage" she would paint
these men so black that their own sons and
daughters would be ashamed of them. Miss
Anthony gave some interesting points in her
early efforts in behalf of the cause. She would
not tell just now how many years ago that was
—but if she lived to be as old as Methusalem,
"whose oldest daughter I am said to be, I will
still advocate woman suffrage."
	Mrs. Laura deForce Gordon then delivered
an address on "Is our Civilization Civilized?"
Ar uing like a lawyer, as she is, decidedly in
the negative. The loss of courtesy by men
would be more than compensated by the fact
that she, would then be the equal of man. She
said she would rather suffer any amount of in-
dignity than that little jackanapes should be
able to insult her with the remark, "you can't
vote."
	Mrs. Stanton read a short closing address. It
was very important that the government should
be kept secular, and equally so that woman
should know the danger of ecclesiastical con-
trol, as they were so soon going to vote. She
wanted women to remember that under the
old Roman law women had far more liberty
than since the Canon law put its withering
hands upon their civil rights. Law and eccle-
siastical students read the degrading statements
and then preached or practiced on the inspir-
ation of the directions given to the Corinthians
or Phillipians. She demanded an exporgated
edition of Blackstone and the Canon laws, as
well as of the Bible. All the revisions of men
had been failures, and she wanted the next to 
be made by women.

[Column 3]

	The convention was then declared ad-
journed.

NOTES.
	During the debate on Wednesday on the
anti-church doctrine resolution, Mrs. Stanton
in her remarks tending to show that organized
religion had ever tried to keep woman in abase-
ment, asked what it was that bound the Hin-
doo woman on the funereal pyre of her dead
husband? "Religion! Religion it is that keeps
the harems of Turkey full; and what but re-
ligion could ever induce the Mormon women
to endure that abominable and destroying prac-
tice of polygamy?"
	The character of ladies who participated and
who were present was intellectual, at least in
appearance, if not in fact. There were many
young women present. There were not a
great number of men, but the number of males
increased each day. Mrs. John T. Caine and
Mrs. F. S. Richards of Utah were interested
spectators and listeners to a large part of the
proceedings.
	Several of the ladies who are attending the
convention have never attended the Washing-
ton convention before. Mrs. Gordon of Cali-
fornia makes her appearance here now for the
first time. Br. Chapin of Albany, Mrs. Fray
of Ohio, Miss Burr of Hartford, Mrs. Colby of
Nebraska and Mrs. Ellis of New York are also
here for the first time. Mrs. Halleck and Mrs.
Hull are Quakers, and there were several
other ladies from Maryland belonging to the
Society of Friends present to-day. Miss An-
thony is of Quaker ancestry; Mrs. Colby of
Nebraska is editor of a paper; Miss. Laura
White of Kentucky is sister of Representative
White of that state. She is a student of arch-
itecture, and proposes to make architecture her
profession. Mrs. Post of Wyoming is the wife
of Delegate Post. She has been the foreman
of a jury and served on a jury when she was
the only woman present.

EXECUTIVE SESSION.
	The first executive session of officers and
members was held in the red parlors of the
Riggs house on Tuesday morning, Miss An-
thony presiding. The unfinished business of
last year was considered and committees ap-
pointed for the work of the convention. Thir-
teen states were represented, one territory, and
the District of Columbia. The following are
the committees appointed:

morning the Woman Suffrage Association elec-
ted the following officers: President, Elizabeth
Cady Stanton; vice-presidents at large, Susan
B. Anthony,Matilda Joslyn Gage, Rev. Olym-
pia Brown, Phoebe W. Cousens, Abigail Scott
Duniway; chairman of the executive commit-
tee, May Wright Sewall; recording secretaries,
Ellen H. Sheldon, Washington ;Eliza T. Ward,
Washington; Pearl Adams,Illinois; correspond-
ing secretary, Rachel G. Foster, Philadelphia;
foreign corresponding secretaries, England,
Caroline A. Page, London; Lydia E. Becker,
Manchester; France, Mme. Marguerrette Ber-
ry Stanton; M'lle Hubertine, Anclert, and
Charlotte B. Wilbur, Paris; Germany, Clara
Neyman, Berlin; treasurer, Jane H. Spofford,
Washington; auditors, Ruth C. Dennison and
Julia A. Wilbur, of this city. In addition to
these, honorary and regular vice-presidents and
members of the executive committee were
chosen to represent the different states and ter-
ritories. The honorary vice presidents for this
District are Rosina M. Parnell, Susan Edson,
M. D., and Ruth C. Dennison, the regular lo-
cal vice president being Ellen M. O'Conner.
The chairman of the executive committee is
May Wright Sewall, and the local members
Belva A. Lockwood and Caroline B. Winslow.
	The chairman of the executive committee
submitted a number of recommendations and
suggestions, including a proposition for the es-
tablishment of a national headquarters and a
paper to be the organ of the association; and
also the holding of conventions in the different
states. Mrs. H. R. Shattuck, of Massachusetts,
reported a plan for a basis of delegate repre-
sentation.		WANDERER.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


CORRESPONDENCE.

	SNOWVILLE, Box Elder Co., Utah.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	As I have never read anything in your val-
uable columns about Snowville (which I will
state for explanation is named in honor of our
beloved Apostle Lorenzo Snow, and not be-
cause we have any more snow than you do in
Salt Lake City), I thought I would drop you a
few lines concerning our ward. We are, the
most of us, striving to be Latter-day Saints.
Our Relief Society is in full force, with Sister
Mary Goodliffe as Prest. They look after the
poor in purse and spirit, and the sick also;
they have lots of wheat on hand, in fact the
Bishop says they are the richest people in the
ward, and if you want anything done without
fail, call on the Relief Society, and it is done.
	The Y. M. and Y. L. M. I. A. are in a most
flourishing condition; they meet conjointly
every Sunday evening. This is the Bishop's
plan to keep the young folks together, and a
most admirable and successful one it has


proved. Their meetings are conducted similar
to those in Salt Lake City; every one, without
exception, tries to do the best he can when
called upon. I have never yet heard but one
person ask to be excused when called upon to
speak.
	The Primary Association is not behind, but
is doing its best. The Presidency, M. A. Green,
E. Ballam and S. Hurd. are alive to their du-
ties and interested in the work, striving to do
all they can to teach, instruct and interest the
children. Truly the Primary work is a great
and glorious one.
	We have a nice Sunday School, which would
compare favorably with any, I think. Of
course we have many obstacles and drawbacks
which is consistent and common to all young
settlements.
	Last, but not least, we have a Bishop (A.
Goodliffe) and counsel that are up to the mark.
They are awake and doing. We also have
that element known as "Liberalism," which is
encouraging, though trying, and a cause many
times of sorrow to the true Latter-day Saint;
but the Lord has said that there must needs be
opposition, it is necessary to taste the bitter to
know and appreciate the sweet, and where the
work of God is, there is opposition, or Satan's
work, also.
	Before closing I will tell you about a sur-
prise gotten up on the Prest. of the Y. L. M.
I. A., Sister E. E. Goodliffe, it was her birth-
day and a few relatives, etc., had been invited 
to supper, when lo and behold! here comes a
whole party and literally stormed the place,
taking our beloved President at a decided dis-
advantage. It was indeed a most enjoyable
affair. There were speeches, in which one and
all bore testimony to the goodness and worth
of Sister Goodliffe, songs, dancing and lots of
pies and cakes, etc.
	Your paper is a welcome guest in our homes
and we say long life to "Aunt Em" and her
able staff and God bless you.
	Secretary.


[Column 1]
	All the subscribers of the WOMAN'S EXPO-
NENT in the city will please send their address,
name, street and number, to this Office, in or-
der to have their papers delivered by the mail
carriers.

	The Referee, an illustrated comic paper, has
been started in this city. It is always full of
smart and funny things, at least the three
copies we have received were. The editors are
Messrs. J. Rich and S. Kenner, both celebrated
for their originality and sarcasms, puns, etc.
If the Referee does not succeed, it will not be
for lack of talented and witty editors.
