000 in taxes, and exported more than $7,500,-
000 worth of beer.
	George C. Miln, after being in turn a clergy-
man and an actor, is about to become a lectur-
er. Mr. Miln would make an admirable front-
ispiece for the "Descent of Man."—New York
Graphic.
	Life is warfare, and those who climb up and
down steep paths and go through dangerous
enterprises are the brave men and the leaders
in the camp, but to rest basely at the cost of
others' labors is to be a coward, safe because
despised.—Seneca.
	Sometimes the "heaviest wheat of all" may
spring up from seeds dropped in an accidental
way. What a motive to the maintenance of
personal holiness! The accidental is a shadow
of the intentional. Influence is the exhalation
of character.—W. M. Taylor.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

VOL. 14 13	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 1, 1885	No. 19.


MUSIC! MUCH I OWE TO THEE!

Minstrell of an art divine—
God has blest that Soul of thine
With a gift directly given
From the chancery of Heaven!
Mode and method may be taught,
But the spirit must be fraught
With the affatus all divine,
To inspire, inflate—and shine
Into every craving Soul,
And o'er it throws a charm'd control,
That lifts the list'ning ear entranced
Into a region all enhanced
By whisp'rings, language cannot tell,
But which the co-mate knows so well.

Hug this gift with miser's care,
Gift exalting, gift so rare!
Pray the Giver to inspire
All thy Soul with holy fire;
Ask Him to direct thy hand
To strike the chords that shall command
Holy feeling—rapturous joy,
Crushing out life's base alloy.

Thy gift shall be a costly gem,
Eternal in thy Diadem;
Calm and chasten'd mayst thou be,
Walking in humility,
Walking in the blessed light
Which thine art shall o'er ignite;
Grateful to thy God—that He
A gift divine has sent to thee,
Hug it with a miser care—
Gift exalting—gift so rare!

	HANNAH T. KING.
Salt Lake City, 1885.


BETWEEN THE YEARS.

Time's river flows without a break or bridge,
	The moments run to days, the days to years:
Strange how we pause on the dividing ridge,
	Which 'twixt Old Year and New our fancy rears!

With backward survey o'er the dark "has been,"
	With forward gaze into the dark "to be."
Summing the good and ill that we have seen,
	As if God's purposes stood plain to see—

As if 'twere man's to reach heaven's far off ends;
	To reckon up Time's harvest in the seed;
To write off gains of good and ill's amends—
	The balance of their books as traders read.

As thick a fold between us and the past,
	As e'er between us and the future lies:
The ills we grieve for may work good at last:
	Out of our seeming good what ills may rise!

Only one thing we know, that over all
	A wise and loving power holds sovereign sway:
This knowing, let us stand between the years,
	Bent but to do the duty of the day;

Speaking the truth and holding to the right,
	As we the truth can reach, the right can read;
Trusting the hand that steers, through dark and light;
	By His lode-star, not ours, to ends decreed.

Between our larger and our lesser worlds,
	Of self, home, city, state, or continent,
There is no variance of far or near,
	Of great or small, in that Guide's measurement,

Twixt strokes of policy that hit or miss,
	And sleights of skill that make or mar a cause.
Then, grateful, take his gifts, his strokes, submiss,
	And look to man for rule, to Heaven for laws.

		PUNCH.


THE PAST, THE PRESENT AND
THE FUTURE.

DEAR FRIENDS:
	Thinking that a letter from this part of the
vineyard would be of interest to some of your
readers, if not all, I take the liberty of writ-
ing.
	While I reflect on the past, gaze on the
present and look through the gloom into the
future, it causes many serious reflections to
cross my mind. What do those throughts bring
to mind—does it bring always pleasure? I
think not. Is it all sorrow? No, not by any
means; but I think it is necessary for us to have
these trials; in order to make us think, and to
think of the past as well as the future, for in
looking back over our past life we see, very
often, where we have done wrong, and if we
have a heart that desires to do right, we will
make an effort to do so; and let us not think
because we have done wrong that we can-
not do anything that is right in the future, but
let us, each morning that we rise from our beds,
see if we cannot do a little more good that day
and a little less evil, so that when that future
comes, that seems so far and is yet so near,that
we may have some good laid up to balance
against the bad. We call ourselves Saints, but
I fear some of us are not aware of what per-
fect beings we ought to be; I think that we
should live so that the word saint will not be
wrongly applied, for we do not want to estab-
lish anything that is false. Let us try and live
the lives of Latter-day Saints, for we know not
the day nor the hour that we may be called to
depart from this life, and it will then be our
painful duty to give an account of the deeds
done in the body, and of what use we have
made of the time that our Heavenly Father
has granted us.
	If our time and means have been spent for
good and the advancement of His cause and
the comforting of his children it will be well
for us, if not it will be very painful indeed to
our souls. I trust that all who claim to be
Saints may be faithful to the end of all right-
eousness.
	We have a Relief Society in working order,
also a Y. M. and Y. L. Association in a good
condition. There is a Primary Association,
but it has not done much for the past year. I
think it is going to be enlivened up soon; I
think it is the greatest thing for the benefit of
the children that has ever yet been in our day,
when it is strictly attended.
	May the blessings of God rest upon the
young, that their minds may expand.
	K. E. D.


[Column 1]

AUTOBIOGRAPHY

OF EMILY D. P. YOUNG.

Continued.
	When I look back and remember the great
responsibility that rested upon my father as
first Bishop—his poverty and privations, and
the hardships that he had to endure, the accu-
sations of false brethren, the fault-finding of
the poor, and the persecutions of our enemies
—I do not wonder at his early death. And
when I remember his conversations with my
mother, and can now comprehend, in my ma-
ture years, his extreme weariness of soul, it

[Column 2]

brings to my mind a clause of his blessing,
which says, "Thou shalt stand in thy office un-
til thou shalt desire to resign it, that thou
mayest rest for a little season."
	I think it was the first fall of our stay in
Clay Co. that a slaughter yard was established
on the banks of the river not far from where
we lived: thousands of hogs were killed and
packed for the market, giving employment to
the Saints in that vicinity. The men killing
and cutting them up, and the women and chil-
dren cutting up and trying out the lard,having
a share of all they did, and in this way the
people were provided with meat and lard,which
was a great blessing at that time. I remember
of going with mother, and doing what I could
to help her, day after day.
	While we remained in Clay County the
brethren did all they could to regain possession
of their homes. They petitioned the Governor,
employed lawyers, and tried in various ways to
gain redress, but all their efforts proved to be of
no avail. The Prophet Joseph Smith manifest-
ed great anxiety concerning the Saints in Zion
He was constantly writing letters advising
them what to do, and sending words of com-
fort and encouragement.	Revelations were
given assuring the Saints that the Lord re-
membered them in their afflictions. A father
could not have manifested more love and anx-
iety if his best beloved son had been in deep
trouble, than the Prophet did in regard to the
Saints in Missouri and their persecutions.
	The following are extracts from one of the
letters that the Prophet sent to the brethren in
Zion: "I would inform you that it is not the
will of the Lord for you to sell your lands in
Zion, if means can possibly be procured with-
out. Every exertion should be made to main-
tain the cause you have espoused and to con-
tribute to the necessities of one another as
much as possible in this your great calamity;
and remember not to murmur at the dealings
of God with His creatures. You are not as
yet brought into as trying circumstances as
were the ancient Prophets and Apostles. Call
to mind Daniel, the three Hebrew children,
Jeremiah, Paul, Stephen, and many more too
numerous to mention, who were stoned, sawn
asunder, tempted, slain with the sword, and
wandered about in sheep' skins and goat's
skins, being destitute, afflicted, tormented, of
whom the world were not worthy. They wan-
dered in deserts and in mountains,and in dens,
and in caves of the earth; yet they all obtained
a good report through faith, and amidst all
their afflictions they rejoiced that they were
counted worthy to receive persecution for
Christ's sake. We know not what we shall
be called to pass through before Zion is deliv-
ered and established; therefore, we have great
need to live near to God and always be in
strict obedience to all His commandments,that
we may have a conscience void of offence
towards God and man. It is your privilege to
use every means in your power to seek redress
for your grievances of your enemies, and pros-
ecute them to the extent of the law; but it will
be impossible to render you any assistance in a
temporal point of view, as our means are
already exhausted, and we are deeply in debt,
and know of no means whereby we can extri-
cate ourselves. The inhabitants of this county
threaten our destruction and we know not how
soon they may be permitted to follow the ex-
ample of the Missourians. We learn by Elder
Phelp's letter that the brethren have surren-


[Column 1]

dered their arms to the enemy and are fleeing
across the river.  If that is the case, it is not
meet that they should recommence hostilities
with them[-] but if not, you should maintain the
ground as long as there is a man left, as the
spot of ground upon which you were located is
the place appointed of the Lord for your inher-
itances, and it was right in the sight of God,
that you contend for it to the last.
     "You will recollect that the Lord has said
that Zion should not be removed out of her
place;therefore, the land should not be sold,
but be held by the Saints until the Lord in His
wisdom opens the way for your return; and un-
til that time, if you can purchase a tract of
land in Clay County for the present emergen-
cies, it is right you should do so, if you can do
it and not sell your lands in  Jackson County.
It is not safe for us to send a written revelation
on the subject, but what is written above is ac-
cording to wisdom."
     The foregoing letter was written at Kirtland
on the 5th of Dec. 1833.  A letter was also
written dated Kirtland Mills, Ohio,Dec. 10th,
1833. 
	"Beloved brethren, E. Partridge, W. W.
Phelps, J. Corrill,J. Morley, and all the Saints
whom it may concern: This morning's mail
brought Bishop Partridge's, and Elders Corrill
and Phelps' letters, all mailed at Liberty, Nov[-]
19th, which gave us the melancholy intelli-
gence of your flight from the land of your in-
heritance, having been driven before the face
of your enemies in that place.  From previous
letters we learned that a number of our breth-
ren had been slain, but we could not learn from
those referred to above, as there had been but
one, and that was Bro. Barber, and Bro. Dib-
ble was wounded in the bowels.  We were
thankful to learn that no more had been slain,
and our daily prayers are that the Lord will
not suffer His Saints who have gone up to His
land to keep His commandments, to stain His
holy mountain with their blood.  I cannot
learn from any communication by the Spirit to
me that Zion has forfeited her claim to a Celes-
tial Crown, notwithstanding the Lord has
caused her to be thus afflicted, except it may
be some individuals who have walked in dis-
obedience and forsaken the new covenant; all
such will be made manifest by their works in
due time.
     "I have always expected that Zion would suf
fer some affliction, from what I could learn
from the commandments which have been
given.  But I would remind you of a clause in
one which says that after much tribulation
cometh the blessing. By this, and also others,
and also one received of late,I know that Zion,
in the own due time of the Lord, will be re-
deemed; but how many will be the days of her
purification, tribulation and affliction the Lord
has kept hid from my eyes;and when I inquire
concerning this subject the voice of the Lord
is, be still, and know that I am God:  All
those who suffer for my name sake shall reign
with me, and he that layeth down his life for
my sake shall find it again.
     "Now, there are two things of which I am
ignorant, and the Lord will not show them un-
to me, perhaps for a wise purpose in Himself,
I mean in some respects; and they are these:
why has God suffered so great a calamity to
ocme upon Zion, and what the great moving
cause of this great afflication is.  And again,by
what means He will return her back to her in-
heritance with everlasting joy upon her head.
These two things, brethren, are in part kept
back that they are not plainly manifest,in con-
sequence of those who have incurred the dis-
pleasure of the Almighty.
     "When I contemplate upon all things that
have been manifested, I am sensible that I
ought not to murmur only in this, that those
who are innocent are compelled to suffer for
the iniquities of the guilty; and I cannot ac-

[Column 2] 

count for this, only on this wise, that the say-
ing of the Savior has not been strictly ob-
served, 'If thy right eye offend thee, pluck it
out and cast it from thee; or if thy right arm
offend thee, cut it off and cast it from thee.'
Now, the fact is, if any of the members o[-] our
body are disordered the rest of our body will
be affected with them, and then all is brought
into bondage together; and yet,  notwithstand-
ing all this, when I know that you, my breth-
ren, with whom I have had so many happy
hours, sitting, as it were, in heavenly places in
Christ Jesus, and also having the witness
which I feel, and ever have felt, of the purity
of your motives, are cast out, and are as stran-
gers and pilgrims on the earth, exposed to hun-
ger, cold, nakedness, peril, sword, etc. I say,
when I comtemplate this, it is with difficulty
that I can keep from complaining and mur-
muring against this dispensation; but I am sen-
sible that this is not right, and may God grant
that notwithstanding your great afflictions and
sufferings, there may not anything separate us
from the love of Christ. 
	"Brethren, when we learn your sufferings it
awakens every sympathy of our hearts, it
weighs us down, we cannot refrain from tears,
yet we are not able to realize only in part your
sufferings.	And I often hear the brethren say[-]
ing that they wish they were with you that
they might bear part of your sufferings: and I
myself should have been with you, [-]ad not
God prevented it in the order of His provi-
dence, that the yoke of affliction might be less
grievious upon you; God having forewarned
me concerning these things, for your sakes; and
also Oliver Cowdery could  not have lightened
your afflictions by tarrying longer with you,for
his presence would have so much the more en-
raged your enemies.  God hath dealth merciful-
ly with us.
     "O brethren, let us be thankful th at it is as
well with us, as it is, and we are yet alive, that
preadventure God hath la[-]d up in store great
good for us in this generation, and  grant that
we may yet glorify His name."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

﻿ONLY A TRAMP.

	My husband, returning home from a short
walk one Sunday morning, related to me a lit-
tle incident that took place while absent. He
said:
	"As I was walking leisurely along, deeply
absorbed in thought, a tramp, as they are com-
monly called,suddenly came upon me unawares.
I saluted him wtth a pleasant 'good morning,
sir,' received a gruff answer in return, and as
we were both going in the same direction, we
both passed along together. The conversation
turned upon the topic uppermost in almost
every person's mind, that is, hard times. He
stated that he had just come from Idaho; had
been all through seeking work—he had rode
but a short distance, and had walked the rest
of the way to Salt Lake City, but the famine
for work being so great he had been so far un-
successful.
	He said it seemed as though there was
nothing but scoffs and jeers for him, and that
it was printed on every board, "There is
nothing here for you." And he also said: "I
shall not starve in a land of plenty. If I can-
not get means by honest labor I shall resort to
other ways!" With this, and other similar
careless and reckless speeches he left me.
	It set my husband to contemplating: Could
it be that we were driving a great many of
our fellow beings to destruction, simply by
giving them careless, cold, indifferent speeches,
when it would not take any more exertion to
give a few kind, encouraging words? for it is
far better to help a fallen brother up hill,than
to kick him down.
	While he was relating this incident a young
girl about twelve years of age, being in the

[Column 2]

house, commenced laughing and jesting about
an experience she, with some other playmates
of about the same age, had had with two
tramps.
	Coming home from school one night, and
seeing a couple of so called tramps ahead of
them; they immediately devised mischief to-
wards them; they threw stones at them, at the
same time calling them abusive names that were
entirely uncalled for.
	The said tramps turned upon them angrily,
thereby frightening them some, but as soon as
they resumed their way they immediately be-
gan their abuse again until they were beyond
their reach.
	Thus it was that two men, fellow creatures,
walking along, minding their own business,
were molested and abused, simply because they
were ungainly in appearance.
	To my question put to her, "Did those men
interfere with you in the first place?" she said,
"Oh, now!" "Then why did you interfere with
them?" "Oh, they were only tramps, and we
wanted some fun."
	Methinks it should be one of the first princi-
ples that parents should instil into the minds
of their children, to be kind to every one.
Never speak a harsh word, or perform a rude
act, just because their outward appearances
are ungainly. Strive to act the lady and
gentleman to all, to the rich and to the poor—
the poor are just as much deserving and some-
times much more.
	Remember that sometimes the fairest flower
has oft hidden within its bosom some subtle
poison, and those who inhale its perfume are
slowly imbibing death; and it is just so with
some people; they are dressed fine, have a
smooth, oily speech, and seem without tarnish
or taint, but notwithstanding all this, beneath
lurks some evil, venomous spirit; and some-
times the humblest flower has hidden some
kind, healing power, likewise with some people
whom adversity has frowned on too severely,
they have an ungainly exterior, but under-
neath all of this may beat a warm, true heart.
So remember and beware, if you scoff and jeer,
make sport of a poor, fallen creature because
of his ungainly attire you may be abusing
a diamond of the first water, for you know we
have to dig deep to find the finest diamonds, or
what would be far worse, this same one might
be one of God's chosen elect.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	Mary Howitt still lives in Tyrol. She is now
eighty-five years old.
	Miss Matilda Hindman makes her depart-
ment in the Pittsburg Commercial Gazette in-
teresting and useful.
	Rosa Bonheur's last painting, a cattle scene
in the Pyrenees, has been offered to a New
York dealer for $12,000.
	The Canada Citizen says that the municipal
vote of the women in the late electoral contest
decided the election of mayor in Fergus and
Belleville.
	In the Ecole de Médecine in Paris, there are
seventy-eight female students, of whom forty
six are Russians,fourteen Frenchwomen, eleven
Englishwomen, and seven Americrns.
	A bill for municipal suffrage for women has
been formally introduced in the Michigan Sen-
ate by Senator Belknap. It was given a first
and second reading by title, and referred to
the Committee on Judiciary.
	Mme. Patti telegraphed from America, in
holiday week, ordering that a tradesman at
Swansea should send three wagon loads of
blankets and flannel to Crag-y-nos for distribu-
tion among the poor in the neighborhood of
her country residence.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


MY RECORD.

	BY ESTHER A. BENNION

All day long I have baffled with care -
	Go from my presence, thou restless sprite,
Leave me to rest in my old arm-chair,
Leave me alone with my thoughts to night.

Slowly rolls backward the backward the curtain of time,
	Bringing me back to my early youth;
Back to my home in my native clime,
	Where gladly I heard the message of truth.

I plainly remember the vows I made
	To serve my God with a faithful heart,
To seek to become a Saint indeed,
If strength sufficient He would impart.

Year after year has faded away;
	Joys and sorrows have come to me;
The hair of my head is sprinkled with grey,
	My step more feeble than used to be.

Have I been true to the vows I made -
	What is the record my conscience bears?
Have I been true to the light I had -
	instead of wheat am I sowing tares?

Come, little children, and plead for me:
	Have I not loved you with love untold?
Have I not taught you most earnestly
Truths that are better than silver or gold?

You that were lent to me, sons of my heart,
Will you not answer me frankly and just,
Have I not acted a true mother's part,
Have I not tried to be true to my trust?

Where are the aged and weary to-night,
Have I not striven to comfort and bless,
Have I not ever defended the right,
And held out a hand to help in distress?

I wish I oculd peep at the records above--
In courts of heaven--by angels kept,
To see if my name is written in love,
Or branded as one who "slumbered and slept."

Resting awhile in my old arm chair,
All alone, in the dead of the night,
With noe but God and my conscience near,
I fear my record is far from bright.

Ah me!  I have made so many mistakes;
Have failed so often my duty to see;
"The spirit is willing, the flesh is weak,"
	Father, I pray Thee, deal gently with me!
Feb. 17th, 1885.


TO THE GIRLS.

Be true to yourselves, girls, always be true;
Be honest, be upright, whatever you do;
Give heed to the monitor God placed in you
To guide you aright, girls—'twill carry you through.

Be true to yourselves, girls, be pleasant, be kind,
For in giving true friendship true friends you will find;
And for every good deed in your lives that you do,
Will be paid back ten fold in good deeds to you.

Be modest, be gentle, be helpful to all,
Let your tongue not speak words they would wish to
	recall;
Girls, keep no company not worthy of you,
For if you choose wisely, you've enough to pass
	through.

Speak kindly to mother—if you could but know
The pleasure you give, how it makes her heart glow—
By a sweet, loving sympathy sharing her cares,
Removing by kindness the hardships of years.

And now, dearest girls, of one thing take heed,
If you cannot see into your good parent's creed,
Are there things in their faith that you don't compre-
	hend,
Inquire of them kindly, and never contend;

For they have the knowledge, God gave, it to them,
Of the truth and the power of their faith, and to men
Who have proved it, and left all their friends for the
	cause,
'Tis hurtful to hear you make light of God's laws.

And oh! dear young sisters, in years that's to come,
When you look back and think of your childhood's
	home,


And know you were kind, and loving, and true,
It will comfort and cheer you all your life through.
		ANNIE FREE.
	Manuscript Paper, "Farmer's Ward Gazette."


[Column 1]

COLLEGE EVILS.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	It is a serious question if the risks people
take in sending their children away from home
to be educated do not far out-balance the prob-
able good an education will bring. It is true
that evil is not necessarily evil associated either
with going away from home, or with an educa-
tion; and it is just as true that there is no neces-
sary association of what is commonly under-
stood by the word "greatness" with a college,
or a superior education. The object of life is,
or should be, perfect manhood and womanhood
—that perfection which embraces all the moral,
intellectual and social excellences known to
the age we live in, and to the strong and crav-
ing spirit of humanity.
	Education in its broadest sense implies all
knowledge, and all experience; so that to live
and to retain the power of intellect, is, in that
sense, ever to become better educated. But
special pursuits, special studies (and it is with
these we now deal), do not comprehend that
fuller education which comprises all things.
College education, nor any special education, is
not the great object.
	Like all other segregated fragments of a
grand and perfect whole, it is but one of many
equally important and essential attainments;
like all the rest, it is but a means to an end—
that end, human perfection. This fact does
not detract from the value of scholastic attain-
ments, for all knowledge is a power—but a
power, it must ever be borne in mind, equally
potent for good or for evil. The element of
goodness, or virtue, or moral excellence, is out-
side of scholarly erudition, it exists independ-
ent of it; wider knowledge simply widens the
scope for the exercise of those attributes which
are comprehended in the general term good;
consequently there is a possibility that exten-
sive, or even limited learning may be acquired
at the expense of those higher and nobler and
more desirable attributes or attainments known
as moral excellences, and it is often the case
that the little benefit which college pursuits
and experiences bring to young men are dearly
bought, particularly where the price is the
moral basis, foundation or intuition. That is
why the assertion contained in the introductory
sentence was made. "It is a serious question
if the risks people take in sending their child-
ren away from home to be educated don't far
out-balance the possible good an education will
bring."
	Young men, or boys, sent to college, leave
home at a time when character is forming. The
plastic condition of the mind and the suscepti-
bility of nature at that age, which render in-
struction so satisfactory and productive of such
lasting results—are not without dangers, for
the same mobility of mind and susceptibiliiy of
nature are as often to the influences of evil as
of good. The character is then a tender bud,
developing into the perfect flower, where the
conditions are congenial, but dwarfed, and
scraggy and unsightly if the surroundings are
adverse, or if the care is not uniform. It is
the most dangerous period in the life of man or
woman, the hour when most is needed the
watchful eye of the earnest parent. At that
trying hour the parent who sends his son away
from home influence, or permits him to leave
it, offers him as a bait to every temptation of a
vile and wicked world.
	The faculty of a college deal with the intel-
lect not with the moral nature of the pupil, for
three reasons: First, the parent does not want
the teacher to teach that branch; the faculty

[Column 2]

could not do it, and the student will not sub-
mit to it. The boy is social; he seeks compan-
ions who, like himself, want entertainment.
Study is work, and work, save to prodigies, old
or young, is irksome. To escape study is to
be free from work. They plan to be idle, not
meaning harm, and then begins the awakening
to that endless round of sin, to which the
vices and ingenuity of the wicked and the vile
have, for century upon century, been accumu-
lating. There is no loving, no watchful no
guarding eye. The boy is his own master. He
cannot appreciate the danger, and he tastes and
tastes again, until he drinks of the cup, filled
with what seems to be wondrously delicious
drafts. yet at the bottom of which is the poison
of disease and death. Is it a wonder that boys
fall? Money is sent them and they send it
as they will. Half—nay, three-fourths of the
boys who leave a college, depart from it little
if any the better, intellectually, for the time
spent there, and they are moral wrecks; their
consciences are inured to sin; the highest law
known to them is personal gratification with
such reasonable or unreasonable restraint as
they may determine from themselves. The
fact that all are guilty, or equally "free from
old fogy restraint," as it is put, serves only to
give them confidence, and the old sting is ulti-
mately realized if license in the man, which
begins with the boy, widens the mistaken idea
that it is liberty.
	I was in New York on Thanksgiving Day.
The college boys indulged in a holiday; there
was a brutal contest called "foot ball." At
night a friend asked me to go with him; we
visited several concert rooms, and they were
crowded beyond the power of words to express
with these college students, boys not yet out of
their teens, smoking, chewing, drinking and
drunken. They were swaggering and stagger-
ing about, displaying money, and were the easy
victims of vile men, and of the vile (I can
hardly say unfortunate) women, who crowded
about them like leeches, luring them to that
loss of morality which should be above all
price—that highest intellectual, moral, spirit-
ual and social attainment—virtue. I wondered
how much the mothers of these boys knew of
their college life; and it does not take me long
to decide that the probable benefits were not
sufficient compensation for the risks taken, for
the almost certain evil to follow. This is not
an unusual event; it is the rule where opportuni-
ty is afforded.
	If College education is the great desidera-
tum then send your boys away from home. If
it is only a means to an end I  believe the end
can better be accomplished. Of course this
evil is not a necessary attendant; boys go and
return pure, but the temptation is to all. Those
who resist it are few indeed.
	WANDERING BOY.


On Wednesday, January 28th, the direct ca-
ble was broken about 400 miles from the coast
of Ireland, and it is believed that it cannot be
repaired until spring. This makes the fourth
cable lost by the allied companies--the two
Gould cables, the French and the direct. The
Anglo and the Commercial are the only two 
lines in working order.

	Patents have been issued for the week end-
ing Feb. 3, 1884:
	Martha E. Boyd, Carthage, Ill., foot-warm-
er.
	Sarah A. Moulton, Grand Rapids, Michigan.
meat-tender.
	Margaret E. Knight, Ashland, Mass., spit
for roasting meat.
	Anna M. Rittenhouse, Philadelphia, Pa., ink-
well guard.
	Mary R. Walpole, Mayfield, Ky., wash-
boiler.

SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 1, 1885


THE INAUGURATION OF CLEVELAND.

	An event of great importance to the people of
the United States is now just at hand. The in-
auguration of the Chief Executive of the Re-
public. To all who love their country and ap-
preciate the administration of law and justice it
will be a momentous time. It will doubtless
portend great changes in many places and in
different respects. The Democratic party have
been waiting long for an opportunity to show
their strength in the nation, and at the time of
the election of Rutherford B. Hayes in 1877, the
party felt sure of a complete victory, but were
obliged to submit with the best grace possible to
their defeat. This time there seems to be no
shadow on their cloudless horizon, but all is
clear, and every prospect bids fair to usher in
the President elect with all the pomp and cere-
mony a Republican form of government makes
admissible.
     Already grand and elaborate preparations are
being made at the Capital, and a gathering of
noted people from all parts of the country has
commenced in good earnest. Washington will
be fairly ablaze with splendor for one day at
least, and although Grovesnor Cleveland is said
to be a very plain and unassuming man in the
matter of display and eclat, yet on this one oc-
casion society and custom will compel him to
accept for the day at least the formal attentions
and homage of an enthusiastic and delighted
populace.  The very fact of the long time that
has elapsed since a Democratic President filled
the office most honorable in the gift of the peo-
ple, will serve to heighten in a very great de-
gree the exultation of the people over the vic-
tory, now successfully gained. It is but justly
due to the lofty position of the President of so
great a nation, that some formal steps should be
taken to make the occasion one long to be re-
membered, one significant in all its bearings of
the true spirit of Americanism. It impresses
the masses of the people much more forcibly
than the simple fact itself. They would almost
forget that such an even had taken place were
it not for the pomp and ceremony, the elaborate
display of flags and banners and the noise of
cannons and martial music. The common peo-
ple, plain as they may seem, glory in these out-
ward symbols of greatness. Besides, this is a
day of show and parade and festivities, gay col-
ors and ostentation, when everywhere we see
flaunting in the ranks of the civil and the mili-
tary the most elaborate display in style and or-
namentation. When men and women prefer
the sunflower to the violet, and aspire to aesthet-
icism no matter how insipid and insignificant
they may make themselves, what is the civi-
lized world to expect?
	But not to diverge from the inauguration,
there can be no doubt that everything will be
managed on a most magnificent scale.  It is the
American style to spend money profusely, and
when it is for the purpose of inaugurating a
Democratic President after so many long years
of weary waiting, no pains will be spared to
make it the event of the day in which we live.
	The dedication of the stupendous Washington
monument is barely over, when close upon the
heels of destiny comes a national event of the
greatest significance, either for good or ill to
every citizen of this commonwealth.
	To be sure, much depends upon other things
connected with the office; for instance, it is a 
well authenticated fact in history that it is often
the case, the power behind the throne is greater
than the power on the throne.
	It is thought now that the members of Pres.
Cleveland's Cabinet will be Bayard, Secretary of
State; Manning, Secretary of Treasury; Lamar,
Secretary of Interior; Garland, Attorney Gener-
al; Vilas, Postmaster General, with a probability
that Whitney will be Secretary of navy, and
Endicott Secretary of War.  It is well known
that three at least of these men are pronounced
antiMormon in thier views and will be likely to
approve and justify the harshest measures
against this people.  In view of the new admin-
istration we can see nothing to be hoped for as
far as Utah is concerned, except a change of
officers with decidedly opposite views, and
sometimes a radical change is a good thing, even
if no other benefit be derived from it than get-
ting in new people who are not initiated in all
the ways and means through which the present
officials and their helps have brought about this
religious crusade.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

     ERRATA.--In the first paragraph of “Scenes
and Incidents,”in our last issue, a sentence was
omitted which rendered the remainder of the
subject almost senseless.  It was written in the
journal: “By Bro. Kimball’s request I went to
assist Shter Hyrum Smith to move up from the
river.”
	THE young ladies of Bear Lake Stake have is-
sued a small magazine entitled, “The Literary
Garland,” representing the Y. L. M. I. A. of
that Stake of Zion. Miss Libby Laker, Editor.
It is published at St. Chairles, Idaho, and bears
the motto, "Knowledge is Power.” The copy
shown us by a friend la No. 2, Vol.1, published
July, 1831, and contains 20 pages.  The editorial
is well written and contains excellent advice to
the young ladies.  The other articles of prose
and poetry are mostly from the young girls of
that Stake, and are a great credit to the Asso-
ciations.  It is a very commendable effort on
the part of the young ladies in that locality, and
we sincerely hope their enterprise will succeed.
The WOMAN'S EXPONENT offers congratulations
and would be glad to exchange.
	“THE NEW ERA,” successor to “Our Herald,”
is now  published in Chicago, Elizabeth Boynton
Herbert, well known to our readers and to the
public generally as one of the representative
women of America, is the editor.  It is in square
magazine form and contains sixty-two pages, is
published monthly.  The articles are all from
the pens of able writers; and we think it one of
the very best womens’ papers of the present
time, when there are many that are good.
These journals and magazines in the hands of
thoughtful and intelligent women are doing a
vast amount of good, more than we can enumer-
ate. They reach all classes of society and are
sowing not only in good ground, but broadcast,
much precious seed that will spring into bud,
blossom and fruit in due time for the advance-
ment of the human family.


WHAT ARE WOMAN'S RIGHTS.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
     Having a few moments to spare I thought I
would jot down a few of my reflections upon the
reading of the minutes of the “Woman Suffrage
Convention, lately held in the City of Washing-
ton, D. C., and published in your issue of  Feb-
ruary 15, especially the following on page 150:
"Whereas the dogmas incorporated in religious
creeds derived from Judaism, teaching that wo-
man was an afterthought of creation, her sex a
misfortune, marriage a condition of subordina-
tion and maternity a curse."  And concluding
with the words, "that in true religion there is
neither male nor female, bond nor free, but all
are one."
     I am writing now not for the purpose of pro-
voking discussion with the estimable ladies of
the East who are so earnestly advocating and
working for the rights of their sex, but for the
women of Mormondom, who, above all the rest
of womankind, have need to understand correct-
ly the relation that man and woman, especially
husband and wife, sustain to each other; be-
cause their religion (which Mrs. Stanton after-
wards claimed had a debasing tendency) teaches
some principles which, if true, involve eternal
consequences, but if false, then are we most
grossly deceived.
     What are the facts?  Adam was the first man
and Eve the first woman;  no matter whether
she came in company with him or followed af-
ter.  We find the two together living in har-
mony and peace; we have no record of conten-
tion, jealousy, or distrust manifesting itself dur-
ing their entire residence in the Garden of Eden.
Although history is silent in regard to the time
they spent together in this happy state, yet it is
evident from records that some time did elapse,
also that they were familiar with the Almighty
and received visits from Him during that
period.
     But a change is wrought.  By whom?  By the
serpent.  First seriously affecting Eve, because
she yielded to his wiles, afterwards Adam, be-
cause he voluntarily partook; and through these
the entire creation involving consequences and
conditions that are beyond the power of argu-
ment to reason away or philosophy to disperse,
no matter how profound.
     The f[-]at of the Almighty (Jehovah) goes
forth--read the third chapter of Genesis from
the ninth to the nineteenth verses, inclusive.
Nearly six thousand years of our time has
elapsed since then, exhibiting humanity, in fact
all creation, in every possible phase and condi-
tion.  Tell me, has God's word as pertaining to
the earth, to the serpent, to Eve, to Adam,
failed in any one particular?  Does the serpent
crawl upon his belly; is there enmity between
him and man?  Does woman still bring forth
in sorrow?  Is the ground still cursed; and does the
thorn and thistle still grow spontaneously, in-
volving constant labor to keep in check?  Does
the sweat drop to-day from the brow of the hus-
bandman, and does man finally die, and woman
too?  All nature answers with one united voice,
"'Tis true."
     Now we come to one little clause contained in
the text: "Thy desire shall be to thy husband,
and he shall rule over thee."  Shall we patient-
ly submit to all the rest because we are unable
to alter and fight against, protest, ignore, and
finally defy the Almighty to pronounce these
words?  No.  We will accept of the penalty in
its entirety, for it is a penalty. We will submit
to the ordeal and thank God for the privilege,
because it is the road to life, immortality and
eternal glory.  But this rule of man, in what
does it consist?  Can its powers be defined?  Is
woman necessarily man's slave; is there left to
her no right to reason, no power of choice?


[Column 1]
﻿Because she was deceived by the serpent must
she be deprived of will power during her mortal
career? Mormonism inculcates no such princi-
ples, teaches no such doctrine.  If woman at-
tains to exaltation with man, it will be because
she merits in her own right that exaltation. If
she ever strives at a condition of glory it will be
because the elements necessary to such a condi-
tion dwell within herself.
   But can woman be advised, counseled and di-
rected by man, and yield thereto without sacri-
ficing in the least her womanhood?  Most cer-
tainly. As man can yield to his fellow man and
stlll retain his manhood.
   Can man attain to a fulness of exaltation
without woman?  No, verily.  For as the race
cannot be perpetuated upon earth without wo-
man, so the lives cannot be continued hereaf-
ter without her.  Then is she not necessary to
man, both here and hereafter?  Most truly.  As
woman would be utterly helpless without man
in accomplishing her destiny.
   Then instead of claiming"that in true religion
there is neither male nor female,” we assert
that, it is most essential that the sexes be pre-
served intact with their peculiar gifts, qualifica-
tions, endowments and rights, operating and la-
boring in their peculiar sphere to accomplish and
bring to pass the purposes of Jehovah in their
creation, and this labor should be entered upon
and continued harmoniously without jar or dis-
cord.  The man who is jealous of woman in the
exercise of all the powers that belong to her is
an object of pity; the woman who presumes to
perform the functions of man unsexes herself.
   But is woman man’s equal? This seems to be
the grand culminating point in all discussions
upon this subject.  Viewed from the woman’s
suffrage standpoint I would answer the ques-
tion, most certainly.  She should vote in com-
pany with man at every election untram-
melled and free, for if the sentiments of
Rev. Olympia Brown, as expressed in the Con-
vention, be true concerning man, then is woman
not only his equal but more than his peer.  But
from a Mormon standpoint I would answer the
question more fully.
   The fact of woman enjoying the right of fran-
chise in Utah for so many years past, and that,
too, a voluntary gift of our Legislature without
her asking, is a proof positive that we have no
fear of our wives and daughters, and we shall
fight most determinedly against any infringe-
ment in this regard  All who are acquainted
with Mormon customs know that from the first,
woman has had the right to vote in common
with men upon all public matters, and especial-
ly so in regard to the exercise of priestly author-
ity and rule, thus carrying out the will of the
Almighty that“all things shall be done by com-
mon consent.”
   But is man the head of the woman? Paul says
that he is, as Christ is the head of the Church.
There is no authority or office of the Priesthood
but man can receive of himself without the
woman, and exercise its powers and functions
independent of her.  Whereas, if woman ever
receives any degree of Priesthood it will be in
connection with and because of her relation to 
man. To use some familiar terms by way of il-
lustration: If a woman ever becomes a queen it
will be because of her relation to a king; if a
Priestess, because she is the wife of a Priest, he
having preceded her and received of himself his
authority, she following in possession first be-
cause of merit; second, because of right of in-
heritance by virtue of relationship to the man,
their union having been sanctified by the pow-
ers of heaven. Their increase inheriting all that
belongs to both father and mother.  Such a
union of man and woman perpetuating eternal
dominion in which is incorporated all the glory

[Column 2]
that is possible to conceive.  Child submitting
to parent, wife yielding to husband, man bow-
ing down to the Savior, and that Savior giving
up all to the Father, that “God may be all in
all.”  Each one while thus yielding, still retain-
ing their individual power and place unimpaired,
filled to a fulness according to their position and
calling, without one feeling of distrust or jeal-
ousy in regard to the other. Can man, woman
or child ask for more than this?
   While we sympathise, with the women of
Christendom in their earnest endeavors to ob-
tain their rights, and while we sense the wrongs
of which men are guilty and the terrible condi-
tion of the world viewed from every standpoint,
should we not be thankful for what we have re-
ceived of the true knowledge of God and the
principles of eternal life thst renders unneces-
sary any argument or contention in regard to
individual br sexual rights, for is not our calling
and election made sure?
						  J. E. TAYLOR,
   Salt Lake City, Feb. 22, 1885.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LOVE. 

	This is a little word of only four letters, yet
how potent, how powerful, and with it comes
our liberty or bondage. If we love right, we
are free; free in our souls, pure in our hearts, let
come what may, though oft through surround-
ing circumstances and worldly influence we are
swerved from the right, not having the moral
courage to resist evil, or know for ourselves the
sad results of weakness and hatred until too
late.
	Love, not fear, prompts the Latter-day Saints
to take upon them this title of honor. God so
loved the world that He gave His Son as a ran-
som; and in all His life and attributes we find
nothing but the purest love and the greatest self-
sacrifice for us. Can we be ashamed to own our
Lord? Never.
	History gives but few exceptions where man
who held power loved his fellow man better
than himself, even science must see her advo-
cates give their lives for the self-evident truths
she blesses mankind with; and when once cus-
tom sets her foot in a certain channel, woe to
those who divulge therefrom, whether it be
right or wrong. From Abraham to Washington,
divine ravelation has told the Saints of these
men and their mission, end no where in this
fair land are Columbus and Washington appre-
ciated and understood as here in Zion. The
legacy of freedom given us by our noble ances-
tors in this goodly land, we prize and mean to
maintain, here where the oppressed can find a
refuge, here where a nation has grown from in-
fancy to manhood in so short a time, we would
naturally expect liberty to live a life of vigor
for hundreds of years, but we find feeble old age
has come all too soon in some portions of the
land, and poor Idaho is evidently letting her
standard of liberty sink into an untimely grave.
	Is it for the love of their fellow men that citi-
zens of the United States are declared slaves in
Idaho? Let the world read and judge what men
love to do with the rights of their fellow men
when ones their souls are fettered with hate in-
stead of love for those who are walking in a bet-
ter and purer atmosphere than their own easy,
downward road. Behold how crooked can they
make the straight and just way of constitution-
al liberty bond.
	Because the Saints believed that God had
spoken from the heavens again and in His love
for His children had revealed the truth were
they branded and driven. Again comes the
voice of our Father that there is a higher and
better law. Will we obey it? Yes; the noble

[Column 2]
men and God-fearing women would honor the
source of their being, would honor the cause of
social regeneration, and be the noble pioneers of
a higher and holier life; for this are they ac-
cused of crime, yet will they contend to protect
virtue and love to bestow all the rights the fair
daughters of Eve are entitled to through their
obedience to the laws of God.
	It has become popular to hate the Mormons,
but do those who revile know what the Gospel
we have embraced is? We answer it in two or
three words—a consistent life. We love the
truth and control ourselves, for this are we as-
sailed on the right and on the left.
	The men that love to defile the daughters of 
Eve are unworthy the name of men, and no
man has the right to lay the weight of his
hand on a woman, "except in the marriage re-
lation." Would to God the women of the world
had the same protection that we, the Mormon
women, have: the blighted lives and sad hearts
that are seen by our Heavenly Father caused
by the lust and inconstancy of lustful Mormon-
hating men is enough to cause the very heavens
to weep.
	Where can we find pure love? In the homes
of the Latter-day Saints; there ir sits enthroned.
If sorrow and death visit such a household there
is comfort and relief at hand. A friend of mine
was called to part with his first wife. When
she was dying he said, "Mother, what do you
wish to say about the children?" With a look
of love and confidence she replied, "Anna will
do just as well by you and the children as I."
And there are thousands of such instances that
prove our confidence in and love for each other.
Is this love or lust?
	Another instance--In conversation with a 
brother who has thirty-six children, he said not
one ever gave him a saucy or unkind word; his 
descendants, all told, number over one hun-
dred. Lust kills the evidences of its crime, love
saves, cherishes and rears them to become noble
men and women, and claims the wives and
children as his own wealth for time and all eter-
nity.
	Over 4,200 lovely, bright, intelligent children
partake of the Lord's Supper every Sabbath, and
there are over 3,200 little ones under eight years
of age in their dear native Utah. And when we
look around among our noble boys and girls,
who can help exclaiming, surely the Lord is
with His people. Proud might any nation be
of such treasures. Is this the sign of decay, of
degredation, of disloyalty to our government?
Not to any right-minded, reasonable person. Our
principles are perfect, and we will continue to
love them and strive to live them,God being our
helper, protest the rights of others, envy no
one, be pure in our daily walk in life and leave-
the result in God's hands.
Zina D.H. Young


MIND IN MEDICINE.
	I have just laid down a small book bearing
the above title; and think it would be quite as
appropriate to call it medicine in mind. I have
read it through twice, and dipped into it fre-
quently besides; I felt from the first perusal it
was worthy a study. The author is metaphysi-
cal, and his thoughts flow so rapidly that he tel-
escopes his ideas; consequently the book must
be read closely to read it understandingly, that
the reader may take in his original and unique
assertions. Much truth is embodied within its
leaves, which, without close attention, we
should fail to realize.
	A deep thinking mind is exhibited through
its pages, and I have read parts over and over
again to make myself sure that I comprehend-
ed the author's meaning. I feel that it contains


much that will benefit its readers if reduced to
practice. To those who read that they may be
edified and instructed, such writers as the author
are public benefactors, and such should be the
motive-power of all readers.
	He vividly portrays the effect of imagination
with regard to ailments and disease upon an
over-sensitive and excitable nature, fear produc-
ing the disease so much dreaded; as prophecy,so
called, has often been known to be the cause of
its own fulfillment. The author evidently pos-
sesses one of the greatest gifts of God, viz. com-
mon sense! and his book should lie on the writ-
ing table of all, that it may be taken up in
spare moments, and its ideas will be found to
have a fructifying influence, more or less, on all
who study its contents to be instructed, it is de-
cidedly a book of reference, a wise monitor whom
the wise will appreciate.
	Even the strong and well balanced mind has
clouds flitting over it at times. The sun itself
has its spots,and the brightest day its little float-
ers ranging over its glorious concave, hence a
friend as “Mind in Medicine” is a friend in
need.
	I will give a brief extract or two from his
unique ideas: “Is sickness a sin? Yes, often
through some of our antecedents—‘some one has
blundered that line of Tennyson’s is true of all
aches and pains! Every saint by his or her
sanctity cleanses themselves and their off-
spring.” “A healthy body is a sign of a holy
life."
	“Mental medicine is not only sure, but often
wonderfully swift; a ray of light from a kind
countenance, a conviction that you are dear to
somebody, gives an impulse of courage from a
Christ like reassurring WILL—a word may be a
medicine, a letter, a piece of paper thrown into
the mall, a white-winged messenger,more beau-
tiful than doves, flying thousands of miles on
its errand of love, across the continent, on the
mighty ocean; but it reaches me, I touch it, and
I feel the warm current of life rushing through
my heart and veins—It is a cordial, but from no
bin or flask—it is a comforter, but not of silk or
elder-down or wool.

[Column 2]
were perfectly at ease in their very difficult
parts. Their singing, their acting and their
witticisms all merited the enthusiastic applause
they received from the audience. Mrs. Leviberg
as Lady Jane sustained the part with all the en-
thusiasm of a professional. Her voice is good
and thoroughly cultivated. Both Patience and
Lady Jane received quite a number of floral of-
ferings. Angela, Birdie Cummings, and Saphir,
Louise Wells, both looked and acted their parts
with becoming grace and dignity. The sex-
tette, in which Edna Russell, Louise Wells and
Lucy Young, and Messrs. Pyper, Crawford
and Barnes sang, was one of the gems of the
evening Fred Clawson created considerable
enthusiasm whenever he appeared, and added
to the humorous character of the play. The or-
chestra played exquisitely and was warmly ap-
plauded.
	Prof. Krouse has labored assiduously while
training the young people for Patience, and de-
serves great credit for the success of the per-
formance. Altogether it was quite a notable
event, quite as much so, if not more than Pina-
fore. The whole affair was very well arranged
and admirably managed.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


PLEASED WITH PATIENCE AND ITS
PERFORMERS.

EDITOT DESERET NEWS:
     I went to see Patience last evening, and was
amply repaid, as was every body else, I am sure,
who saw it.  One feature of the performance
was yery noticeable, and indeed, very enjoyable
to me, as it must have been to every person of
age and experience, who had the pleasure of
witnessing it. namely, that sweet, that inno-
cent refined look of purity that filled the stage
from the beautiful Mrs. Leviberg to the charm-
ing little Miss Thatcher—a feature, I am sorry
to say, seldom adorns the stage of to-day.  This,
Mr. Editor, to such as I who have traveled and
known the world for many years now, proved a
treat never to be forgotten, and on this account
homes and that same country which should
have protected them.
     Mrs. Sessions continues: “3rd. Bro. Brig-
ham took dinner with us.  Next day went
back three miles to where Parley P. Pratt was
camped, his wife had a daughter born.  On
the 4th, Independence Day, the Indians rode
out in style, came to the camp and danced for
us.  It was a war dance, and they were paint-
ed after their fashion.  6th. Crossed the Mis-
souri River; we are here situated between the
mountains, in the valley, no road cut yet, but
the men are at work making one as fast as
they can.  Wednesday, 8, the team has gone
back to fetch up Bro, Call, Tuesday, 14.
Eliza Lyman was taken sick, I was sent for;
she has a son; also on the same day Sophia
Dame with a daughter,”
“Thursday, 16.  A meeting in the morning
and counsel given to all who are ready, to go
on to Grand Island for the winter, others to re-
main."
     On the 20th she writes: “Peregrine put some
projection boards bn to our new wagon.” This
may not seem much of an item to the readers,
but if they had traveled as the saints at that
time did, and felt the inconvenience of narrow,
close wagons where one scarcely seemed to
have air enough to breathe, they would realize
what a luxury six or eight inches more in
width would be for the inmates. “Thursday,
23, Orson Pratt, his wife and children came
over the river and took dinner with us.  Fri-
day, 24, I start for Grand Island, go nearly all
he way on foot, found gooseberries and choke-
cherries.  27, the men have gone to work on
the bridge,” and so they continued until it was
completed.
     On Thursday she says the girls gathered
uantitiesof wild grapes with which-Sister
Sessions made wine.  On the 31st she speaks
of being very lonely, she says, “We are here
almost alone, only three tents,Holman’s, Ever-
ett’s and ours; and she adds, “I want to see the
rest of the company so much that I am quite 
discontented."
     Sister Sessions and family returned to the
camp at Winter Quarters in September, she


ary she had a few friends to celebrate her
birthday, and the afternoon was spent in a very
happy manner in blessing her, etc.  This shows
how the saints could enjoy themselves even un-
der the most trying circumstances and difficul-
ties.  "Sister Eliza called in the evening to in-
vite me to a little party, and when I told her
it was my birthday and wished her to bless me,
she said if I would go to the party they all
would bless me.  After going and attending
one of the sisters who had a child born, I went
to the party and had a good time singing, pray-
ing and speaking in tongues; but before the
party broke up I was called away to attend Sis-
ter Morse and from there to Sister E. A. Whit-
ney's, who was not well.  Friday, 5th, I went
to see Sister Joanna Roundy, she said it was
the last time I would ever see her in this world.
I gave her a message to carry to my children
who had died in their infancy and childhood.
In the evening I went to the party of the 'Sil-
ver Greys,' Sister Eliza went with us.  Sister
Joanna Roundy died this evening.  Saturday,
6th, attended to my household work and made
a quantity of soap.  Sister Whitney sent for
me again, I went to wait upon her, she had a
son born at eleven o'clock p.m.'
	This son was the little boy that many of our
readers will remember, a little sufferer all the
days of his life, who lived to be nine years of
age and never walked. Sister Whitney sent for
me again, I went to wait upon her, she had a
son born at eleven o'clock p.m.'
	This son was the little boy that many of our
readers will remember, a little sufferer all the
days of his life, who lived to be nine years of
age and never walked. Sister Whitney called
him "a child of promise," in answer to her
prayers to have a son born in the fulness of the
new and everlasting covenant.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SCENES AND INCIDENTS AT
WINTER QUARTERS
BY HELEN MAR WHITNEY. 
(Continued.)
	The same date Horace wrote, "This evening
Porter arrived, bringing Emmeline’s sisters and
brother,their mother having died on the way.”
	Emmeline, the present editor of the Ex-
ponent, was then the wife of Bishop Whitney,
and her liitle sisters and brother were received
and cared for, as were other members of his
family—being under the charge of Sister Em-
meline and Mother Whitney.
	“Saturday, the 21st.		*	*	This
evening Brothers Egan, Stephens and Gulley
came in from the army.  Brother Egan in-
formed me that they had been to Santa Fee,
and that most of the brethren in the army had
gone from there to California.  These brethren
came back for counsel.” The same day a
number of teams came in from St. Joseph with
goods.
	The next day, Sunday, Brother Woolley ar-
rived.  Meeting was held at the stand.  The
main subject, was the sending off of a lot of
men in the spring, also, a few families, and the
remainder to stay at Winter Quarters.  The
evening of the 24th Horace says: “Brothers
Kimball and Brigham met with two men
(mountaineers) from the Salt Lakes,where they
have been settled for sixteen years.  They
narrated their adventures and gave an account
of the climate, etc , which was quite interest-
ing.”
	The same day in speaking of the weather
he said, “There seems to be a particular dis-
position of Providence in our behalf, for al-
though we have,had several cold, frosty nights
the weather has been remarkably mild for win-
ter, and we have not, as yet, experienced the
first snow storm, as is usual in other coun-
tries." 
	The night of the 24th he speaks of experi-
encing the heaviest frost of the season.
	The next day he wrote: “Most of us, viz.,
Howard Egan, John Davenport, Dan Davis;
Jacob Frazier and myself engaged in cutting
and drawing wood from the bottom close by.
Brother Kimball cautioned the folks this even-
ing against using our wood lavishly, sitting up
late at night and keeping a fire burning, as, he
said, before the winter was out it would be
a very scarce article.” We had previously
learned that the Pottowatomie agent had for-
bid us to cut any more timber on the other
side of the river.  He wrote:
	Friday, the 27th.  This evening Brother E.
Benson got back from the eastern country,
where he has been on business.  I learned that
a brother who went with him, and who had
some pecuniary expectations from some of his
friends, was taken up for insanity, because he
belonged to the “Mormons,” and in order to
cheat him out of his property.”
	“Sunday, the 29th.		*	*	Meeting
was held at the stand, at which it was deter-
mined that every man should immediately turn
out and assist in making the race way, in
order that the mill may be finished speedily.
“Brother Kimball called his family together for
the purpose of giving them good instruc-
tion."
	The 1st of December there came quite a
change in the weather—the wind blowing
from the north.  That day Horace mentions
being at work with some of father’s boys, lay-
ing up the logs to Brother Wallace’s house.  A
day or two later they were engaged in putting
up another house for Sister Miller, how Sister
Finch, whose husband had died a short time
previous.
	On the 7th, Abigail Pond, a wife of Bishop
Whitney’s, died.  Two of her sisters had died
a day or two before.  They had lately arrived
from Nauvoo, and were still living in tents. 
Trading commenced at the store that day.
	“Wednesday, the 9th,” Horace wrote, “warm
day for the season.  This morning, early,
learned that during last night a party of'
Indians came from the other side of the river,
which they crossed on the ice, and attacked a
small party of the Omahaws, who are en-
camped near us, and severely, if not mortally
wounded two or three of their number, viz.,
“Big Head,” the chief of the nation, and his
squaw.  Mary, an Indian interpreter with the
Omahaws, says that it was the Iowas who com-
mitted the outrage.  The Omahaws being very
much frightened, to-day moved their encamp-
ment and pitched their tents near Brigham’s
house on Main Street.  The two invalids were
taken to his house to have their wounds dressed.
“Big Head” was shot through the head and
arm; his wife through the arm only, which is
considerably mutilated, and I understood that
it was the intention to have it amputated.
	On the evening ot the followirig Saturday
the Indians “had a great pow wow among them,
screeching and yelling in a horrible manner,
on account of their having just received the
intelligence that a large number of their war-
riors, who went on the hunting expedition a
short time since, had been killed off by the
Sioux.”
	Sunday, the 13th, being a fine day, meeting
was held at the stand.  Brother Luke Johnson
was present.  He had, just come on—had
buried his wife at St. Joseph.  There was 
quite a rejoicing among the old Kirtland
Saints, to see Bro. Luke Johnson among, them
again.  He stayed one night at father’s house.
His'sister, Marinda Hyde, who was living on
the river, came there with him.
	On the 14th Horace wrote, “Brigham, Heber,
E. Benson, Father Cahoon and Ira Eldredge
went down the river about eight miles to the
place where Sarpee used to trade, to make ar-
rangements for the building of houses for the
Omahaws; that being deemed a proper place
for them to settle themselves—it is so well
fortified by nature, that they can discern the
approach of an enemy at some distance.”
	“Sunday, the 20th,” he says, “meeting was
held at Bro. Kimball’s house. Bro. Joseph
Nobles, the Bishop of this ward, presiding, at
which arrangements were made to relieve the
poor, and for establishing a school, etc.”
	Friday, the 25th, was a most beautiful day,
and being Christmas, at evening we had a lit-
tle party at father’s, which, was very enjoyable
and passed off finely.


R S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS

	ALMA WARD, MESA CITY.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Owing to our isolated situation we see but
few communications from this part of the 
country in your valuable paper, hence the
liberty I take in penning a few lines to you,
hoping you will be kind enough to give them
space in your paper.
	We were organized May 31st, 1883, under
the direction of President McDonald, with
Anna Klienman, President; Henrietta Stand-
age and Laureta Staples, Counselors; Sarah
M. Mullens, Secretary; Sophronia A. Standage,
Assistant Secretary; Hannah M. Stewart,
Treasurer. We have thrity-five names en-
rolled, with an average attendance of nineteen.
We hold our meetings semi-monthly, and have
a very good time. The sisters are trying to do
all they can in helping to roll on this great
work; they are ever willing to lend a helping
hand to those that are in want or distress, and
have a desire to do all the good they can, but
owing to the disadvantages and pioneer life of
the people, we have been unable to do as much
as we would like to do. The sisters are trying
to live their religion and serve God. The EX-
PONENT is read in our meetings, with other 
Church works, striving to render them a source
of benefit and mutual improvement.
	Wishing for the success of the EXPONENT,
I remain,
	Your sister in the Gospel,
	S. A. S.

	HARRISVILLE. 
	Minutes of a Primary Conference, held at
the east school house, the Primary Association
of West Harrisville meeting with us, com-
mencing at 1 o'clock p.m., Prest. Mary E. 
Taylor presiding. Singing, "In our Lovely
Deseret." Prayer by Bro. L. J. Taylor. Sing-
ing. Minutes of previous meeting read and
approved.
	The President made the opening remarks.
and gave some very good instructions. She
was then followed by the children, with songs,
dialogues, recitations and select reading, which
was very interesting to those who were present
There was a goodly number of the parents
present, who seemed very much pleased with
the exercises of the Primary children. Prest.
Harriet Daybell felt pleased with the children
of the Association; thought they manifested 
great interest in their meetings.
	The speakers were Coun. Wm. Rawson, L.
J. Taylor, T. Brown. N. L. Shurtliff, Jane
Rawson, Lydia Martin, Lovina Hegstead; each
one made some very appropriate remarks, en-
couraging the children in well doing; thought
it a great privilege for the children in these
days to have a meeting where they can be 
taught the principles of the Gospel. It was
also remarked that we should be thankful for
our comfortable homes, when there are so many
out in the world without homes such as we
have. We should live prayerful, and pray for
each other; the Lord will hear your prayers
just as quick as He will your parent's. You
should honor your fathers and mothers and be
kind to every one. There were many other
good instructions given.
	The President said she had been well edified
in listening to the exercises, also the good in-


structions given by our brethren and sisters
that were present, and felt like thanking the
parents for coming out to see us. 
	Adjourned the joint meeting for three
months, to be held at the west school house
Singing, "Lord, dismiss us," etc.  Benediction
by K. D. Brown.  
	ANNIE E. SHURTLIFF, Secretary, 
	MARY ANN TAYLOR, Asst. Sec. 

	SALT LAKE CITY. 

	The Ladies' regular semi-monthly meeting
was held in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly
Rooms, Saturday, February 7th, Coun. B. W. 
Smith presiding.  Opened with singing. 
"Arise, my soul, arise."  Prayer by Coun. E. 
Howard. Singing, "Mid scenes of confusion, 
and creature complaints."  Minutes of last
meeting were read and accepted; also minutes
of P. A. of 21st.  11th.  4th and 5th wards. 
Counselor Smith made opening remarks; said
we want to live near to God, and follow what
Prest. Taylor said—"If we get into a storm, 
round up our shoulders and face it the best 
we can, for it cannot last forever."
	Sister Lula Richards said we all know that
God can inspire us; we want to be obedient
and try and sustain our brethren in their hours
of trial;  said she knew that the prayers of the
Saints availed much; exhorted the sisters to 
teach their children to pray, for their innocent
prayers would do much for this people. 
	Sister Bird expressed her gratitude that she
lived in this day and was numbered among
the Saints, and her desire to be ever faithful 
to the cause of truth. 
	Sister Ann Odell of Ogden, spoke of her
joy in being associated with the sisters; alluded
to her having been President of a Primary As-
sociation for six years, and felt grateful for 
that experience; exhorted the sisters to be 
faithful to God's cause and He would protect 
us to the end. 
	Sister Elizabeth Stevenson gave good advice
to mothers in regard to bringing up their
children, and advised them to be wise and
cautious and watchful of what they said, and
pray for our brethren. 
	Sister Lawson bore a faithful testimony and
expressed her gratitude for God's care over the
Saints: Sister Friel also bore her testimony. 
Sister Richards related an anecdote that would
be well to teach the children. 
	Coun. S. M. Kimball said, we are truly liv-
ing in perilous times, and while I feel that the
Lord "doeth all things well," we must fit our-
selves to meet whatever may come to try us. 
We will each be tested that we may prove our-
selves before God.  Made many other good re-
marks. 
	Coun. E. Howard spoke of the word of wis-
dom, and urged the sisters to observe it. 
Thought we should be wise in our conversa-
tion; gave very good instruction to the young
girls in regard to truth and virtue. 
	Sister Smith, Sister Jones and other sisters
spoke a short time, and meeting adjourned for
two weeks.  Singing, "The Spirit of God like
a fire is burning," etc.  Benediction by Sister
Julia Howe.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S VOICE. 

EDITOR EXPONENT: 

	As it has been quite a length [-]f time since
there has been a word from Manti, I thought
I wou[-]d improve the present opportunity of 
writing you a few lines. 
	The Saints, generally, manifest increased de-
sires to go forward in the great and glorious
work in which we are engaged, notwithstand-
ing our enemies are on the alert trying to bring
to naught that which our Father has decreed


He will bring to pass in the day in which we
live.  I often think how true was the saying of
our Divine Master to His disciples, and also
how true it is in our day, that if we were of the
world the world would love us.  But history
repeats itself, and it is the same now as it has 
always been when the Lord has had a people
on the earth.
	I sometimes think that it cannot be that we 
are living in this great and glorious Republic
—the land I have loved so well, the home of
my ancestors for generations past, even to the
earliest settlement of New England.  When I
was a little girl my greates delight was to hear
my grandfathers tell tales of the Revolution,
for they were both engaged in that great strug-
gle for liberty for their land; they also told me
of the great rejoicing throughout the land
when the war was over, for they fondly hoped
to leave the pricelss boon, for which they had
endured so many privations, to their posterity;
but to-day, when I reflect upon the situation of
my country, I feel that we, as mothers and
grandmothers, should tell the story of our
wrongs to our children and granchildren; yes,
let us tell them of our troubles in Missouri and
the sorrows of Nauvoo—how we were forced to
to leave our homes and the beautiful temple
which we had reared under such difficulties, as
only those who were there can realize or under-
stand.  Our beloved Prophet and Patriarch
had been massacred, and that, too, under the
promise of protection of the Governor of
Illinois. 
	We know and realize that the special care of
our Father has been over His people, and we 
have faith in Him for the future, that the
beautiful Zion which the old prophets had so
much pleasure in contemplating will be built
in her beauty and glory, when the oppressors
shall cease to oppress.	R. W.
	Manti, Feb. 21st, 1885.


OLD FOLK'S PARTY AT BLOOMING-
TON.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Thinking a few lines from this part of the
country would prove acceptable, I will give you
a short account of our annual old folk's party,
which was held on the 4th of February; in the
Bloomington schoolhouse. As is usual with
these parties, it proved a decided success. At
2 p.m. seventy-five or eighty persons sat down
to an excellent dinner prepared by members of
the Relief Society under the direct supervision
of our much respected bishop, Wm. Hulme,
and our beloved president, Sarah Greenhalgh.
We were honored on the occasion with the
presence of the Presidency of Stake, Wm. 
Budge and J. H. Hart, also the Stake Presi-
dent of Relief Society, J. P. Lindsay, Elizabeth 
Collins and L. M. Hart.
	The meeting was opened as usual by singing 
and prayer, then a roll was called of those who
had attained the age of fifty years and up-
wards. Two, I am grieved to say, have de-
parted this life since our last gathering. Min-
utes of previous meeting were then read and
accepted, after which we had a very interesting
programme, consisting of songs, speeches and
dancing, we also had the pleasure of listening
to most interesting and instructive remarks by
the brethren and sisters of Stake Presidency,
also from our veteran brethren and sisters. I
think I should be correct in saying not one left
the house but felt blessed and comforted by the
meeting.
	With best wishes for the success of our pa-
per, I beg leave to subscribe myself, your sis-
ter in the Gospel.
	E. C. PIGGOTT, Sec.
